title: R v Michael Misick; R v Thomas Chalmers Misick; R v McAllister Hanchell (CR 18 of 2014; CR 35 of 2012; CR 36 of 2012) [2026] TCASC 11 (4 February 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF
THE TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
Action No: CR 35/12
CR 36/12
CR 18/14
BETWEEN
THE KING
AND
MICHAEL MISICK
MC ALLISTER HANCHELL
THOMAS CHALMERS MISICK
BEFORE: RAJENDRA NARINE J
APPEARANCES:
MR. ANDREW MITCHELL KC AND MR. QUINN HAWKINS AND MS. N
GOMERSALL FOR THE CROWN
MR. GILBERT PETERSON SC, MR. ADRIAN KAYNE AND MR.
JAHMAL MISICK FOR MICHAEL MISICK
MR. JEROME LYNCH KC, MR. JAMES SHEPHERD AND MR.
LEMELKO MISSICK FOR MC ALLISTER HANCHELL
MR. OLIVER SMITH KC, MR. KAYODE SMITH, AND MR. JAHMAL
MISICK FOR THOMAS CHALMERS MISICK.
DECISION
1

1. The first defendant Michael Eugene Misick (MM) was the Chief Minister
and subsequently the first Premier of the Turks and Caicos Islands (TCI).
He is charged with three counts of bribery.
2. The second defendant, Mc Allister Eugene Hanchell (McH) was a former
minister of government of TCI. He is charged with two counts of bribery.
3. The third defendant, Thomas Chalmers Misick (TCM), is the brother of MM,
and was a practicing attorney-at-law in the TCI. He is charged with four
counts of concealing or disguising the proceeds of criminal conduct, the
criminal conduct being that of MM and McH.
AGREED FACTS – CONSTITUTIONAL AND GENERAL MATTERS
4. TCI is a British Overseas Territory. The King of England is the Head of
State. At the time of the alleged offences, Queen Elizabeth the Second was
the Head of State. The Head of Government was the Chief Minister who
became the Premier after the 2006 Constitution came into force on 9th
August 2006. The Queen reigned over the TCI through a Governor
appointed by the Queen on the advice of the Secretary of State of the
Foreign Commonwealth Office (FCO). During the period 11th July 2005 to
14th July 2008 Richard Tauwhare (RT) was the Governor of TCI. The
Governor reports to the Director of the Overseas Territories Directorate
(OTD) in the FCO.
5. By section 6(i) of the 1988 Constitution the executive authority of the TCI
was vested in the Queen who exercised that authority through the Governor.
Section 7 created the Executive Council (ExCo), which consisted of the
Governor, a Chief Minister appointed by the Governor, five ministers
appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, the Chief
2

Secretary, and the Attorney-General (AG). The Governor presides over the
ExCo. The members all had equal standing during ExCo meetings.
6. The Governor was required by Section 5 of the 1988 Constitution to consult
the ExCo on the formulation of policy and in the exercise of all functions
conferred upon him except when acting under instructions given to him by
the Secretary of State.
7. Under Section 13, the Governor was responsible for defence, external affairs,
international and offshore financial relations, resources and services, and
internal security, including the police force and the administration of the
public service.
8. The 1988 Constitution was replaced by the 2006 Constitution which came
into force on 9th August 2006. The ExCo was replaced by the Cabinet and
the Chief Minister was now to be known as the Premier.
9. The 2006 Constitution was partially suspended by the United Kingdom
Government in 2009, and was replaced by a new Constitution in November
2011.
10. The Constitution requires that Cabinet give its decision in the form of advice
to the Governor who normally accepts and acts on the advice except where
the Constitution provides to the contrary. The Cabinet minutes use the form
of language “H.E. the Governor concurred and ordered accordingly”.
11. In 1998 a document entitled “Responsibilities and Procedures for ExCo and
Government Business” was published. In part 5 paragraph 32 included a
term that if matters were not on the agenda, they could only be considered
with the Governor’s consent and only if the matter was exceptionally
3

urgent. It was also provided that the ExCo would not normally be expected
to decide on policy matters on a subject raised orally.
12. All members of the ExCo were jointly responsible for all decisions under
the principle of “collective responsibility”. Each member was expected to
participate fully in discussions of proposals for decision whether in favor or
against. However, once a decision was reached members were obliged to
accept responsibility for the decision even if they held strong views against
it. The alternative was to resign.
13. The 2006 Constitution changed the Chief Minister’s title to Premier, and
ExCo’s to Cabinet, which consisted of the Governor, the Premier, the A.G.
and six ministers. One of the six ministers was designated as Deputy
Premier. Members of the Cabinet were also members of the House of
Assembly which consisted of the Speaker, 15 elected members, 4 appointed
members and the A.G. Under the 1988 Constitution the legislature was
known as The Legislative Council.
14. Cabinet meetings normally took place on a Wednesday. The Clerk to the
Cabinet gave the Governor draft copies of the cabinet papers five or six days
before the meeting. Cabinet papers were discussed in advance of the
meeting by the Governor with the AG and the Deputy Governor (who was
the Chief Secretary before 2006), and separately, in a bilateral meeting by
the Governor with the Chief Minister/Premier the day before the cabinet
meeting. These facts were denied by McH in the schedule of agreed facts.
However, they are supported by the evidence of RT.
15. The Premier chaired a “pre-cabinet” meeting with his ministers to discuss
issues and co-ordinate their views amongst themselves. This fact was
denied by McH, but supported by the evidence of RT.
4

16. Issues raised orally at Cabinet meetings without a supporting Cabinet paper
were referred to as “Oral Mentions”. Sometimes other documentation such
as correspondence from attorneys acting on behalf of a developer was made
available for consideration at oral mentions. These facts were denied by
McH, but were given in evidence by RT.
17. After Cabinet meetings, the Clerk to the Cabinet prepared draft minutes for
the Governor to approve before they were presented at the next meeting.
Once approved the Clerk issued Action Minutes to the person at the relevant
ministry responsible for putting the decision into effect.
18. The policy of ExCo/Cabinet requires that members who had an interest in
any matter before it (including the allocation of Crown Land), that member
was required to declare his interest and withdraw from the meeting. (This
was denied by McH).
19. The approval of ministers in Cabinet was required for funds falling under
the Governor’s special areas of responsibility. The Governor needed to
secure the agreement of the ministers for such funding. The TCI
Government received no funding from the UK Government except for
specific projects such as improvements to the prison. (These facts were
denied by McH.)
20. There are two main political parties in the TCI. The Progressive National
Party (PNP) and the People’s Democratic Movement (PDM). The PDM
was in power from 1995 to August 2003, when the PNP won the general
elections, and remained in power until 2009.
21. Floyd Hall was the Treasurer of the PNP as at 21st November 1997 up to
April 2010.
5

22. The PNP held two bank accounts – First Caribbean International Bank
(FCIB) Account No. 1537005 and Belize Bank Turks & Caicos Limited
(BB) Account No. 5001837 which was opened in June 2002. The four
signatories to the FCIB account were Floyd Hall, Trevor Cooke, Arabella
Smith and MM as at 6th November 1997 until April 2010. The signatories
on the BB account were MM, Floyd Hall and Arabella Smith. The account
application stated that the account was opened for the purpose of depositing
the party’s money and paying the party’s bills, and that the funds will be
used for campaign purposes and operating the party’s offices.
23. In November 2006 a FCIB account no. 10391978 was opened in the name
of South Caicos 2007 PNP.
24. In 2003 the PNP campaigned on the issue of corruption. In its manifesto it
promised to pass anticorruption legislation, and to ensure that ministers and
holders of public office do not accept gifts or hospitality nor in any way
compromise, or appear to compromise their office or make them beholden
to any individual company or entity.
25. MM took his oath as a member of the legislative council on 9th May 2003,
and as Chief Minister on 15th August 2003.
26. McH took his oath of office on 20th August 2003. He was appointed
Minister of Communications, Works and Utilities on 25th August 2003.
27. In September 2004 by oral mention in the ExCo the annual salary of the
Chief Minister was determined to be $125,000.00, and that of Minister
$115,000.00.
28. On 6th February 2006, McH was appointed Minister of Natural Resources
with responsibility for the Department of Conservation, Environment and
6

Coastal Resources, (DECR), Land Registry, Lands and Survey, Land
Valuation and Agriculture.
29. On 9th February 2007 the PNP was re-elected, winning 13 out of 15 seats.
On 12th February 2007, MM was appointed Premier and swore his oath of
allegiance. On the same day McH was sworn in as Minister of Natural
Resources, Fishing and Environment. On 23rd February 2007, MM and
McH became members of the House of Assembly.
30. On 18th April 2007 the annual salary of the Premier was determined by
Cabinet to be $150,000.00 plus certain benefits, and that of ministers to be
$125,000.00 plus $5,000.00 per month in allowances.
31. On 6th February 2008, a new ministerial code was accepted by Cabinet, but
was not brought into force prior to the departure of RT.
32. In 2007 an Integrity Bill was drafted and eventually passed as the Integrity
Commission Ordinance 2008 on 24th April 2008.
33. On 17th February 2009, McH became responsible for the Planning
Department.
34. The Gazette was the official government publication of legal and other
notices. The Responsibilities and Procedures for Executive Council and
Government Business was gazetted on 28th August 1998. Each member of
the ExCo was provided with a copy following an election.
35. Each member of the legislature (including ministers and the Chief Minister)
was required to file an annual report of their interests in compliance with
the House of Assembly (Registration of Interest) Ordinance 1993, detailing
his interests, and those of his spouse and children, together with his income
7

to the Registrar of Interests who collated and published them. In default,
the member may be referred to a select committee which may require that
defaulting member to comply. If the member made a false statement in a
declaration, he was liable to be fined up to $5,000.00 or suspended from
sitting and voting in the House of Assembly. Registers were kept for the
years 2003-2009.
36. The AG had the prime function of legal adviser to the Cabinet and was
responsible for issues relating to the legal profession, technical legislation,
the judiciary and the courts. David Jeremiah was the AG from 1997 to 2004
followed by Kurt De Freitas from January 2005. Rhondalee Braithwaite-
Knowles (RBK) the present AG, became Crown Counsel in the AG’s
Chambers in 2000, Principal Crown Counsel in 2006, and Deputy AG in
2008.
37. Each minister was supported by a senior public official called a Permanent
Secretary (PS).
BELONGERSHIP
38. The term “Belongership” was introduced by the Immigration Ordinance 13
of 1992. Up until 2012 it could be acquired in the following ways:
(i) By a person who was born in the TCI and at least one parent held
Belonger status or was born in the TCI;
(ii) By a person who was born outside the TCI and lawfully adopted by
a Belonger;
(iii) By the grant of a Certificate of Belonger status by the Govenor for
having made an outstanding contribution to the social or economic
development of the TCI;
(iv) By a person who is the dependent child of a Belonger, and
8

(v) By a spouse of a Belonger, who has made an application to the
Governor, and has lived with the Belonger for five consecutive
years.
39. A Belonger had the exclusive right to vote at a general election, sit on a
jury, apply for a scholarship, acquire certain types of business licences, and
purchase Crown Land at a discount.
40. Applications for Belonger status were placed before Cabinet for decision.
Ministers applied their own subjective criteria as to what constituted an
outstanding contribution to the economic and social development of the
Islands which was not defined in the Immigration Ordinance. The role of
the Governor was limited to checking for previous criminal convictions and
any past involvement with the TCI.
41. Applications for Belongership were placed before Cabinet by the
Permanent Residence Unit (PRU). Once agreed in principle by Cabinet, the
application was returned to the PRU, which published notice of the intended
award in the Gazette to provide an opportunity to members of the public to
object to the application. If no objections were received after two
consecutive advertisements, a Belonger Certificate was prepared by the
PRU for the Governor’s signature.
42. Mario Hoffman was granted Belonger status on 17th November 2006.
43. LisaRaye McCoy, MM’s wife, was proposed for Belonger status and
accepted by Cabinet on 7th March 2007. The decision was re-affirmed on
27th February 2008.
CROWN LAND
9

44. The Registered Land Ordinance requires that all land be registered either as
Crown Land or private land. Crown Land is defined by Section 2 of the
Physical Planning Ordinance 1998 as land of which the Crown is owner, or
is registered as the lessee under the Ordinance, or which is otherwise vested
in the Crown. Section 94 of the 2006 Constitution vested Crown Land in
the Governor who may dispose of it on behalf of the Crown.
45. Crown Land Policies (CLP) were issued in 1994 and 2005. The 1994 CLP
was updated and amended with effect from 1st April 2004. The 2005 CLP
was effective from 2nd November 2005, and was amended in early 2008.
46. Paragraphs 4.1 and 4.2 of the 1994 CLP expressed the commitment of the
Government to develop the TCI in order to improve the standard of living
of the Belonger population. As part of the development strategy, the
Government intended to use Crown Land for commercial and residential
development to encourage Belonger participation in the economy to benefit
the Belonger population and the community as a whole.
47. Developments of Crown Land were divided into the following categories:
(i) Residential;
(ii) Belonger only small commercial developments;
(iii) Belonger only large commercial developments;
(iv) Non-belonger developments, and
(v) Joint ventures.
48. Shaaban Hoza was the Chief Valuation Officer for TCI.
49. The Surveys and Mapping Department was formerly known as the Lands
and Surveys Department. It was within the Ministry of Environment and
District Administration, formerly the Ministry of Natural Resources. This
department was responsible for mapping the TCI, maintaining up to date
10

records of all land information and ensuring that surveys of Crown Land
were correctly executed.
50. On each of the Islands the land is divided into a number of contiguous
blocks. Each block is numbered. Each parcel has its own unique reference
number.
51. Applications for Crown Land were filed either at the Lands and Survey
Department or at various other offices at the Ministry. They were recorded
on a data base and forwarded to the PS of the Ministry of Natural Resources.
The PS or the Minister would then indicate which applications should be
processed.
52. From 2002 Conrad Higgs (now deceased) was the Chairman of TCInvest, a
statutory body established by the Turks and Caicos Islands Investment
Agency Ordinance 1975. TCInvest was concerned with promoting and
facilitating inward investment. According to Conrad Higgs and RBK,
TCInvest and the AG Chambers were supposed to be involved in the “front
end” of the registration of a Development Agreement (DA) with the
Attorneys for the developer. However, it not infrequently happened that
developers approached the Ministers before their proposals were referred to
TCInvest.
53. Clyde Robinson was the Director of Planning from 2003 to 2010. He
reported to the PS of the relevant ministry. Before 2003, that was the office
of Home Affairs. From 2003, it was the Premier’s Office until it came under
the auspices of the Ministry of Environment where McH was the
responsible minister.
54. The role of the Planning Department was to check planning applications for
compliance with the Planning Ordinance, standards and building
11

regulations. The Planning Department consulted with the DECR and other
relevant agencies. Once all the information has been submitted and advice
taken from the Planning Officer, the Director makes a recommendation to
the physical Planning Board either:
(a) To approve the application, or
(b) To approve with conditions, or
(c) To reject the application.
55. The Physical Planning Board (PPB) was a statutory body of not less than 7
members appointed by the Governor. The PPB usually met once a month.
Appeals from decisions of the PPB were dealt with by the Minister of
Planning.
56. When Crown Land was allocated to a developer, Cabinet was required to
approve, in outline, the terms of a Development Agreement (DA), including
concessions such as reductions in stamp duty, import duties and guarantees
of work permits for the construction staff. If the outline was approved, the
AG Chambers, the department responsible for protecting the interests of the
government, would take that as their basis for negotiations with the
developer.
57. Generally, DAs required the developers to invest a particular sum in the
project, setting out the phases of the development to be completed within a
set time-frame. When the final draft of a DA was completed, the AG
presented it to the Governor for signature. Proposed changes to the DA
were referred to Cabinet for approval, before being signed by the Governor.
Some proposed developments raised environmental issues or encroached
upon protected areas. All major commercial developments required an
Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) to be conducted to identify any
environmental issues, before the terms of the DA were concluded. (These
particular facts were denied by McH.)
12

58. On 1st April 2004 the 1994 CLP was updated with several amendments in
keeping with the government’s policy to empower Belongers. The amount
of land that would be allocated to a Belonger was increased from one acre
to one acre per island, provided that a home was completed on another
island.
59. The Minister of Natural Resources was given power to allocate land without
the matter being considered by the full ExCo, provided that the sub-division
of the land had previously been approved by ExCo.
60. On 2nd November 2005 a new CLP took effect. The new CLP set out a list
of 21 key principles to be considered in allocation decisions. It was
formulated to provide long-term benefits to Belongers by increasing their
role in commercial land development and broadening the availability of
land for residential use. The stated intention was expressed as
“Empowering our people, building our nation”.
61. The 2005 CLP provided for a maximum discount of 50% for Belongers on
the purchase price of Crown Land for residential purposes. If the land was
sold within 5 years, the full discount was to be repaid. If sold within 5-10
years, half the discount became repayable.
62. With respect to commercial developments, the 2005 CLP provided that no
individual Belonger would be granted a discounted on more than 10 acres.
For large developments, two or more Belongers were permitted to combine
their 10 acre entitlements. The discount of 50% did not apply to
Providenciales, where a discount of 25% was applicable. In the case of joint
ventures, the policy provided that Belongers must have at least a 51%
participation for the joint venture to qualify for a discount. Only the portion
of land to be purchased by Belongers would qualify for the discount. The
13

policy further required that new large-scale developments would be subject
to review and comment by the Planning Department and the DECR, and
their input would be included in the DA to be signed by the Governor and
the developer. The new requirements for open tendering and 51% Belonger
interest did not apply to projects already under consideration.
63. There was a practice underpinned and permitted by primary legislation, of
granting concessions to major commercial developers in order to attract
investment.
64. Building in protected areas was constrained by the CLP. These included
areas of significant ecological or historical importance. Paragraph 13 of the
2005 CLP provided that these areas will not be allocated for development.
65. On 23rd August 2006, Cabinet delegated authority to the Minister of Natural
Resources to renew offers on commercial CLPs provided that the Planning,
Lands and Survey and valuation conditions remain unchanged and the land
did not exceed 10 acres. The Minister was also authorized to approve
freehold titles on residential and commercial leases of Crown Land once the
lessee was in possession of a letter of commitment from a lending
institution. On 6th September 2006 Cabinet further delegated authority to
the minister to authorize freehold title on offer letters before CLPs were
executed, once the lessee was in possession of a letter of commitment from
a lending institution.
66. In April 2008, it was agreed that an independent review of the allocation of
Crown Land on Salt Cay, be conducted by Deloitte and Touche. The report
was completed in June 2008, and discussed in Cabinet on 3rd July 2008. It
did not find any evidence of wrongdoing by the developers.
14

67. On 11th June 2008, approval was granted to allow persons holding offer
letters of residential and commercial leases dated on or before that date, to
be granted an option to drawdown on the freehold title to that land (without
the requirements of a letter from a financial institution or a building up to
belt beam) at an additional 10% on the existing offer. The offer was valid
until 31st December 2008.
68. On 10th July 2008, the Governor of TCI announced a Commission of
Inquiry (COI). The COI’s interim report was submitted on 23rd February
2009. A final report was submitted on 31st May 2009.
69. MM resigned on 23rd March 2009.
AGREED LEGAL DIRECTIONS
70. In accordance with the current practice in several jurisdictions including the
UK, the attorneys have agreed legal directions. The general directions are
set out below. The directions that apply to the specific offences charged
will be set out and applied later in the judgment when the Court deals with
the specific counts on the indictment.
71. The burden is on the Crown to prove that each defendant is guilty of the
offences charged. The standard of proof required is that the Crown must
prove their case beyond reasonable doubt, that is to make the court sure of
the guilt of the accused. There is no burden on any of the defendants to
prove his innocence. Indeed, there is no burden on any defendant to prove
any issue in the case.
72. The court must consider the case of each defendant separately on each count
on the indictment, and must return separate verdicts on each count against
each defendant. Where a defendant faces more than one count the court
15

must consider each count separately and return separate verdicts on each
count. In addition, where a defendant is charged together with another
defendant on the same count, the court must consider the case against each
defendant separately and deliver separate verdicts for each defendant.
73. The defendants elected to remain silent, that is, not to give evidence. This
is their right. They are entitled to remain silent and argue that the
prosecution has not proved the case against them. No adverse inference
may be drawn against the defendant from exercising his right to remain
silent. In this case it is not in dispute that the defendants have no previous
convictions and are of good character.
74. The principles set out by the English Court of Appeal in R v Vye [1993] 1
WLR 471 establish that the good character of a defendant must be taken
into consideration in two ways:
(i) In assessing his credibility that is whether or not he is speaking the
truth, and;
(ii) In assessing his propensity, that is the likelihood that a man of good
character will commit the offence for which he is charged.
75. Good character supports the credibility of a defendant. It must be taken into
account where a defendant has given evidence or has given pre-trial
statements to the police, whether in interview or otherwise. In this case,
none of the defendants have given pre-trial statements and have all elected
not to give evidence. It follows that there is no evidence or pre-trial
statements to which the credibility limb of the Vye direction can be applied.
76. However, all counsel argue, and the court concurs that the propensity limb
must be considered in relation to all three defendants. The court must
consider that it is less likely that a defendant who has no previous
convictions, and against whom no allegations of previous misconduct or
16

offending against the criminal law have been made, is less likely to commit
the offence for which he is charged. This principle will be applied to each
defendant on each count.
77. The events on which this case is based took place in some cases, more than
20 years ago. The court must consider the effect of the passage of time on
the memories of the witnesses, particularly where the evidence is not
supported by contemporaneous documents. In addition, while there is no
direct evidence on the availability of witnesses, the court must consider that
the defendants may not have been able to call witnesses who may have died,
or cannot be traced, or who are now unable to recall events or details of
certain matters. In addition, documents that may have been useful may now
be lost or cannot be traced.
78. The court must further consider whether or not the defendants might have
been disadvantaged by their inability to cross-examine witnesses, who due
to age or infirmity or death, did not appear at the trial and whose testimony
at the aborted trial before Harrison J. was played at this trial.
79. Where the delay has placed a defendant at a material disadvantage in
meeting the case against him, the court must take this into account in his
favor: R v Hewitt [2020] EWCA 1247.
80. Certain facts were agreed or undisputed by the parties. The facts set out in
the updated schedule of facts are accepted by the parties as the truth of the
fact stated, without necessarily accepting that it was within their knowledge
or suspicion at the time and “subject to errors or omissions, and/or subject
to further explanations and/or clarification elicited from any witness”
during the course of the trial, or relevance to the issues being tried.
17

81. The statements of Conrad Higgs were read into the record because he is
deceased. The admission of the statements did not mean that the Crown
and the defence agree that all the contents are true. It is for the court to
decide on the weight to be attached to the statements bearing in mind the
following limitations:
(i) The statements were not made under oath or affirmation, albeit that
Conrad Higgs signed a formal declaration at the beginning of each
of his statements that his statement was true and he knew that he
could be prosecuted if he deliberately made a statement that was
false.
(ii) The defence were deprived of the opportunity to cross-examine the
witness, which may have shaken the credibility or reliability of the
evidence.
(iii) In deciding the weight to be attached to the evidence the court must
consider it in the light of other evidence in the case.
82. The Crown’s case relies heavily on circumstantial evidence. The directions
on this issue are of vital importance and the Court will set out in full the
agreed legal directions.
Circumstantial Evidence
“36. Circumstantial evidence must always be “narrowly examined...It is
also necessary before drawing an inference of an accused’s guilt from
circumstantial evidence to be sure that there are no other co-existing
circumstances which might weaken or destroy the inference.” - see R v
Teper [1952] UKPC 15, per Lord Normand. Any doubt as to effect of an
inference or any doubt as to the effect of co-existing circumstances must be
resolved in favour of the defendants.
18

37. In some cases, there is direct evidence that a defendant is guilty, for
example evidence from an eyewitness who saw the defendant committing
the crime, or a confession from the defendant that he/she committed it.
38. In other cases, like the present one, there is no such direct evidence and
the prosecution rely on (what is sometimes referred to as) circumstantial
evidence. That means different strands of evidence which do not directly
prove that MM/McH/TCM is guilty, but which do, say the prosecution,
leave no doubt that each considered separately is guilty when the strands
are drawn together.
39. On the other hand, the defence say that that evidence, in particular, gives
rise at least to reasonable doubt that each of MM/McH/TCM is guilty.
40. The interrelationship between the various strands of the circumstantial
evidence is best defined by the renowned passage in Exall 176 E.R. 850 (at
853) where Pollock CB expressed the test over one hundred and fifty years
ago as:
“It has been said that circumstantial evidence is to be considered as a chain,
and each piece of evidence is a link in the chain, but that is not so, for then,
if any one link breaks, the chain would fall. It is more like the case of a rope
comprised of several cords. One strand of the cord might be insufficient to
sustain the weight, but three stranded together may be quite of sufficient
strength. Thus, it may be in circumstantial evidence-there may be a
combination of circumstances, no one of which would raise a reasonable
conviction or more than a mere suspicion; but the three taken together may
create a conclusion of guilt with as much certainty as human affairs can
require or admit of.”
41. Pitchford LJ in R v Massih [2015] EWCA Crim 477, suggested the
correct question is this:
19

“could a reasonable jury, properly directed, exclude all realistic
possibilities consistent with the defendant’s innocence?”
42. A further statement of principle is to be found in a Privy Council appeal
from Hong Kong, Kwan Ping Bong v R [1979] A.C. 609 in which Lord
Diplock referred to an inference of guilt as having to be compelling. He put
it thus: “The requirement of proof beyond all reasonable doubt does not
prevent a jury from inferring, from the facts that have been the subject of
direct evidence before them, the existence of some further fact, such as
the knowledge or intent of the accused, which constitutes an essential
element of the offence; but the inference must be compelling - one (and
the only one) that no reasonable man could fail to draw from the direct
facts proved.”
43. In R v Jabber [2006] EWCA 2694, Moses LJ made the following
observation of Lord Diplock's dicta above in the context of a submission of
no case to answer at the close of the prosecution’s case:
“Read literally, Lord Diplock’s dicta might be understood to be saying
that an inference was only to be regarded as compelling if all juries,
assumed to be composed of those who are reasonable, would be bound to
draw such an inference. In short, an inference could only be drawn if no
one would dissent from it. We reject that as an approach to be taken by
the judge at the close of the prosecution case, even where the evidence is
only circumstantial. The correct approach is to ask whether a reasonable
jury, properly directed, would be entitled to draw an adverse inference. To
draw an adverse inference from a combination of factual circumstances
necessarily does involve the rejection of all realistic possibilities consistent
with innocence. But that is not the same as saying that anyone
considering those circumstances would be bound to reach the same
conclusion. That is not an appropriate test for a judge to apply on the
submission of no case.
20

The correct test is the conventional test of what a reasonable jury would
be entitled to conclude.”
44. The specimen direction in respect of circumstantial evidence is as
follows (at 10-3 of the Crown Court Compendium Part I July 2024, page
175-176 pdf):
There is no direct evidence that D committed the crime with which D is
charged. For example, there is no CCTV footage of D committing the
offence.
Instead, the prosecution rely on what is sometimes known as circumstantial
evidence. Circumstantial evidence means pieces of evidence relating to
different circumstances, none of which on their own directly proves that D
is guilty but which, say the prosecution, when taken together, leave no doubt
that D is guilty.
[Summarise the pieces of evidence on which the prosecution rely
and the conclusions they say should be drawn from them.]
The defence say that you should not accept [some of] these pieces of
evidence.
[Identify the pieces of evidence concerned, and summarise the
defence arguments about them.]
The defence also say that the evidence on which the prosecution rely does
not in fact prove D’s guilt at all. They say that there are too many gaps and
too many unanswered questions.
[Summarise the defence arguments about this.]
You must decide which, if any, of these pieces of evidence you think are
reliable and which, if any, you do not. You must then decide what
conclusions you can fairly and reasonably draw from any pieces of evidence
21

that you do accept, taking these pieces of evidence together. You must not
however engage in guesswork or speculation about matters which have not
been proved by any evidence. Finally, you must weigh up all of the evidence
and decide whether the prosecution have made you sure that D is guilty.
45. The Court should therefore examine each of the strands of
circumstantial evidence relied on by the prosecution, decide which if any it
accepts and which if any it does not, and decide what fair and reasonable
conclusions can be drawn from any evidence that the Court accepts.
46. However, the Court must not speculate or guess or make theories about
matters which in its view are not proved by any evidence.
47. It is for the Court to decide, having weighed up all the evidence put
before it, whether the prosecution have made the Court sure that one or more
of the defendants, if any, is guilty.”
83. A politician dealing with Crown Land owes a fiduciary duty to the Crown
and has a duty of disclosure out of loyalty to the Crown’s interests.
THE INDICTMENT
84. At the beginning of the trial the indictment contained five counts, three
counts of bribery involving MM, and McH, and two counts of concealing
or disguising the proceeds of criminal conduct against TCM.
85. Count four charged TCM with concealing or disguising the proceeds of the
criminal conduct of MM between 1st August 2003 and 31st August 2009,
while count five was a similar charge involving the proceeds of the criminal
conduct of McH, between the same dates.
22

86. In his written closing submissions Mr. Oliver Smith KC submitted on behalf
of TCM, that the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance 1998 under which TCM was
charged was repealed and replaced by the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance
2007 which came into force on 8th October 2007. Mr. Smith KC argued
that transactions that post-dated the life of the 1998 Ordinance should not
be considered by the Court, and must be excluded in considering Counts 4
and 5.
87. In his Written Reply, Mr. Mitchell KC pointed out that it was open to TCM
to make an application to exclude the post 8th October 2007 transactions
when the evidence was being led during the Crown’s case, but did not
choose to do so. Mr. Mitchell KC suggested that the Court should determine
the issue before closing speeches were made. He further submitted that the
offence was a continuing offence, and continues as if it had not been
repealed. He relied on Sections 22 and 23 of the Interpretation Act and
Section 4 of the 2007 Ordinance. In the event that the Court disagreed with
his submissions, Mr. Mitchell KC indicated that he would seek to amend
the indictment to add Counts 6 and 7 to meet what he considered to be a
merely technical objection in relation to post 7th October 2007 transactions.
The proposed addition of Counts 6 and 7 were intended to include specific
acts of TCM which occurred after 7th October 2008.
88. The Court dealt with the issue at the start of Mr. Smith’s oral address, in
which he submitted that section 30(2)(a) of the 1998 Ordinance under which
TCM was charged, was materially different from s.124 of the 2007
Ordinance, which replaced that section. He argued that s.124 imposed strict
liability whereas s.30(2)(a) of the 1998 legislation required the Crown to
prove mens rea, being knowledge or reasonable grounds to suspect that the
property in whole or in part was the proceeds of criminal conduct. The
proposed amendment would therefore be “massively prejudicial” to TCM,
and ought not to be allowed.
23

89. In response, Mr. Mitchell KC referred to s.122 (1)(b) of the 2007 Ordinance,
which requires the Crown to prove that the alleged offender knows or
suspects that the criminal property constitutes or represents the benefit
derived from criminal conduct. It followed that the offence was not one of
strict liability.
90. In reply, Mr. Smith KC submitted that there was a substantial change in the
mens rea of the offence. The Crown was no longer required to prove
“reasonable grounds” to suspect. Instead the required mental element was
“suspects”. Further the Crown was relieved of the burden of proving the
purpose of the actus reus being, to avoid the making or enforcement of a
confiscation order or avoiding prosecution for unlawful conduct.
91. The Court considered the submissions of counsel. The Court disagreed with
the Crown that these were continuing offences. Where there was a course
of criminal conduct, the criminal acts that are the subject of counts 4 & 5
are discrete acts, not a continuing unlawful act such as a public nuisance.
92. The Court further considered that there was no significant change to the
Crown’s case against TCM. The evidence was the same. The change in the
mens rea from “reasonable grounds to suspect” to “suspects” may actually
favor the defence, since the language appears to require a subjective rather
than an objective basis for criminal liability. In practice, the distinction may
be purely academic. TCM elected to give no evidence. It was open to him
to testify that he did not suspect that he was receiving and disbursing
proceeds of crime. He elected not to give evidence, as is his right. In any
event even if he decided to give evidence, it is open to the tribunal fact to
assess the evidence, and decide on the basis of the evidence, whether or not
he actually “suspected”. Whether or not he actually suspected must contain
24

an objective analysis of the knowledge he would have possessed at the
material time.
93. With respect to Mr. Smith KC’s submission that the burden of the Crown
was made less onerous since they were no longer required to provide a
specific intent to avoid the making or enforcement of a confiscation order
or avoiding prosecution for unlawful conduct, that is a matter of inference
for the finder of fact from the evidence. There is no change in the evidence
or in the case that the defence has to answer.
94. The court further considered the authorities that were cited by the Crown.
In Tirado [1974] 59 Cr. App. R 30, Lord Widgery LJ, was quite emphatic
in his affirmation of the basic principle that the essential test is whether the
amendment to the indictment can be made without injustice to the
defendant.
95. The cases of Teong Sun Chuah and Teong Tatt Chuah [1991] Crim LR
463, Nelson [1977] 65 Cr. App. R 119, Bonner [1974] Crim. L.R. 479 and
Collison [1980] 71. Cr. App. R.249, all support the principle that an
amendment to the indictment may be allowed even at a late stage of trial
once it can be made without injustice to the defendant.
96. Having considered the submissions of counsel and the authorities cited, the
Court determined that the proposed amendment to the indictment does not
significantly change the case that TCM has to answer. Accordingly, the
Court granted the amendment sought on the basis that it would not result in
injustice to TCM.

COUNT 1
98. Count 1 charges MM with the offence of bribery in that, between 1st June
2004 and 31st March 2007 he accepted inducements directly or indirectly
on behalf of the Beaches group (including Gordon Arthur Butch Stewart)
by unlawful corrupt payments or other rewards.
99. The Crown’s case against MM is set out in its summary dated 15th July
2024. The facts upon which the case is based have been largely undisputed
or agreed, and are supported by references to documentary evidence which
has been admitted into evidence without objection by the Defence. The
Court will highlight matters in dispute later in this judgment when
consideration is given to the submissions of Defence Counsel.
Undisputed Facts
100. Beaches is a resort on Grace Bay in Providenciales operated by TCI Hotels
Ltd (Beaches), a subsidiary of Sandals Resorts International (SRI). Unique
Vacations Inc. (UVI), a company based in Miami, USA, is responsible for
reservations, sales, marketing, advertising and promotion of the Sandals
brand. Gordon Arthur Stewart (GAS), widely known as “Butch” Stewart
was the Chairman of SRI. Dr. Jeffrey Pyne was Treasurer of SRI. Dr. Pyne
was responsible for movement of cash. Lourdes Blanco, Financial
Comptroller of UVI, received instructions from Dr. Pyne as to
disbursements of money received by UVI on behalf of SRI.
101. Beaches planned to extend its resort on Providenciales. It needed to
acquire additional land for this purpose. To this end, it sought to acquire
the shares in Summer Nights Ltd. (SNL) which held the rights to 5 acres of
29

beachfront adjoining land. In October 2002, SNL and the TCI Government
(TCIG) signed a Development Agreement (DA) for a 140 room hotel to be
built on parcel 60803/89. On 24th February 2005 the parcel was transferred
by TCIG to SNL.
102. On 5th March 2004, TCI Hotels Ltd entered into an agreement with the
shareholders of SNL to purchase the shares in SNL. On 23rd November
2004, a further agreement was signed for the remaining shares for $1m.
103. On 22nd April 2005, Misick and Stanbrook (M&S), a firm of attorneys
acting for SRI, wrote to MM asking for a valuation of 1.5 acres of Crown
land of parcel 60803/81 and suggested that an additional 1.5 acres might be
dealt with at the same time. The writer asked MM to take the necessary
steps to obtain the approval of ExCo for the transfer of 3 acres at a market
value to be determined by the Valuation Office.
104. On 19th May 2005, Galmo Williams, then Minister of Natural Resources,
introduced Paper 05/353 to ExCo, recommending the sale of 1.5 acres to
Beaches and 1.59 acres to remain Crown land to be set aside as a cultural
market. The paper further noted a valuation of the 1.5 acres at $825,000 by
the Chief Valuation Officer and a value of $737,000 for 1.34 acres as
additional land for the Beaches expansion.
105. The parcels of 1.5 acres, 1.59 acres, and 1.34 acres were originally part of
parcel 60803/81 of Crown land. The 1.5 acres was beachfront, while the
1.59 acres was to the back. There were two problems with the proposed
sale of the 1.5 acres of beachfront. It had been earmarked for a cultural
market for vendors who sold their articles on the beach. The vendors were
unhappy with the proposed relocation of the market. In addition, Provo
Water Company (Provo) held a lease over that portion of land, and a well
was located on it, which would have to be relocated. Sandals indicated to
30

TCIG that they were prepared to build the cultural market on the 1.59 acre
parcel.
106. On 7th July 2005, by Oral Mention at ExCo, MM raised the matter of the
paper brought to ExCo on 19th May 2005. He noted that there was “a huge
difference” between the government valuation of the plots and a private
valuation carried out. He further noted that Beaches had offered to build
the vendors’ stalls at the cultural market, which would result in savings to
Government, and that the proposed expansion would generate a lot of
revenue for the economy. He suggested a reduction in the sale price for the
two plots of 1.5 and 1.34 acres to $700,000 for both. Council agreed to the
proposal. The market value of the plots were assessed at $825,000 and
$737,000. The reduction therefore saved Beaches $862,000.
107. On 22nd December 2005, Ariel Misick QC of M&S wrote to MM, referring
to a meeting between MM and Dr. Pyne of SRI two days earlier, and
requesting confirmation that the stamp duty on 20 parcels of land purchased
by Beaches had been reduced from $2.497m to $1m. On 9th January 2006
Floyd Hall, Deputy Chief Minister, wrote to the Registrar of Lands,
advising that in accordance with section 32(1) of the Stamp Duty
Ordinance, stamp duty should be assessed in the sum of $1m as outlined in
Ariel Misick’s QC, letter of 22nd December 2005, thus saving Beaches
$1,497,012.50 in stamp duty.
108. By letter dated 4th July 2006 to MM, Ariel Misick QC outlined the
proposed expansion of the Beaches Hotel and Resort to include an Italian
Seaside Village and Water Park at an estimated cost of US$100m, and a
casino and entertainment complex. To this end Mr. Misick QC proposed
certain concessions to be included in the new Development Agreement
(DA).
31

These included:
- exemption for payment on all taxes, duties and levies on the importation
of all hotel promotional literature for the life of the hotel;
- exemption from the payment of all customs and other duties for a period
of 25 years on building and construction materials, furniture fittings and
equipment;
- exemption from payment of property taxes for 20 years;
- exemption from the payment of customs duties on 4 motor vehicles to
be used by the hotel; including a 15 seater bus, and 2 limousines;
- exemptions from customs and other duties on 5 battery powered
vehicles/carts;
- exemption from payment of any taxes on profits, gains and other
turnover attributable to the development;
- exemption from any levy or taxes on capital invested;
- exemption from customs import duties and other duties on the
importation of construction equipment, and
- government support for a casino license to an operator nominated by
Sandals.
109. On 12th July 2006, by Oral Mention at an ExCo meeting MM circulated
the letter of 4th July 2006, and outlined Beaches’ plans for expansion, and
the benefits that both the TCIG and Beaches would achieve. The Acting
A.G. asked whether an independent assessment of the economic benefits of
the project to TCI would be conducted given evidence elsewhere in the
Caribbean that all-inclusive resorts provided limited benefits to the wider
economy. The President (RT) agreed, and argued that the proposed
concessions might be more than was strictly necessary to attract the
investment. He argued that in the absence of an active negotiation to reach
a mutually acceptable deal, it could not be established that TCI was getting
the best possible value for money. MM agreed that his policy was not to
encourage more all-inclusive resorts. However, he was fully satisfied that
32

the benefits to be gained far outweighed the concessions on offer. After
further discussions Council advised that TCInvest and the AG Chambers
should negotiate a DA with Beaches on substantially the same concessions
requested by Beaches, except that a 5% rate of customs duties was to be
imposed on building and construction materials and furniture and fittings
for 25 years, and a rate of 5% on customs and other duties on the 4 motor
vehicles to be imported.
110. On 19th July 2006, MM wrote to Ariel Misick QC setting out ExCo’s
decision. On 13th September 2006, Cabinet authorised the Governor to sign
the DA, which contained the concessions as agreed by the ExCo on 12th
July 2006, as well as a scholarship contribution of $35,000 per year, which
RBK testified should have been $114,000 a year. On the same day
Belongership status was granted to GAS and Dr. Pyne. The DA was
eventually signed by the Governor on 11th October 2006.

122. The facts as set out above are not disputed. The decisions made by TCIG
and MM are all supported by contemporary documents, as are the payments
made to Prestigious Properties, Chalmers & Co, and the PNP. The case for
the Crown is that clear and irresistibly cogent inferences to be drawn from
the undisputed evidence lead to the sure conclusion that the payments were
intended as bribes to MM to provide advantages to the developer, and were
received by MM as such.
Defence Submissions
123. MM elected not to give evidence or to call witnesses in his defence, as he
is entitled to do. On his behalf Counsel has made submissions on the
evidence and on the law, which are set out hereunder.
124. On behalf of MM, Mr. Peterson SC pointed out that at the material time
there was no law regulating political funding. The Political Activities
Ordinance did not come into effect until 20th August 2012. There were no
laws to control how political parties disbursed political donations, or the
quantum of such donations.
37

125. Mr. Peterson SC referred to the evidence of Barrie Cooke given at the first
trial on 25th July 2017. Mr. Cooke was the beneficial owner of the HAB
Group. He testified that the group made contributions to both parties, and
expected no favour from either party. Similarly, Mark Durliat, Chief
Executive of Grace Bay Resorts testified at the first trial, that they were
approached by both parties for donations, but they were under no pressure
to make any donations. They made donations to both parties.
126. Mr. Peterson SC further submitted that the Crown called no witness from
the PNP to complain of the misappropriation of political donations by MM.
It is argued that when MM drew on these funds, he was entitled to do so,
since there was a rebuttable presumption that there was an implied
understanding within the party at this time that MM could draw on these
funds at will, as and when a need arose, and that there was a co-mingling of
funds.
127. Mr. Peterson SC at paragraph 30 of his written submissions concedes that
on the face of it, given the amounts of the payments, and the way some
payments were distributed, might raise a suspicion. However, he argues
that the Crown has to concede that the amounts and the disbursements alone,
were not sufficient to displace the presumption of innocence.
128. In reply, the Crown pointed out that the case against MM was not based
on the Political Activities Act, but on the common law fiduciary duties, and
the disclosure obligations created by the House of Assembly (Registration
of Interest) 1993 Act Cap 1.6. Further, the fact that there was no cap on the
amount of political donations does not explain the secrecy with which the
funds were treated. If they were legitimate, why were they not disclosed?
One would expect that for the honest and prudent politician, the disclosure
obligations and fiduciary duties would come into play given the large sums
received.
38

129. The Crown further submits that the presumption of innocence is not a
defence. It is capable of rebuttal. It is the combination and coincidence of
the large payments, their concealment, their disbursement, and the making
of decisions favourable to the developer that provide the compelling
evidence.
130. Mr. Peterson SC submits that the concessions granted to the developers
involved in this trial were lawfully granted and in compliance with section
6 of the Encouragement of Development Ordinance CAP. 20:01. He argues
that the concessions offered to developers were a matter for political
judgement, balancing the need for inward investment, development and the
enhancement of the islands’ economy against any perceived advantage to
the developer. There was nothing illegal in any of the political decisions
taken, provided that they were not influenced by corruption. If the Court is
not sure that the concessions were influenced by corrupt payments or other
rewards, they cannot be characterized as criminal. Mr. Peterson noted as
well that the concessions requested by Mr. Misick QC were not all granted.
131. With respect to the Crown Land Policy which was raised from time to
time, Mr. Peterson SC pointed out that it was merely a policy which was
never enshrined by statute, and so had no force of law. The TCIG was free
to depart from it whenever it considered it necessary or desirable to do so
in order to deliver on its electoral mandate.
132. Senior Counsel further submitted that the document entitled
“Responsibilities and Procedures for Executive Council and Government
Business” gazetted on 28th August 1998, which set standards of behavior
and ethical guidelines for Ministers of Government did not have force of
law, and were in fact “wholly unrealistic”. Accordingly, departure from the
guidelines did not constitute criminal conduct.
39

133. As to the Register of Members’ Interests, Senior Counsel inferred from the
evidence of Alfred Been and RT that there was a “long standing and deep-
rooted culture of complacency, non-compliance and non-disclosure in
relation to the completion of the annual registers and that nothing sinister
should be read into any finding that particular sections of the Register were
not completed”. He described the procedure as “effectively useless,
toothless and difficult to enforce”.
134. Mr. Peterson SC referred to the evidence of RBK, who argued that there
was nothing wrong in principle with the use of oral mentions in Cabinet
meetings, and that the governor could defer the matter, or call for further
information, if he considered it appropriate. Counsel also referred to the
evidence of RT in cross-examination, where he stated that the Premier
would generally inform him at their bilateral meetings the day before
Cabinet met, that he intended to make an oral mention. He agreed that there
was nothing necessarily sinister with the use of an oral mention.
135. Senior Counsel further argued that RBK’s evidence that the concessions
granted to Beaches were “overly generous” was no more than an opinion
expressed. He further submitted that the concessions were “not dissimilar”
to those granted to SNL under the previous administration.
136. With respect to MM’s letter of 19th July 2006 to Ariel Misick QC
informing him of the concessions granted by ExCo on 12th July 2006, Mr.
Peterson SC suggested that the proper inference to be drawn was that MM
was simply conveying to the developer that the TCIG fully supported the
expansion plans.
137. Senior Counsel criticized the evidence of RT, who felt that the concessions
granted were “overly generous” and made without the benefit of an
40

objective cost benefit analysis. In Mr. Peterson’s submission, if RT was so
strongly opposed to the concessions, he could have deferred the decision
until a cost benefit analysis was done, instead of caving in to MM’s
contention that the concessions were a fundamental part of the deal,
construction was ready to begin, and a delay could be costly. Further, RT
agreed in cross-examination, that in coming to his opinion that concessions
were overly generous, he had no comparator, and did not recall doing any
research on concessions granted before his arrival in TCI.
138. In Mr. Peterson’s view, the expansion project was a “hugely significant
economic development” for TCI, with a substantial investment of $114m.
It was not surprising that Beaches sought significant concessions.
139. With respect to the payments made by UVI, Mr. Peterson SC pointed out
that the witnesses Lourdes Blanco and Steve Carey had no personal
knowledge of the reasons for the payments and were no more than
“document producers”, who simply acted on instructions received from Dr.
Pyne. Ms. Blanco was unable to say whether the payments may have been
campaign financing payments “misdescribed” in the SRI/UVI records.
Further, the documents produced were internal accounting records of UVI
and SRI and there was no evidence that they were ever seen by MM.
140. Further, Senior Counsel submitted that Inspector Paul Farrell who
produced the Beaches Charts, did no more than show the movement of
funds, and produce banking and other documents. Mr. Farrell had no direct
personal knowledge of the reasons behind the payments, and had not
addressed his mind to the issue of campaign financing.
141. In addition, the payments of $150,000 on 30th October 2006, and $350,000
on 22nd January 2007 came through a reputable firm of attorneys (M&S),
against whom there is no allegation of impropriety. The Court was invited
41

to conclude that the firm would not knowingly have involved itself in
channeling funds that might be remotely regarded as bribes. Further, the
payments preceded the 9th February 2007 elections, raising the clear
inference that they were campaign contributions.
142. With respect to the payment of $500,000 paid to Chalmers Misick and
Co’s FCIB account, Counsel submitted that the court should consider it in
the light of the letter dated 17th September 2010 from Chalmers Misick &
Co to Patterson Mair Hamilton, which states that the payment was a
campaign contribution.
143. In closing his submissions on Count 1, Mr. Peterson SC posed the question
whether a fair-minded tribunal of fact, properly directing itself on the
essential elements of the offence of bribery, could reasonably infer, to the
exclusion of all other reasonable inferences which are plainly open to it on
the totality of the evidence, that any of these payments were:
(a) bribes paid by Beaches (including GAS),
(b) intended as such by them, and
(c) knowingly and intentionally received as such by MM so that he
would act contrary to the ordinary rules of honesty and integrity
expected of him as a Minister of the Crown.
144. The conclusion, Counsel submits, is that the Court should find that the case
is not proved beyond reasonable doubt.
Crown’s Submissions
145. The Crown accepted that the granting of concessions to developers was
settled practice in TCI, and the concessions granted were within the
confines of the Encouragement of Development Act. Their contention is
that MM was influenced by benefits given to him as a reward for
42

concessions and other advantages offered to the developer. The Crown
agreed with MM’s submission that “there is nothing illegal in any of the
political decisions taken in this case provided that they were not influenced
by corruption”, and submitted that MM (and McH) were motivated not by
any desire “to change the economic landscape of the islands for the better”
but by personal enrichment.
146. In relation to the $500,000 payment from UVI to PPL on 4th March 2004,
the Crown referred to a letter of instruction to Hambros Bank, signed by
Steven Carey, a Director of SRI, and Phyllis Thompson, Company
Secretary, requesting the transfer to PPL, and stating that the payment
represented a deposit on the purchase of 3 acres of land in TCI. The Crown
submits that there is no evidence that MM or PPL were acting as agents for
the sale of land to SRI, nor was MM selling land to SRI. The $500,000 was
quickly disbursed by PPL for MM’s benefit. The plain inference, the Crown
submits is that the payment was corrupt, and was disguised as a deposit to
hide it.
147. Further, the Crown argues, there is no evidential basis for the contention
that the payment was a political donation. If it was, why was it stated to be
the payment of a deposit on land purchased, and why was $400,000 of it
used to purchase properties for MM? If it was a loan why describe it as
something else, and where is the evidence of a loan agreement between PPL
and SRI, and why is there no evidence of repayment?
148. In response to MM’s submission that Inspector Farrell did not address his
mind to the issue of campaign financing, and that all the officer purports to
do was to show the movement of funds, the Crown replies that it was
precisely the movement of funds, and the way they were recorded on
sending, in business documents, and concealed in accounts on receipt, that
“speaks volumes” as to the true intention.
43

149. The Crown further sought to connect the Oral Mention of 7th July 2005,
made by MM in the ExCo meeting in which MM proposed the reduction in
the purchase price of two parcels of land to Beaches to $700,000, in the face
of government valuations for $825,000 and $737,000 (resulting in a saving
to Beaches of $862,000.00), to the payment of $350,000 from UVI to PPL.
This payment was described as “LAND PAYMENT IN TURKS” in the
internal accounts of UVI. Again, the Crown submits, there is no evidence
of a sale of land by MM or PPL to SRI.
150. The Crown made reference to the relocation of the proposed cultural
market, and the surrender of the lease by Provo Water, and the eventual

151. The Crown further pointed to events on 20th, 21st, and 22nd December
2005, which the Crown suggests are connected. There was a meeting
between MM and Dr. Pyne on 20th December 2005. The next day UVI

firm M&S (SRI’s attorneys), wrote to MM referring to the meeting two days
before between MM and Dr. Pyne, in which there was an agreed reduction

(Minister of Finance) to the Registrar of Lands. The saving achieved by

MM by SRI through UVI.
152. The Crown submits that the payments made by UVI to Chalmers Misick
& Co in March 2006, were for a corrupt purpose and disbursed by TCM for
the benefit of MM.
44

153. The payment of $500,000 was referenced in UVI Daily Funds Distribution
document as “GAS (Legal)”. Mrs. Blanco testified that she did not address
her mind at the time what the word ‘GAS’ meant. She later came to know

154. The Crown further submits that despite numerous Production Orders being
served on Chalmers Misick and Co., no ledgers were ever produced. The
only documents that provided any kind of ledger account were those

which was a ledger sheet for Windsor West Ltd (WWL), and CX 1839,

CX 1789 records a $200,000 payment on 03/06/2006 as a “deposit” in the

155. The Crown submits that the lack of any client ledger entry in respect of

45

to account for some of the payments which came to his client account for

156. The Crown submits that some of the disbursements made by TCM from

- $130,000.00 on 9th March 2006 to Preston Bailey was connected to the

- $30,000 on 15th March 2006 to Sky Juice, for caterers who provided

- $28,994.85 to Brilliantmont International School, Switzerland, recorded

- $33,213.32 on 22nd March 2006 for MM’s Amex Centurion credit card.
- $1.1m drawn on Chalmers Misick & Co (FCIB) client account and

157. The Crown submits that the payments of $500,000 made by UVI to
Chalmers & Co and the subsequent disbursements, coupled with the

158. The Crown estimates that between December 2005 and January 2006,
Sandals made savings of just under $3m because of MM’s influence.
Shortly thereafter $500,000 was paid into Chalmers Misick & Co’s client

relation to reality”. Notably, the UVI internal account described the
payment of $200,000 as “GAS LEGAL”, while that payment and a later

account, in the absence of any evidence of any sale of land or anything else.
46

The clear inference, it is suggested, is that the payments were corrupt
payments made in return for decisions made by MM for Sandals’ benefit.
159. The Crown further highlights that the reductions granted to Beaches on the
sale of the 1.34 acre plot valued at $737,000, and the 1.5 acre plot valued at
$825,000, resulted in a saving to Beaches/Sandals of $862,000. The later
reduction in stamp duty resulted in further saving of $1.497m. In addition,
the concessions requested in the letter from Ariel Misick QC were
substantially granted except that ExCo declined a total reduction in customs
duties and imposed a rate of 5% customs duties for 25 years, and an

160. With respect to MM’s submission that the Governor could have exercised
his power of deferral when the decisions were taken, the Crown replies that
RT’s reluctance to exercise this power must be seen in the context of his

elected government and the hostility with which his perceived interference
in their work was met. Further RT (and RBK) were unaware of the
payments to MM at the time.
161. With respect to the payments made by Misick & Stanbrook (M&S) to the
PNP accounts, and MM’s suggestion that M&S were a highly respected
firm of attorneys against whom there is no allegations of impropriety, and
the Court may safely conclude that the firm would not have knowingly
involved itself in the channeling of bribes, the Crown replies that the Court
has heard no evidence on the issue of M&S’s knowledge and can draw no

162. The Crown refers to correspondence between TCM and firms associated
with Sandals following the start of the Commission of Inquiry (COI). On
47

16 June 2010, M&S wrote to TCM stating that it was their “understanding”
that the payments made to Chalmers Misick & Co in March 2006 were
donations to the PNP. The letter requested information of receipt of the
funds, how they were disbursed providing details of dates, identity of the
payees, and the person who gave instructions for payment.
163. Chalmers & Co responded on 18th June 2010, confirming receipt of the
funds into their client account, but declining to give further information,
because they did not have their client’s instructions to do so. The writer,
TCM, also indicted that he spoke to Dr. Pyne on or about 20th May 2010,
(some weeks before the M&S letter) on the matter. Importantly, the writer
confirmed that the firm did not act for PNP “currently” or at the time of
receipt of the funds.
164. On 18th August 2010, Patterson Mair Hamilton (attorneys for
Sandals/Beaches in Jamaica) wrote to Chalmers & Co stating that his letter
of 18th June 2010 confirmed that the payment was not a political
