Chalmers & Co. In addition neither GAS, Chairman of the group, nor any
other senior officer had any knowledge, or authorised the transfer of the
funds. The writer further threatened that unless full and frank disclosure
was made within 14 days of the beneficiary of the payment and the person
within the Sandals/Beaches group, if any, who made the arrangements with
Chalmers & Co, the matter would be referred to “the appropriate
authorities”.
165. One month later, on 11th September 2010, TCM responded, stating that the
US$500,000 “received on behalf of our client” was a political contribution
made by Sandals/Beaches to assist the re-election campaign of the PNP.
The writer (TCM) further denied that GAS or any senior officers did not
have knowledge of or authorised the transfer of the funds to the firm “on
48

behalf of our client”, and were well aware of the political contributions,
when they were made and how they were made.
166. The Crown argues that if these payments were political donations, why
were they described as “GAS LEGAL” and “deposits”, and why were they
disbursed by TCM for the benefit of MM and LRM?
167. The Crown further referred to the evidence of RBK as to the procedure
normally followed where a developer makes a proposal for investment in
the TCI. Her evidence was that the proposal would be submitted by the
developer to TCInvest, a government agency set up to attract and facilitate
investment in TCI. The proposal is then circulated to all relevant
government departments for comment and due diligence. These
departments included the DECR, the Department for Economic Planning,
and Statistics, and the Planning Department. TCInvest would then collate
the information and finalize a draft Cabinet Paper for ExCo. If approved,
ExCo issues an Action Minute, which is sent to the relevant Permanent
Secretary. The action minute would form the basis for the drafting of a
Development Agreement by the AG Chambers.
168. In the case of the Beaches expansion, however, RBK received a draft DA
from M&S, attorneys acting for Sandals/Beaches. RBK testified that it was
unusual to have a DA drafted by attorneys for a developer. It appeared to
RBK that the deal had been agreed between MM and the developer without
going through the usual procedure through TCInvest.
169. Mr. Peterson SC, submitted on behalf of MM, that the Court should
balance RBK’s belief that other government departments were not
consulted in the light of Clayton Been’s evidence that government
departments often did not respond to requests for input, and the need to
accelerate development in the TCI through inward investment. In reply the
49

Crown suggested that given the inefficiency of the civil service, the land
registration system and the valuation department, there was an added
burden of responsibility placed on ministers as fiduciaries to scrutinize very
carefully the proposals that were put before ExCo.
170. In reference to MM’s letter from 19th July to Ariel Misick QC informing
him of the government’s decision to grant the concessions, Mr. Peterson SC
submitted that the proper inference to be drawn was that MM was doing no
more than showing the developer the support that TCIG was giving to the
project. The Crown observes that MM’s enthusiasm to convey the decision
promptly and personally to the developers is consistent with his personal
financial interest.
THE LAW OF BRIBERY
171. Russel on Crime (12th Ed. 1964) defines bribery at common law as the
receiving or offering of any undue reward to any person in a public office
in order to influence his behavior in office, and incline him to act contrary
to the known rules of honesty and integrity. It is a crime to bribe the office
holder. It is also a crime if the office holder accepts the bribe.
172. In Floyd Basil Hall and Clayton Stanfield Green v Rex (CR-AP 5/2023
and CR-AP 6/2023), a judgment delivered by the Court of Appeal of TCI
on 10th July 2025 the Court referred to the case of Field v R [2012] 2 LRC
1 in which William Young J stated at paragraph [18]:
[18] The word “bribe” customarily denotes a payment (or other
benefit) which is provided (or offered to influence the behavior of a
public official or agent in a way that is contrary to recognized rules of
probity. This sense of the word appears in the definition in Black’s Law
Dictionary (9th Ed. 2009):
50

“A price, reward, gift, or favour bestowed or promised with a
view to pervert the judgment of or influence the action of a
person in a position of trust.”
and the Shorter Oxford English Dictionary (6th Ed. 2007):
“A sum of money or another reward offered or demanded in
order to procure an (often illegal or dishonest) action or decision
in favour of the giver.”
173. In the agreed Legal Directions reference was made to Whitaker 10 Cr.
App R. in which the Court of Appeal of England and Wales held that where
a person in the position of a trustee to perform a public duty, takes a bribe
to act corruptly in discharging that duty, it is an offence by the payer of the
bribe and the official who accepts it. For the official to place himself in a
position where his duty and his personal interests are in conflict, so that he
is not in a position to act as a perfectly free man, was held to be a serious
offence.
174. The attorneys agree that the following propositions can be extracted from
Whitaker:
(a) It is an offence at common law for a public officer/minister to accept
an advantage as an inducement for him to show favour or forbear to
show favour to any person towards whom an impartial discharge of
the officer’s duty, demands that he should show no favour or
disfavour.
(b) A cloud of suspicion is raised by payment of large sums at dates
approximating the dates of various contracts.
(c) When an officer has to discharge a public duty in which the public
is interested, to bribe that officer to act contrary to his duty is a
criminal act. To induce him to show favour or abstain from showing
51

disfavour where an impartial discharge of his duty demands that he
should show no favour or that he should show disfavour, is to induce
him to act contrary to his duty; where this is done corruptly, it is an
indictable misdemeanour at common law.
Discussion
175. The Crown and the Defence have agreed the legal directions, and the
issues the Court must consider in arriving at a verdict on each count. With
respect to Count 1, the issues posed are framed in the form of 4 questions
which the court must answer.
Question One
176. Question One is framed as follows:
- Did Beaches, Gordon Stewart, Jeffery Pyne and/or related entities
directly or indirectly make payments to MM between the dates set out
in Count 1?
- If yes, then move to question 2.
- If no, then MM is not guilty on count 1.
177. The payments made by UVI on the instructions of Dr. Pyne, Treasurer of
Sandals, to PPL and Chalmers Misick & Co. are set out in paragraphs 111
and 113 ante. The payments made by UVI on the instructions of Dr. Pyne
to Misick & Stanbrook, which were paid to the PNP, are set out in
paragraphs 114 and 115. The fact of these payments was not challenged by
MM. Unsurprisingly, in his oral address Mr. Peterson SC acknowledged
that the fact that the payments were made, is not in issue. It follows that
question one must be answered in the affirmative, and so the Court moves
on to question two.
Question Two
52

178. Question Two is framed as follows:
- Was MM a Minister of the Crown when he directly or indirectly
received payments from Beaches, Gordon Stewart, Dr. Pyne and/or
related entities?
- If yes, then move to question 3.
- If no, then MM is not guilty on count 1.
179. As with the issue of the payment of the funds, the issue of the receipt of
the funds by MM during the time he was a Minister of the Crown, was not
challenged by MM. Once more, Mr. Peterson SC acknowledged in his oral
closing submissions that Question Two is not an issue in this trial. This is
hardly surprising, given the undisputed documentary evidence tracing the
movement of funds from source to ultimate destination. The fact of MM’s
position as Chief Minister and later as Premier during the relevant period
during which the funds were received is clearly a matter of record. The
answer to question two is in the affirmative, requiring the court to move to
the next question.
Question Three
180. Question Three is framed as follows:
- Were the payments made by Beaches, Gordon Stewart, Jeffery Pyne
and/or related entities intended to induce MM to an act in a way that
was corrupt and therefore contrary to the obligations of honesty and
integrity which he had as a Minister of the Crown?
- If yes, then move to question 4.
- If no, then MM is not guilty of count 1.
181. This issue relates to the intention of Beaches, Gordon Stewart and Dr.
Pyne. What was their intention in making the payments or giving directions
to have other related entities make the payments? Mr. Peterson SC makes
53

the point that there is no direct evidence from any witness as to the intention
of the persons/entities that made the payments.
182. The Crown relies on circumstantial evidence to prove that the intention of
the persons/entities who made the payments, was to induce MM to act in a
corrupt manner. Among the items of evidence that the Crown alleges
demonstrate that intention are:
(a) The passing of funds through different corporate entities;
(b) The deliberate misdescription of the reason for payments;
(c) The passing of the funds through Chalmers Misick & Co;
(d) The absence of any legal business between the attorneys and MM;
(e) The co-mingling of the funds with other funds in clients accounts;
(f) The absence of ledgers showing the receipt and disbursement of
clients’ funds;
(g) The rapid disbursement of payments upon receipt for the direct or
indirect benefit on MM;
(h) The coincidence of the payments with decisions made by MM that
directly or indirectly provided benefits to Beaches/Sandals and;
(i) The absence of any evidence that the payments represented political
donations or any other legitimate purpose.
183. The payments of $500,000, $350,000 and $150,000 via wire transfer by
UVI to PPL in March, July and December 2005, are not disputed. The
Defence submits that these payments were political donations to the PNP.
Assuming that they were, the question arises, why were they sent to PPL, a
private real estate company in which MM has an interest, instead of being
sent to PNP, as was done on 27th October 2006 and 18th January 2007. In
addition, why was the payment not made directly from Beaches/Sandals to
the PNP rather than passing the donations through UVI and PPL?
54

184. The description of the payments also gives cause for some concern. The
payment of the sum of $500,000 is described in the Sandals letter of
instruction to Hambros Bank in the Bahamas as a deposit on the purchase
of 3 acres of land in TCI. There is no evidence that MM or PPL were
involved in any transaction for the sale of land to Sandals.
185. Further the payments of $200,000 and two payments of $150,000 made in
March 2006 from UVI to PPL and described in the Daily Funds Distribution
an internal account of UVI is “GAS LEGAL”, suggesting the payment of
legal fees by Gordon Arthur Stewart to Chalmers Misick & Co. There is no
evidence in this case of any legal work being performed by Chalmers
Misick & Co for GAS. In fact, the evidence shows that Misick and
Stanbrook acted for Sandals in their legal transactions in TCI.
186. Curiously, the ledger sheet for Windsor West Ltd. (WWL) found on a
memory stick on TCM’s person upon his arrest, records the payments as
“Deposits”. There is no evidence that Chalmers and Co was handling any
transactions for the sale of land or anything else for Beaches/Sandals.
187. The position becomes even more curious, when the Court considers the
letter of 16th June 2010, in which Misick & Stanbrook wrote to Chalmers &
Co seeking confirmation that the firm received US$500,000 in donations in
March 2006 to the PNP from Sandals. TCM replied on 18th June 2010,
confirming receipt of “the funds”, but indicating that he did not have
instructions from his client to provide any information on how the funds
were disbursed, to whom, and who gave instructions for disbursement.
Interestingly, TCM further confirmed that the firm did not act for the PNP
at the time of the receipt of the funds nor at the time of writing.
188. On 8th August 2010, Patterson Mair Hamilton (PMH) a firm of attorneys
in Jamaica, acting for Sandals, noting that TCM’s letter of 18th June 2010
confirmed that the payments were not a “political contribution” and that
55

Sandals had no legal business with his firm, and “can find no record relating
to or justifying, the payment”. The letter further noted that neither GAS or
any senior officer of Sandals had any knowledge or authorised any of the
payments. The letter further demanded full disclosure of the identity of the
beneficiary of the payments, failing which the matter would be referred to
the “appropriate authorities”.
189. By letter dated 17th September 2010, TCM responded, denying that his
earlier letter stated that the payments were not a political contribution to
assist in the re-election campaign of the PNP, and insisting that the
payments were made with the knowledge and authorization of GAS and
senior officers of Sandals.
190. Interestingly, it must be noted that this correspondence took place two
years after the Commission of Inquiry was announced on 10th July 2008.
M&S and PMH, firms of attorneys representing the interests of Sandals,
appear to be engaged in an exercise designed to distance their client from
any allegation of impropriety.
191. The passing of the sum of $500,000.00 through Chalmers Misick & Co, in
the absence of any legal business between the firm and MM, and the rapid
disbursement of the funds upon receipt for the direct or indirect benefit of
MM clearly support the inference that the funds were paid to Chalmers
Misick & Co for the benefit of MM. It is interesting to note that in the
correspondence with Sandals’ attorneys, TCM makes reference to
instructions from his “client” but does not disclose the identity of his client.
However, he emphatically declares that at no time was he acting for the PNP
party.
192. Mr. Peterson SC submits that the payments to PPL and Chalmers Misick
were in fact political donations to the party to finance its re-election
56

campaign. The question arises, if the payments were indeed political
donations, why were they sent to PPL and Chalmers Misick & Co rather
than to the PNP bank accounts, as was done through M&S in October 2006
and January 2007?
193. The Crown further invites inferences to be drawn from the proximity in
time of the payments by UVI/Sandals to benefits and advantages to
Beaches/Sandals being granted by TCIG through MM. One such example
is the payment of $350,000 on 21st July 2005 by UVI to PPL. This followed
an oral mention by MM in ExCo on 7th July 2005, in which MM proposed
that two parcels (of 1.5 acres and 1.34 acres of Crown Land) valued at
$825,000 and $737,000, be sold to Beaches for $700,000, saving
Beaches/Sandals $862,000. Another example is the payment by UVI to
PPL of the sum of $150,000 on 21st December 2005. This payment
followed a meeting on 20th December between MM and Dr. Pyne, in which
MM agreed to a reduction in stamp duty on lands to be purchased by
Beaches/Sandals. The meeting is referenced in a letter dated 22nd December
2005 by Ariel Misick QC to MM. The saving in stamp duty was $1.497m.
194. In assessing the circumstantial evidence adduced by the Crown to prove
the intention of the developer, the Court keeps at the forefront of its mind
the fundamental principle that the burden lies on the prosecution to prove
the guilt of MM beyond reasonable doubt, or to the extent that the tribunal
of fact feels sure of the guilt of MM. In this case the burden of proof remains
on the Crown throughout. The accused is not required to prove any issue in
this case, and importantly, there is no burden on him to prove his innocence.
195. The Court is also mindful of the dictum in R v Teper [1952] UKPC 15
per Lord Normand that circumstantial evidence must be “narrowly
examined” and that before drawing an inference of guilt, the court must be
sure that there are no other co-existing circumstances which might weaken
57

or destroy the inference of guilt. Any doubt as to the effect of co-existing
circumstances must be resolved in favour of the accused.
196. The approach that the court should take in drawing inferences of guilt was
articulated by Lord Diplock in the Privy Council decision in Kwan Ping
Bong v R [1979] AC 433 at 438A which bears repetition:
“The requirement of proof beyond all reasonable doubt does not
prevent a jury from inferring, from the facts that have been the
subject of direct evidence before them, the existence of some
further fact, such as the knowledge or intent of the accused,
which constitutes an essential element of the offence; but the
inference must be compelling – one (and the only one), that no
reasonable man could fail to draw from the direct facts proved”.
197. Mr. Peterson SC succinctly emphasized the principle in his written closing
submissions at paragraph 2:
“2. In a case based largely or exclusively on the proper inferences
to be drawn from the proven facts, the court must be satisfied
so that it is sure that the inference of guilt contended for by the
prosecution is the one, only and inescapable inference to be
drawn and that there are no others, consistent with innocence
or inconsistent with guilt, open to a reasonable TOF (Tribunal
of Fact) to draw, apply the legal directions to the facts as he finds
them to be. Any doubt as to the effect of an inference or any
doubt as to the effect of co-existing circumstances must be
resolved in favour of the defendant.”
198. In the light of these principles, the Court must consider what are the
reasonable and inescapable inferences to be drawn from the items of
58

evidence outlined at paragraph 182 ante, notably the misdescription of the
payments in the internal accounts of UVI, in the letter of instruction signed
by Steven Carey and in what purports to be an escrow account for Windsor
West Ltd (WWL). The Court must ask itself, if these were indeed political
contributions, why were they not described as such, and why were they not
sent to the PNP instead of being directed to PPL and to Chalmers Misick &
Co, who expressly declared that they never acted for the PNP? The Court
also considers the coincidence of some of the payments with decisions
which clearly benefited the developer.
199. Considering these factors as well as all the other evidence in the case, the
Court makes what it considers to be the inescapable and only reasonable
inference that the payments through UVI/Sandals to PPL and Chalmers
Misick & Co, were intended to induce MM to act in a way that was corrupt.
The Court makes that finding of fact beyond reasonable doubt on this issue.
200. The position is somewhat different with respect to the payments made by
SRI through Misick & Stanbrook (M&S) to the PNP on 27th October 2006,
and 18th January 2007. These payments were made by cheque payable to
the PNP, and were deposited into the PNP accounts. The sum of $150,000
was paid in October 2006, and the sum of $350,000 in January 2007.
201. These payments were passed through M&S, who acted in TCI for
Beaches/Sandals, and were not misdescribed or made through corporate
entities, or other attorneys connected to MM. The payments do not appear
to be disguised as anything other than political contributions to the PNP,
and were deposited into the PNP’s bank accounts.
202. Mr. Peterson SC argues that there was no legislation in the TCI before
2009, which controlled political funding, donations and how they were
expended. He further submitted that the Crown has called no witness from
59

the PNP to complain of the misuse or misappropriation of political
donations by MM, or that he was not entitled to draw on these funds. He
suggests that there was an inference “plainly open” to the Court that “there
was or may have been an implied understanding within the party at this time
that MM could draw on these funds at will, as and when a need arose, and
that there was no co-mingling of funds”. Mr. Peterson SC did not indicate
the evidence on which the Court might base such an inference.
203. It is undoubtedly true that there was no legislation in the TCI at the time
governing political donations, the quantum of such donations and the way
they were spent by a political party. It does not follow from this that funds
donated to a political party (especially one in power), may not be intended
by a developer or a person or entity who wishes to secure an advantage from
a Minister or other public officer as an inducement or reward for the
granting of such advantage or benefit. In his oral address, Mr. Peterson SC,
with characteristic candor, conceded that there may be an element of self-
interest that motivates the donor of such contributions. Of course, the
inference to be drawn from a political donation made by a person or entity
who is engaged in negotiations with government officials or Ministers for
commercial developments, is likely to be different where a donation given
is in the absence of any existing or prospective commercial business
between the donor and the Government.
204. In this case, the donations to the PNP were made in October 2006, and
January 2007. Beaches/Sandals had been engaged in negotiations for the
purchase of Crown lands, since at least April 2005, and had been granted
significant reductions in purchase prices and stamp duty, and generous
concessions in customs duties and taxes. To facilitate the Beaches
expansion, the TCIG acted expeditiously to relocate a cultural market that
was to be located on the land required by Beaches, and to terminate a lease
60

to Provo Water, which involved the relocation of existing water
infrastructure.
205. The DA for the Beaches expansion project was eventually signed off by
the Governor on 11th October 2006. The first donation of $150,000 was
made on 27th October 2006, followed by the larger one of $350,000 in
January 2007, shortly before the general elections in February 2007. It
clearly was in Beaches/Sandals interest to have the PNP re-elected.
206. The sum of $25,000 was paid out of the PNP BB account to Yolanda Scott,
the hairstylist of LRM. The instruction for the wire transfer was signed by
MM, a signatory to the account. The transfer was made the day after the
cheque drawn on M&S for $150,000 was paid into the PNP account.
207. The indictment against MM on Count 1 charges that MM “accepted
inducements directly or indirectly on behalf of the Beaches Group
(including Gordon Arthur Stewart) by unlawful corrupt payments or other
rewards.” There can be little doubt that MM indirectly benefited from the
Beaches/Sandals donations made to his party. Though not paid directly to
MM, it is clear that the payments resulted in an advantage to him,
strengthening the prospects of the PNP in the elections, and ensuring a
second term for MM as Premier. It may be inferred as well from the
payment to Yolanda Scott (and possibly the payment of $60,000 to Sky
King which provided airline services) that MM was in a position to use
funds in the PNP bank accounts for his own benefit and that of his wife
LRM.
208. From the evidence discussed in the forgoing paragraphs, this Court finds
that there is abundant evidence from which it can reasonably be inferred
that the payments made by Beaches, Gordon Arthur Stewart, Jeffery Pyne
61

and/or related entities were intended to induce MM to act corruptly. This
inference is cogent and compelling.
Question 4
209. The final question is: “Were the payments knowingly and intentionally
received by MM as an inducement for him to act corruptly and therefore
contrary to the obligations of honesty and integrity which he had as a
Minister of the Crown?”
210. This question addresses the mens rea, or state of mind of MM when he
received the payments. Did he know that the payments were intended by
Beaches/Sandals to induce him to act corruptly and did he intentionally
receive the payments knowing the intention of the sender?
211. There is no direct evidence from any witness with respect to the knowledge
or intention of MM. The Crown relies on inferences that may be reasonably
drawn from undisputed evidence of the actions and decisions of MM and
decisions of ExCo, which were actively pursued by MM and the TCIG, led
at the time by MM.
212. The evidence reveals that Beaches/Sandals received generous concessions
and significant reductions in the purchase price of Crown Lands and stamp
duty. There was, as well, urgent administrative action to relocate the
proposed cultural market and to pursue the surrender of the Provo lease and
the resultant relocation of water installations on lands to be acquired by
Beaches.
213. Mr. Peterson SC has argued quite forcefully that the Beaches expansion
project was important to the TCI economy and would result in a significant
injection of capital investment in the economy, and the creation of jobs for
locals. He further argued that MM and the ministers dealt directly with the
62

developers in order to accelerate the project in view of the undoubted
benefit to the TCI economy.
214. It appears to be undisputed that the civil service in TCI was regarded as
lacking in efficiency, and there were delays apparently due to a lack of
coordination and swift action by various government departments involved
in assessing and moving forward proposed investment projects by
developers. It was for this reason, it is argued, that MM and his ministers
took an active part in negotiations with developers, instead of leaving these
matters to TCInvest and other departments.
215. Mr. Peterson SC further submits that the concessions granted to Beaches
were not unusual, and were within the statutory limits imposed by the
Encouragement of Development Ordinance. He submits that RT’s assertion
that the concessions granted were “overly generous”, was merely his
opinion, which was not based on research or empirical evidence. Further
RT could have delayed the decision pending a cost/benefit analysis, which
he failed to do.
216. There is of course the alacrity with which MM acted after correspondence
with M&S, a meeting with Dr. Pyne, and after decisions were taken by
ExCo, to communicate such decisions. Examples of these actions are to be
found in the request for reduction in stamp duty and for concessions, the
granting of same by ExCo, and the communication of the decisions by MM
to Ariel Misick QC.
217. In particular, with reference to MM’s letter dated 19th July 2006 to Ariel
Misick QC informing him of ExCo’s decision to grant the concessions,
substantially as requested, and the grant of belongership status to GAS and
Dr. Pyne, Mr. Peterson SC submits the proper inference to be drawn was
63

that MM was doing no more than “showing the developer that Beaches’
expansion plans had the full support of government.”
218. Another issue that bears on the knowledge and intent of MM in receiving
the payments is his failure to declare the receipt of the payments to the
Registrar of Interests pursuant to the House of Assembly (Registration of
Interest) Ordinance 1993. Mr. Peterson SC submits that there was “a long
standing and deep rooted culture of complacency, non-compliance and non-
disclosure in relation to the completion of the annual registers and that
nothing more sinister should be read into “MM’s failure to disclose receipt
of the payments from Beaches/Sandals.
219. Further undisputed evidence that bears on the issue of the knowledge and
intent of MM, is the rapid disbursement of the funds received into the
accounts of PPL and the client account of Chalmers Misick & Co for the
direct or indirect benefit of MM. The charts that are admitted into evidence
attest to the urgency with which funds received into the PPL account were
disbursed by MM, and the funds ($500,000) received by Chalmers Misick
& Co, were disbursed in the same month they were received all for the direct
or indirect benefit of MM.
220. The Court now considers the reasonable inferences to be drawn from the
evidence outlined above with respect to:
- The concessions, reduction in price and stamp duty on the purchase of
Crown Lands;
- The alacrity and enthusiasm with which MM pursued the interests of the
developer;
- The failure of MM to disclose the payments;
- The speed with which the payments were disbursed, and
- The evidence that the disbursements were made for the direct or indirect
benefit of MM.
64

221. The concessions, reduction in price and stamp duty, and the urgency with
which the TCIG moved to relocate the cultural market and to cancel the
Provo lease, all evince a clear intention to benefit the developer. The speed
at which MM moved to provide significant benefits to Beaches/Sandals,
together with the receipt of payments, sometimes in close proximity in time
to favourable decisions, or actions taken, lead to the inexorable inference
that his decisions and actions were taken to provide significant benefits to
Beaches/Sandals in exchange for financial rewards from them. This
inference is supported by the evidence of a meeting between MM and Dr.
Pyne on or about 20th December 2005 at which a reduction in stamp duty of
$1.497m was agreed, and correspondence between MM and his brother
Ariel Misick QC, who was acting for Beaches/Sandals.
222. Further, MM’s failure to disclose the receipt of the payments from
Beaches/Sandals in his declaration to the Registrar, supports the reasonable
inference that MM knew that the payments were corrupt.
223. The passing of payments through UVI to PPL rather than to MM directly
further supports the inference that there was a deliberate intention to
disguise the payments. The rapid disbursement of the funds once received
also supports the inference that MM must have known of the receipt of the
funds into the PPL account, and set about disposing of the funds quickly to
avoid detection. The same reasonable inference may be drawn from the
passing of funds by UVI through the client account of Chalmers Misick &
Company, and their rapid disbursement for the direct or indirect benefit of
MM by his brother TCM. The reasonable and compelling inference that
must be drawn from the evidence is that there was a deliberate strategy to
disguise the funds and to channel them into accounts not directly traceable
to MM. The disbursement of the funds for the benefit of MM clearly
supports the reasonable inference that the funds paid through PPL and
65

Chalmers Misick & Co client accounts were intended to be applied for the
benefit of MM. A clear inference may be readily drawn that TCM must
have received instructions from MM to disburse the funds to pay debts and
expenses incurred by MM and his wife LRM. The further compelling
inference was that MM must have known of the receipt of the funds into
TCM’s client account and gave instructions to TCM to make the
disbursements. How else would TCM know the amounts to be paid, for
example, to MM’s wedding caterers and to Brilliantmont International
School for LRM’s daughter’s school fees?
224. The size of the payments, $500,000, $350,000 and $150,000 through PPL
and $200,000, $150,000 and $150,000 through Chalmers & Co, and their
rapid disbursement upon receipt clearly support the reasonable inference
that the payments were corrupt and were received by MM as such. If these
payments were not bribes made to MM to influence him to make decisions
favourable to Beaches/Sandals, then what were they?
225. Mr. Peterson SC has suggested in his cross-examination of Lourdes
Blanco that the payments made to PPL may have been campaign financing
payments made on the instructions of Dr. Pyne. He further pointed out that
the payments to Chalmers & Co were made in the months preceding the
election in February 2007. Mr. Peterson SC referred to the letter of 17th
September 2010 from TCM to Patterson Mair Hamilton (PMH) in which
TCM stated that the US$500,000 received by his firm was a political
contribution to assist in the re-election campaign of the PNP.
226. The thrust of Mr. Peterson’s submissions is that the payments made by
UVI through PPL and Chalmers & Co were or may have been political
donations. He relies on the correspondence between Sandals’ attorneys and
Chalmers & Co in 2010 as evidence that the monies received into their client
account as evidence that they were political donations. The obvious
66

difficulty, though, is that, in his letter of 18th June 2010, while TCM
acknowledges receipt of the funds, he does not refer to them as political
donations, and he does not reveal the identity of his client. He does,
however, confirm that he did not act for the PNP, at the time of receipt of
the funds, or at the time of writing.
227. The obvious question that arises, is why was TCM receiving political
donations from Sandals, if he was not acting as attorney for Sandals or the
PNP? A further question arises from the disbursements made by TCM for
the personal benefit of MM, which were clearly unrelated to the PNP. Mr.
Peterson SC seeks to find a plaster for this particular sore. He asks the Court
to draw an inference that there was or may have been an implied
understanding within the PNP that MM could draw on political donations
at will. Mr. Peterson SC does not identify the evidence upon which this
inference may be drawn, except perhaps the absence of any complaint from
the PNP, that the funds were misappropriated by MM. This submission
begs the question that the funds were political donations in the first place.
228. Further, the undisputed fact that funds were paid by Misick and Stanbrook
acting for Beaches/Sandals directly to the PNP bank accounts, adds support
to the inference that the UVI payments to PPL and Misick & Co, were never
intended to be political donations, but were intended to benefit MM
personally for benefits granted to Beach/Sandals.
229. As found earlier in this judgement, the clear inference is that the political
donations made through M&S were intended by Beaches/Sandals to reward
the PNP, and indirectly MM, for favours granted in relation to the expansion
project, and to assist in the re-election of the PNP, from whom they expected
to continue receiving preferential treatment. In addition, the evidence
shows that $25,000.00 of these funds were paid to Yolanda Scott, LRM’s
67

hairstylist on the signed instructions of MM. The sum of $60,000 was also
paid to TCI Sky King.
230. From the totality of the evidence this Court finds that the reasonable
inferences to be drawn from the evidence leave the court in no doubt that
MM knowingly and intentionally received the payments as an inducement
for him to act corruptly and therefore contrary to his obligations of honesty
and integrity which he owed to the Crown.
231. Mr. Peterson SC submits that the Rules of Conduct for Ministers and other
Members of Executive Council (gazetted on 28th August 1998), which sets
out standards of behavior and integrity expected of ministers, does not have
the force of law, as it is not enshrined in any statute. While this so, it is well
settled at common law that it is an offence for a public officer (including a
Minister) to accept an advantage as an inducement to show favour or
disfavour or forbear to show favour to any person towards whom an
impartial discharge of the officer’s duty, demands that he should show no
favour or disfavour: See Whitaker (ante).
Conclusion on Count 1
232. In coming to a determination on Count 1, this Court is acutely aware of
the important principle that before drawing an inference of guilt from
circumstantial evidence, the tribunal of fact must be sure that there are no
other co-existing circumstances which might weaken or destroy that
inference: R v Teper [1952] UKPC 15. Pitchford L.J. in R v Massih [2015]
EWCA Crim. 477 succinctly expressed the same principle in this way by
asking the question:
“could a reasonable jury, properly directed, exclude all realistic
possibilities consistent with the defendant’s innocence?”
68

233. The Court notes the guidance given by Lord Diplock in the Privy Council
appeal from Hong Kong in Kwan Ping Bong v R [1979] 2 WLR 433 at
438A:
“…The requirement of proof beyond reasonable doubt does not
prevent a jury from inferring, from facts that have been the
subject of direct evidence before them, the existence of some
further fact, such as the knowledge or intent of the accused,
which constitutes an essential element of the offence; but the
inference must be compelling – one (and the only one) that no
reasonable man could fail to draw from the direct facts proved”
234. As the Court has stated earlier in this judgement, there is no issue as to the
fact of the payments, the receipt of the payments and their disbursement.
The issues set out in Questions 3 and 4 of the agreed route to verdict have
to do with the intention of Beaches/Sandals in making the payments, and
the knowledge and intention of MM in receiving them. This Court has
answered these questions in the affirmative, after a careful (and perhaps
repetitive) analysis of the evidence.
235. Having carefully considered the evidence and applied the law to the
undisputed facts, the Court finds the inference of guilt to be compelling.
The Court can find no co-existing circumstances or inferences which might
weaken or destroy the inference of guilt. The inference of guilt in the
Court’s judgment is the only reasonable inference that can be drawn from
the evidence.
236. Accordingly, this Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that MM is guilty
on Count 1. In coming to this verdict, the court expressly takes into account
the fact that a man of good character, such as MM, is less likely to commit
the offence of bribery than would otherwise be the case.
COUNT 2
69

237. Count 2 involves MM and McH. They are both charged with bribery in
connection with a resort complex on the island of Salt Cay. It is alleged that
between 1st January 2005 and 30th April 2009, they accepted inducements
directly or indirectly from Mario Hoffman (MHo) and/or related or
connected entities by unlawful corrupt payments or other rewards (in the
form of cash, credit, entertainment and other advantages), while serving as
Ministers in the Government of TCI, so that they would act in a way that
was contrary to the ordinary rules of honesty and integrity expected of
Ministers of the Crown.
Undisputed Facts
238. Salt Cay is a small underpopulated and underdeveloped island to the
Southeast of Providenciales off the coast of Grand Turk.
239. Mario Hoffman (MHo) was a Slovakian national who owned a villa in
Chalk Sound, Providenciales called Terrapin Villa. MHo was interested in
investing in a resort on Salt Cay. To this end, in February 2003, he acquired
the issued shares in Caribbean Island Investment Company (CIIC), which
owned two parcels of land and an option to purchase a third parcel, through
one of his companies, Salt Cay Estates Ltd (SCEL).
240. Simon Wood a local architect, who had designed an expansion of Terrapin
Villa, introduced Kenneth Christensen, who controlled CIIC to MHo.
Christensen had hired Wood for a project called Castaways. As the project
grew, Wood introduced Christensen to MHo as a potential investor. After
acquiring the land owned by CIIC, MHo enlisted Wood to build two
additional cottages on the land.
241. MHo’s project expanded into a larger luxury resort to contain 40 cottages
and 2 villas on 11 acres on Salt Cay. Wood suggested the acquisition of
additional land for the expansion. The only land available was Crown Land.
70

Wood understood that it was beneficial to have a Belonger to acquire a
Conditional Purchase Lease (CPL) on Crown Land. He introduced MHo to
Benjamin Walkin, a Belonger, who was a building contractor. It was agreed
that Wood and Walkin would enter into a joint venture with MHo and CIIC
(through SCEL) to carry out the project. The arrangement was formalised
in a “Heads of Agreement” dated 14th March 2005. It was agreed that the
Crown Land would be acquired by a separate company to be formed by
Wood and Walkin, which was registered on 20th October 2005 as Salt Cay
Development Ltd (SCDL). CIIC was to finance the construction of the
resort.
242. By letter dated 5th May 2005, Wood and Walkin wrote to MM setting out
their proposals for the project and introducing MHo as the investor. The
letter proposed the purchase of at least 26 acres of Crown Land, and the
creation of a land buffer, possibly a nature reserve or a golf course.
243. On 19th August 2005, Wood introduced MHo to MM at MM’s residence
on Providenciales. On the same day, Wood and Walkin wrote to MM,
thanking him for the meeting and repeating their proposals for the project
as set out in their letter of 5th May 2005.
244. Sometime later, there was a meeting with MM at his office attended by
Walkin, Wood, MHo and members of the Cabinet. The purpose of the
meeting was to present the project to the TCIG. Wood did the presentation.
245. In October 2005, MM and two ministers travelled to Solvakia to meet
MHo. They arrived in Prague on MHo’s private jet, and later attended a
football match with MHo, stayed in a hotel, and were provided with the use
of two cars, all at the expense of MHo.
71

246. On 30th November 2005 at an ExCo meeting, MM introduced a paper on
which ExCo advised the Governor, who concurred that, inter alia:
- ExCo approved conceptually the proposal to establish a resort
community on Salt Cay;
- ExCo declared the Salt Cay Resort and Spa Development and CIIC as
an approved enterprise under the Encouragement of Development
Ordinance 1972;
- Freehold interest of 11.5 acres at open market rate of Crown Land be
granted to facilitate the development of the hotel and spa;
- A conditional purchase lease (CPL) be granted for approximately 30
acres of Crown Land to support the residential/villa development
component of the project;
- Import duties on construction materials be reduced by 5%;
- All plant and machinery to be used to implement the project be
exempted unless re-exported;
- Before the signing of DAs or instruments of transfer the developers
must satisfy the Department of Planning, and the DECR regarding any
issues raised;
- TCIG is satisfied with the bona fides of the developers, and their
financial ability to implement the project, and that TCIG is satisfied that
the Crown Land in question is free and unencumbered, and
- The property is surveyed at the developer’s expense.
247. ExCo further instructed TCInvest with the support of AG’s Chambers to
negotiate separate DAs with CIIC in relation to the Hotel and Spa
Development and jointly with CIIC and SCDL in relation to the residential
component.
248. On 1st December 2005, MM wrote to MHo advising him of ExCo’s
decision.
72

249. On 8th December 2005, MM applied for an American Express Centurion
Card through J&T Banka. Patrik Tkac and Joseph Spiziak, Directors of
J&T supported the application and undertook to underwrite an
unconditional guarantee of $75,000.00(US) for any default in payments.
250. J&T Banka was a European bank providing investment, corporate and
retail services in Slovakia and the Czech Republic. Patrik Tkac was one of
the two founders. J&T Banka was to be the principal lender to the proposed
Salt Cay Development, granting a loan of $36m to SCEL on 10th January
2007.
251. MHo was the chairman of the Board of Directors of Istrokapital which was
owned by MHo. J&T Banka was one of three bankers for Istrokapital.
252. On 13th December 2005, a cheque was drawn on Miller Simons
O’Sullivan’s (MSO) client account for the sum of $50,000 to the PNP.
MSO was acting for MHo in connection with the project. The MSO ledger
for “The Salt Cay Project” has an entry dated “Dec 13/05 Contribution to
Christmas Party - $50,000.00”. By letter dated 14th December 2005 to the
Belize Bank, MSO requested that they reference the deposit on behalf of
MHo as sponsorship of the Chief Minister’s Christmas Party.
253. On 11th January 2006, MM introduced a paper in ExCo, which agreed to
grant a freehold interest to CIIC of 13.9 acres of Crown Land at open market
value for the development of a hotel, spa and sport facilities. ExCo also
approved a Conditional Purchase Lease (CPL) of approximately 30 acres of
Crown Land to Salt Cay Resort and Spa Development for the
residential/villa component of the project.
73

254. On 17th January 2006, Business Ventures Ltd (BVL) was incorporated.
Chalmers Misick & Co was the agent at the time of registration, replaced
by Chalmers Management Ltd. No officers were appointed at the time of
registration. On 18th November 2006, TCM and Chalmers Management Ltd
were appointed as Directors. TCM ceased to be a director on the same day.
Windsor East Ltd. was appointed a director on 20th February 2008.
Windsor East Ltd and TCM were appointed Secretary on 18th November
2006. TCM ceased to act on 20th February 2008, from which date Windsor
East Ltd was the sole secretary. TCM was the sole shareholder from 18th
November 2006 to 20th February 2008, replaced by Sun Beach Ventures Ltd
on that date. BVL was struck from the register on 17th June 2010.
255. On 27th January 2006, Arling Astalt was registered in Liechtenstein by
Markus Hasler. Its beneficial owner at the time of registration was MHo.
On 2nd March 2006, MHo signed a declaration that Brano Prielozny became
the beneficial owner from 15th February 2006. Prielozny was a friend and
business associate of MHo. He would later acquire ownership of one share
in Regent Street Holdings Ltd, a company registered in TCI on 16th January
2007. Prielozny acquired the share from Terrapin Investments Ltd on 6th
June 2008. Arling Astalt (AA) held accounts at J&T Banka.
256. On 19th May 2006 MHo wrote to Walkin informing him that the cost of
the project had escalated, and he (Walkin) would have to invest an
additional $17m to remain as a partner in the project. Wood received a
similar letter.
257. On 1st August 2006, MHo wrote to MM asking for a 99 year lease of 222
acres of Crown Land for the purpose of building a golf course. MHo
indicated that a new company, Salt Cay Golf Club Ltd will be incorporated
to build and operate the golf course.
74

258. Salt Cay Golf Club Ltd (SCGL) was registered on 15th August 2006 by
Chalmers Misick & Co. The Directors were Windsor East Ltd and Stephan
Kral from 15th December 2007. On 14th May 2010, Windsor East Ltd
ceased to be a Director and Secretary. Stephan Kral became sole Secretary
on that day. The shareholders were Business Ventures Ltd (from 15.12.07
to 14.05.10), Harbor Management Ltd (from 15.12.07) and SCEL (from
14.05.10). Windsor East Ltd held the only subscribed share.
259. Harbor Management Development Limited (HMDL) was registered in
Cyprus on 28th August 2006. MHo was its sole shareholder from 29th
August 2006. HMDL held an account at J&T Banka.
260. On 1st August 2006 an account at Belize Bank (TCI) was opened in the
name of My Way Productions 2 Ltd. (MWPL2), which was registered on
12th July 2006. The agent is listed as Chalmers Management Ltd, which
was also a director along with Windsor East Ltd, which is also listed as
Secretary. The sole shareholder was Windsor East Ltd. The authorised
signatories on the bank account were MM, LRM, and TCM.
261. On 2nd August 2006, MM introduced in ExCo by oral mention a proposal
for a 99 year lease to SCGL of 222 acres of Crown Land for the purpose of
an 18 hole golf course. The proposal was approved by the ExCo.
262. On 8th August 2006, MM wrote to MHo informing him of ExCo’s
decision.
263. On 9th August 2006, MHo instructed MSO to pay $100,000 to the PNP.
The MSO Terrapin Investments ledger recorded the payment as
“Progressive National Party”. The cheque was deposited into the PNP BB
account on 18th August 2006.
75

264. On 22nd August 2006, MHo wrote to Walkin referring to his letter of 19th
May 2006, and indicating that since he had received no reply, he no longer
wished to pursue the joint venture.

proceed with the transfer while the independent report, to be prepared by
Deloitte & Touche was pending. On the same day, the Governor instructed
Hoza to value the 122 acres as at September/October 2007.
353. Mr. Hoza submitted his report on 4th June 2008, assessing the value of the
land at $12,932,000. The Governor raised the matter with MM, who
expressed the view that the valuation was inflated, and insistence on its use
would “kill the deal”. The Governor recalled that the discussion was
“heated and difficult”.
354. On 9th June 2008, $10,000 was transferred from BVL’s BB account to
Chalmers & Co BB account.
355. On 10th June 2008, Stefan Kral as CEO of Devco, wrote to the Governor
and MM offering an additional $1m for the purchase of the 122 acres “as a
gesture of goodwill”.
94

356. On 11th June 2008, Cabinet approved the final DA. On the same day
$799,995 was transferred from the J&T Banka Istrokapital account to MSO
Terrapin Investments Client Ledger. On 17th June 2008, $274,621.81 was
transferred from MSO Terrapin client account to J&T Banka to repay J&T
Banka for previous payments made by them to MM’s Amex Centurion card.
357. On 16th June 2008, MM’s $1m loan from Arling Anstalt (AA) was
increased to $2m.
358. On 28th June 2008, the Governor received the Deloitte report. The report
did not find fault on the part of the developer, but noted the use of out-of-
date valuations. Following the receipt of the report, the Governor indicated
to the FCO that he was prepared to accept the findings of the report.
359. On 3rd July 2008, the Governor had a meeting with MM, before the
Cabinet meeting, at which he agreed to sign the transfer. At the Cabinet
meeting the same day, Cabinet gave approval for the Governor to execute
the transfer at the new agreed price of $7,102,000.
360. On 9th July 2008, Stefan Kral wrote to the Governor and MM, withdrawing
the offer to pay the additional $1m.
361. On 10th July 2008, on an Oral Mention raised by Deputy Chief Minister
Floyd Hall, Cabinet recommended that the developers of Salt Cay Resort
and Golf Development withdraw their letter of 9th July 2008.
362. On 10th July 2008, the Governor announced a Commission of Inquiry
(COI) to investigate the activities of the current and previous Government.
363. On 14th July 2008, the amended and final DA was signed by the Governor.
95

364. On 24th July 2008, Devco applied for permission to create a new barge
landing dock on Salt Cay.
365. On 30th September 2008, $302,835.84 was transferred from AA to McH’s
J&T Banka account. The same day $230,337.71 was transferred from AA
to MM’s J&T Banka account.
366. Between April 2007 and September 2008, the expenditure on MCH’s and
his wife’s Amex Centurion Cards was $1,155,120.21.
367. On 7th November 2008, the FSC Licensing Committee granted an
application by J&T Banka for a banking licence in the name of Integris
Bank and Trust (TCI) Ltd.
368. On 18th November 2008, Stefan Kral wrote to MM and McH stating that
the estimated cost of constructing a port had risen to $10m instead of the
$3m originally proposed, due to changes made by the DECR and EMS. He
proposed that the additional $7m be offset against the purchase of additional
land.
369. On 17th December 2008, Clyde Robinson, Director of Planning
recommended that the Physical Planning Board (PPB) reject the application
for a port barge. The Board agreed and the application was rejected.
370. At the meeting of the PPB, the Chairman expressed “concern with the
arrogance in wanting to place an industrial dock in the middle of town, for
eventual destruction Salt Cay’s heritage”. The outcome of the meeting was
conveyed to McH the same day.
371. On 9th January 2009, Clyde Robinson attended a meeting at MM’s office
at which Stefan Kral and McH were present. MM informed the meeting
96

that the PPB could not refuse a Government application. Robinson
disagreed. There was a confrontation between Robinson and McH, who
called Robinson a “prick”. It was suggested that the Board reconsider its
decision.
372. The Board held an extraordinary meeting and reversed its decision the
same evening.
373. On 15th January 2009, HMDL transferred $500,000 from J&T Banka to
Chalmers Misick & Co FCIB account, referenced “for credit to Business
Ventures Ltd, Purchase Price SCGC/Purchase Price for share in SCGC”.
This payment was disbursed between 15th January 2009 to 2nd February
2009, in 22 transactions.
374. J&T Banka made the following payments to pay monthly balances on
MM’s Amex Centurion Card:
- $63,895.42 on 31st December 2008,
- $211,800.82 on 2nd February 2009,
- $91,787.20 on 2nd March 2009,
- $87,771.13 on 31st March 2009, and
- $371.70 on 30th April 2009.
375. On 1st June 2009, J&T Banka registered a collateral charge in the sum of
$36,006,077.35 on the SCEL land.
376. The $6m loan to MM and LRM from J&T matured on 29th April 2008.
The bank took no steps to enforce its security until 29th December 2009,
when it entered into an agreement with Pro Partners Debt Collections Ltd
in respect of two outstanding debts described as:
- “Receivable 1” – the bank assigned the $6m loan contract debt plus
$1,807,835.80 in interest owed, and
97

- “Receivable 2” – the bank assigned $415,064.64 of the Amex
Centurion Card debt plus $26,496.75 interest owed.
377. Between 2006 to 2009, the expenditure on MM’s and LRM’s Amex cards
was $4.881m.
THE CASE AGAINST MICHAEL MISICK ON COUNT 2
378. The case for the Crown is that MM accepted the sum of $13,281,241.00 in
corrupt payments and benefits between 1st January 2005 and 30th April 2009
directly or indirectly from MHo and/or related entities as inducements to
act contrary to the ordinary rules of honesty and integrity expected of
Ministers of the Crown.
379. The alleged corrupt payments and other benefits are set out in the
undisputed facts set out at paragraphs 238 to 377 above.
380. MM, as he is entitled to do, elected not to give evidence or to call witnesses
in his defence. On his behalf Mr. Peterson SC has made submissions on the
evidence, which will now be examined.
SUBMISSIONS OF MICHAEL MISICK
381. In general terms, Mr. Peterson SC submits that the Crown has failed to
prove the essential elements of the offence, and that the Court as the finder
of fact, will not be able to eliminate or reject out of hand other possible
inferences which are “reasonably and credibly inconsistent with the alleged
guilt”. Accordingly, the Court will not be satisfied to the extent that it is
sure of MM’s guilt.
382. In summary, Senior Counsel poses the question: whether a fair-minded
tribunal of fact, directing himself properly on the essential elements of
98

bribery charged on Count 2, could infer to the exclusion of all other
reasonable inferences open to him which are inconsistent with the Crown’s
case, that any of the payments made in relation to Salt Cay, were bribes paid
by the developer, and knowingly and intentionally received as such by MM.
383. Mr. Peterson SC observed that Salt Cay was a small sparsely populated
outlying island which had never seen any substantial inward investment.
The development involved an investment of some $110m(US), as set out in
clause 3.1.6 of the DA dated 28th December 2006. The infrastructure work
was stated to be not less than $15m. Phase 2 of the development was to be
not less than $75m, and Phase 3 was to cost not less than $20m.
384. Senior Counsel referred to the cross-examination of the former Governor
RT, who agreed that it would take “some guts” to make such a substantial
investment in Salt Cay, an expression which was used by MM in a Cabinet
meeting.
385. Mr. Peterson SC referred to the meetings between MM and the TCIG and
the developer, which he submitted “shows the normality and good sense of
a developer choosing to liaise directly with the Minister and the
Government in order to ensure that his proposed development project would
enjoy the necessary levels of support and backing of the government”.
386. Referring to the amendment to the proposed draft DA which stipulated that
there would be no requirement to repay the 50% Belonger discount in the
event that the developer disposed of the land after 5 years, Senior Counsel
submitted that the CLP was a statement of policy which did not have the
force of law. It was not written in stone, and was subject to political
decisions to achieve an outcome which was in the best interests of the TCI.
99

387. With respect to MHo’s application for Belonger status, which stated that
MM and McH had known MHo for three years, Senior Counsel submitted
that the Crown had not proved beyond reasonable doubt that MM ever knew
that he was a referee, or that the Immigration Department had ever made
any enquiry of MM that he was a referee, or that MM in fact provided a
reference.
388. With respect to the 99 year lease of the 238 acres for the golf course at a
yearly rental of $1 an acre, Mr. Peterson SC submitted that the valuation of
$7.76m, was not relevant since the land was being leased, not sold to the
developer.
389. Senior Counsel referred to the evidence of RBK in cross examination by
Mr. Lynch KC, in which she agreed that the TCIG enjoyed a very wide
discretion under the Encouragement of Development Ordinance (EDO),
and that her view of the extent of the concessions offered by the Minister
was irrelevant.
390. Mr. Peterson SC, further submitted that the proposal by Stefan Kral with
respect to the construction of a port on Salt Cay for $3m to be offset against
the purchase price of 122 acres of Crown Land, which was agreed by
Cabinet on the recommendation of MM, was an entirely reasonable
proposal to improve the infrastructure of Salt Cay.
391. Mr. Peterson SC further noted that the TCIG refused the developer’s
request for stamp duty to be zero rated, on the developer’s proposal for the
upgrading of the airstrip and harbour. Instead, a stamp duty of 1% was
imposed on land purchased. This refusal, Mr. Peterson SC submits, would
be surprising, if MM was in fact acting corruptly to benefit the developer.
100

392. With respect to RBK’s opinion that the grant of a 5% concession on import
duty was overly generous, Senior Counsel referred to RBK’s evidence in
cross-examination that the issue of granting concessions were entirely
matters of discretion for the Ministers within the law, and that her opinion
was irrelevant.
393. More fundamentally, Senior Counsel submits, these decisions were plainly
matters of political judgment for MM and the TCIG. A balancing exercise
was required: grant the concessions or risk losing a mega project. The
decision, it is submitted was not tainted by corruption. A fair minded
tribunal of fact cannot be sure beyond reasonable doubt that these decisions
were tainted by bribery, to the exclusion of all other reasonable probabilities
or possibilities.
394. With respect to the issue of the refusal of the Physical Planning Board to
grant permission for the Salt Cay Barge Landing Dock and its subsequent
reversal of its decision, Mr. Peterson SC submits that the proper inferences
to be drawn was not corruption on MM’s part but “Ministerial frustration”
at an unreasonable obstacle which had been put in the way of the
construction of the barge landing dock. It is submitted that the tribunal of
fact cannot reject this possible inference to the extent that it is sure.
PAYMENTS AND TRANSFERS TO MICHAEL MISICK
395. Mr. Peterson SC submits that there is no evidence that MHo had anything
to do with J&T Banka’s decision to grant his application for an American
Express Centurion Card (Amex Card). Further, there was no evidence that
MM requested or was even aware of the $75,000 guarantee provided by the
Directors, Patrick Tkac and Joseph Spiziak, or the increase of the guarantee
to $200,000 by Directors Aser Stepan and Kamil Bendak. It is suggested
101

that the increase was granted “at the Bank’s behest” because of the level of
expenditure being incurred on the card.
396. It is further submitted that there is no evidence that MHo was a Director
or shareholder in J&T Banka at the time. However, MHo was a Director of
Istrokapital, which used J&T as one its bankers. This is evidence of what
is no more than a business relationship between MHo and the “top brass”
of J&T. MHo had no shareholding or interest in J&T. Mr. Peterson SC
submits that these observations apply with equal force to the grant of a $6m
loan to MM and his wife.
397. In response, the Crown notes that MM’s application for the Amex Card
came just a week after MM wrote to MHo informing him that the DA for
Salt Cay had been approved, and setting out the terms and conditions,
including the concessions granted. The Crown further observed that J&T
Banka was to be the principal lender for the project, agreeing to grant a
$36m loan to SCEL for the project.
398. The Crown further noted that MM failed to disclose this benefit in his
Declaration of Interest.
399. In relation to MM’s submission that there is no evidence that MHo played
any part in J&T’s decision to issue the Amex Card, the Crown argues that
this would mean that “out of the blue” a Slovakian Bank that had no prior
relationship with MM was prepared to issue and underwrite an Amex Card
with a guarantee of $75,000 “without any reason”. It was submitted that it
is a “clear and inevitable inference” that MHo, who was a “major long-term
client” of J&T Banka, had sufficient influence with the bank to persuade it
to grant the facility. Without him, it is submitted, “it is impossible to
conceive of circumstances” where the bank would grant such a facility to a
salaried politician of a small island.
102

400. The Crown further submits that MM must have known about the $75,000
guarantee and the increase to $200,000, because he would not have been
able to meet his expenditure without it. When he spent more than the
guarantee, it was increased. Further, it is submitted, it lacks “commercial
logic or common sense”, that the guarantee would be increased without the
knowledge of MM.
401. Mr. Peterson SC submits that the payments made by MHo, through his
attorneys Miller Simons O’Sullivan (MSO) to the PNP were clearly
political donations, and not bribes paid by MHo. These payments were:
- $50,000 made on 14th December 2005 for sponsorship of the Chief
Minister’s Christmas Party,
- $100,000 made on 9th August 2006,
- $100,000 made on 27th September 2006,
- $100,000 made on 1st December 2006, and
- $300,000 made on 16th January 2007.
402. Mr. Peterson SC submits that these were payments paid directly into
PNP’s bank accounts by a reputable law firm against whom no allegation
of impropriety has been raised. He submits that the tribunal of fact cannot
reasonably discount the possible inference that these were political
donations unconnected with and not intended to corruptly influence the Salt
Cay negotiations with TCIG.
403. In response the Crown notes that the $50,000 payment was not used for a
Chief Minister’s Christmas Party but was distributed among politicians
contrary to its stated purpose. Further, the Crown submits, there is no
evidence of MSO’s knowledge of the purpose of the funds and no safe
inference can be drawn from their status in these proceedings. The evidence
is that very large sums of money were flowing from the developer to the
103

direct or indirect benefit of the politicians. Further, the distribution of the
$50,000.00 payment does not support the application of the funds for any
“Chief Minister’s Christmas Party”, as stated, nor does it support any
application of the funds for “a Christmas Party for the PNP”, as suggest by
My Lynch KC.
404. Mr. Peterson SC further submits that there is no evidence that the grant of
the loan to MM and LRM to purchase a house in Los Angeles had anything
to do with MHo, and further, there is no evidence that the transaction was
anything other than “an arm’s length bona fide commercial loan
transaction”. As evidence of this, Senior Counsel noted the engagement of
Pro Partners Debt Collection Ltd by J&T Banka on 29th December 2009, to
recover the loan together with interest accrued. In addition, it’s the Crown’s
“theory” that the loan was a sham designed to mask a bribe to MM by MHo.
It would mean that unidentified senior banking executives at J&T must have
been complicit in the alleged bribery. As a result, the tribunal of fact cannot
be sure beyond reasonable doubt that the loan was a sham used by MHo to
mask corrupt payments to MM.
405. In response, the Crown noted that the Agreement for Sale of the house
dated 2nd October 2006, provided for a purchase price of $8m, with an initial
deposit of $200,000 and a further payment of $3.8m within 90 days. If the
purchaser defaulted, the deposit of $200,000 would be forfeited. At the time
MM was earning an annual salary of $152,829.11. The deposit was more
than MM’s annual salary. In addition, the Crown posed the question: where
was the further deposit of $3.8m to come from?
406. The Crown also noted TCM’s involvement in the loan application. On
17th January 2006, Business Ventures Ltd (BVL) was registered by
Chalmers Misick & Co. On 15th August 2006, Salt Cay Golf Club Ltd
(SCGL) was registered by Chalmers Misick & Co. BVL held a 50%
104

shareholding in SCGL, which was used as security for the $6m loan from
J&T. The Crown questions the necessity for TCM (MM’s brother) to
incorporate SCGL in view of the fact that MSO a well-established firm was
acting for MHo, in relation to the Salt Cay project. The Crown also
questioned why it was necessary for BVL to hold a 50% shareholding in
SCGL, which was formed, it appears, to hold a 99 year lease of 238 acres
of Crown Land for the purpose of establishing a golf course on Salt Cay.
The Crown submits that it made no commercial sense for BVL to hold 50%
of SCGL, and so becoming in effect, entitled to a 50% interest in the 99 year
lease. TCM signed the mortgage of the 50% share in SCGL, as a director
of BVL. In fact, on the date of the mortgage of the shares, TCM was not a
director. He was appointed a director on 18th November 2006, and ceased
to be a director on the same day. On 30th April 2007. Stefan Kral witnessed
the signature of TCM on the security document.
407. The Crown noted as well that the loan was approved on 30th April 2007,
just eleven days after the application was made, and disbursed to Chalmers
Misick & Co FCIB account just two weeks later, and then disbursed by
TCM for the benefit of MM and McH. The Crown further points to the fact
that the proceeds of the loan were never applied to the purchase of the Los
Angeles house. In fact, the evidence of LRM at the first trial on 18th October
2017 was that she had decided not to proceed with the purchase of the house
more than a month after the deposit of $200,000 was paid.
408. In response to the submission of Mr. Peterson SC that if the Crown’s
theory was correct, it means that senior bank officials of J&T Banka must
have been complicit in the bribery, and that the theory amounts to wild
speculation, the Crown refutes the suggestion, and submits that the evidence
invites an inference to be drawn from the fact that MM received
extraordinary sums from a bank with whom he had no prior relationship and
with whom his only connection was MHo. In addition, the Crown points to
105

the fact that J&T Banka took no steps to enforce the terms of the loan
agreement until 29th December 2009. The Commission of Inquiry was
announced on 10th July 2008, and submitted its’ Final Report on 31st May
2009.
SEA HORSE RANCH – UNDISPUTED FACTS
409. Sea Horse Ranch is a large gated development on the north coast of the
Dominican Republic, comprising a number of villas. Villa 127 was wholly
owned by SHR SOLAR 127 SA. incorporated in the Dominican Republic.
410. Eastern Townships Investments Inc., a company registered in Florida,
USA, was the owner of the shares in SHR Solar.
411. Portela Investments Group is a company registered in Panama. At a Board
meeting of Portela held on 12th May 2008, it was resolved that TCM be
appointed as Director/Chair/Treasurer. The shareholding (500 shares) was
issued in favour of TCM, making him the beneficial owner of Portela.
412. On 1st August 2008, there was a share sale agreement between Eastern
Townships Investments Inc. and Portela for the sale of Villa 127 Sea Horse
Ranch by means of a sale and transfer of shares in SHR SOLAR to Portela
for the sum of $3.1m. The seller confirmed receipt of the funds.
413. During the search of MM’s home, the police found the following
documents:
- Invoice from Sea Horse Ranch dated 31.05.10
- Invoice from Sea Horse Ranch dated 30.04.10
- 2 phone bills in the name of TCM for Villa 127 dated 16.04.10,
- Tenancy agreements with the name MM including buildings plans –
“House Del Alto Matua”, Santo Domingo and business cards,
106

- English version of Property Rental Agreement describing MM as the
“Surety Guarantor” with an address of “Pebble Street, Providenciales
Turks and Caicos Islands, BWI, and /or Sea Horse Ranch, Villa 127,
Sousa Cabarete, Rd”, and
- Sea Horse Ranch Sales Brochure.
414. The funds used by TCM for the purchase of the shares of SHR Solar were
provided by HMDL. The relevant transactions are noted in paragraph 351
above.
415. Mr. Peterson submits that there is no evidence that MM purchased Villa
127, or that he owns or has a beneficial interest in it. In his view, the
suggestion that Villa 127 was purchased by MHo for MM’s benefit pursuant
to a corrupt relationship, amounts to “impermissible speculation and
guesswork”. The most that the evidence is capable of proving when taken
at its highest is that MM “had a passing interest” in the address. Further,
the most that a reasonable and fair-minded fact finder could safely conclude
on the evidence, even when looked at against the totality of the Salt Cay
evidence, is that MM did little more than stay at the address during 2009,
after he had demitted office as Premier.
416. Senior Counsel further submits that there is no safe or reliable evidence
that could compel the tribunal of fact to the exclusion of all other reasonable
possibilities inconsistent with the prosecution’s case, that MM had anything
to do with the payments made by TCM to Guzman Ariza Associados, or
that he was “the client” referred to by TCM relating to the payments as made
on “client’s instructions”.
417. Mr. Peterson SC pointed out as well that the phone bills were in TCM’s
name, and the property rental agreement related to another apartment, not
Villa 127. The strongest inference to be made of the fact that MM gave his
107

address as Villa 127, was that MM was staying there when he signed the
agreement as guarantor.
418. In response, the Crown submits that the finding of the documents at MM’s
home establishes MM’s interest and connection to the property, and
supports the inference that he was the beneficial owner of the property.
Further, the alternative submission that MHo (through HMDL) provided,
without any documentation, $3m to TCM for his personal benefit is
unsupported by any evidence. The obvious inference, it is submitted, is that
this was another example of TCM concealing and disguising property for
MM.
419. The Crown submits that the issue is one of inference and not guesswork
or speculation, as suggested by MM. There is no reason, it is argued, why
MHo would fund a purchase of property for TCM. All that TCM did was
play the important role of layering, hiding and dealing with funds that were
meant for MM and McH. The proper inference therefore, was that TCM,
in keeping with this role as conduit, was “the front” for the $3m purchase
of Villa 127 (that HMDL provided) for the benefit of MM.
THE LAW OF BRIBERY
420. The law with respect to bribery is summarised at paragraphs 157-160 ante.
DISCUSSION
421. The discussion will be framed in accordance with the questions set out in
the agreed legal directions and route to verdict.
Question One
422. Question One is framed as follows:
- Did MHo and/or related entities directly or indirectly make payments to
MM?
108

423. The payments made by MHO or related entities have not been disputed.
The Court moves to question two.
424. Question Two
- Was MM a Minister of the Crown when he directly or indirectly
received payments from MHo or related entities?
425. There is no dispute on this issue. Clearly as Chief Minister and later
Premier, MM was a Minister at the material time outlined in the indictment.
The Court moves to question 3.
426. Question Three
- Were the payments made by MHo or related entities intended to induce
MM to act in a way that was corrupt and therefore contrary to the
obligations of honesty and integrity which he had as a Minister of the
Crown?
427. Between 14th December 2005 and 16th January 2007, the sum of $650,000
was transferred by MSO a law firm acting for MHo in respect of the Salt
Cay project, to accounts held by the PNP, of which MM was the political
leader. This is by no means an insignificant sum.
428. Mr. Peterson SC submits that these were clearly political donations made
directly to the PNP through a reputable firm of attorneys, unconnected with,
and not intended to corruptly influence, the Salt Cay negotiations. Mr.
Peterson SC submits that the tribunal of fact cannot reasonably discount this
inference.
429. In considering the proper inference to be drawn from the evidence the
court must weigh the following factors:
109

- the size of the payments;
- the context within which the payments were made, and
- the time at which the payments were made.
430. The payments made to the PNP during the period 14th December 2005 and
16th January 2007 were by no means insignificant, an initial payment of
$50,000, three payments of $100,000, and the last being $300,000. If not
intended to influence or reward the political decisions of the TCIG under
the leadership of MM, the payments were extraordinarily generous,
assuming MHo to be a reasonably astute and prudent businessman.
431. The context within which the payments were made, is important. The
investor was actively engaged in negotiations with MM and the TCIG for
the acquisition of Crown Land for an ever expanding project on Salt Cay
and for favourable concessions from the TCIG with respect to import duties
and stamp duty on purchases of land among other concessions. In the
opinion of RT and RBK, the concessions granted to the developer were
overly generous. In addition, the Crown Lands sold to the developers
included 13.9 acres, 30 acres, 41.5 acres, 22.14 acres, and 100 acres. A 99
year lease for 238.72 acres at a rental of $1.00 per acre per year, was also
granted. A request for a 99 year lease of an additional 637.15 acres was
also approved by Cabinet in August 2007. A Belonger discount of 50% was
applied to the sale of the 41.5 acres, together with a further discount of
$500,000, reducing the purchase price from $9.5m to $4.25m. A sale of
100 acres of Crown Land at $50,000.00 an acre was approved by Cabinet
on 11th April 2007. The land was subsequently valued at $12.932m.
432. The timing of the payments is also of interest. The $50,000 donation was
made two weeks before the ExCo approval of the sale of 13.9 acres and a
CPL of 30 acres on 11th January 2006. A donation of $100,000 to PNP was
made two weeks before the Cabinet decision on 23rd August 2006 to grant
110

an option to purchase 41.5 acres at a price of $4.25m, reduced from $9.5m.
On the same day that Belonger status was approved for MHo by Cabinet
(27th September 2006), a donation of $100,000 was made to the PNP. On
1st December 2006, a donation of $100,000 was made, followed by further
concessions approved by Cabinet on 21st December 2006. This was
followed by a donation of $300,000 on 16th January 2007.
433. The Court has considered the submission of Mr. Peterson SC that these
were political donations made without corrupt intentions. Of course, the
mere labelling of the payments as “political donations” does not mean that
they cannot be intended as bribes. Mr. Peterson SC, himself, candidly
conceded that such donations may be motivated by self-interest.
434. Having considered the submissions of counsel and carefully weighed the
factors set out in the preceding paragraphs, the Court comes to the
inexorable inference that the so called political donations were intended to
corruptly influence MM to make decisions that were favourable to MHo.
The Court makes this inference beyond reasonable doubt on the undisputed
evidence.
THE AMEX CENTURION CARD AND THE $6M LOAN FROM J&T
BANKA
435. On 8th December 2005, MM applied to J&T Banka for an American
Express Centurion Card (Amex Card). On 19th April 2007, the guarantee
of $75,000, signed by two directors of the bank was increased to $200,000.
Subsequently a supplementary Amex Card was given to LRM by J&T
Banka. The expenditure on the Amex cards for MM and LRM amounted
to $4.881m by 2009.
111

436. On 20th April 2007 MM and LRM applied for a $6m loan from J&T Banka
to buy a house in Los Angeles, USA, which was approved by the bank on
30th April 2007.
437. Mr. Peterson’s submissions on the issue of the Amex Centurion Card to
MM and the grant of the loan for $6m from J&T Banka, may be summarised
as follows:
- There is no evidence to suggest that MHo and anything to do with J&T’s
decision to grant the Amex card;
- There is no evidence that MM requested or was even aware of the
$75,000 guarantee signed by Directors Patrik Tkac and Joseph Spiziak;
- There is no evidence that MHo requested the increase in the guarantee
to $200,000 by two different directors of J&T, or that MHo played any
part in the bank’s decision;
- There is no evidence that MHo was a director or shareholder of J&T
Banka at the relevant time;
- The highest the Crown’s case can be put is that MHo was a director of
Istrokapital, and J&T was one of its three bankers;
- There is evidence of a business relationship between MHo and the “top
brass” of J&T.
438. The Crown’s response may be summarised as follows:
- MM’s application for the Amex card was made a week after MM wrote
to MHo informing him of the approval of the DA for Salt Cay, and the
concessions granted;
- J&T Banka was the principal financier for the project agreeing to grant
a loan of $36m to SCEL.
- J&T was a Slovakian bank which had no prior relationship with MM;
- MHo was a “major client” of J&T Banka, who had sufficient influence
over J&T to influence them to grant the facility of the Amex card;
112

- MM must have known about the $75,000 guarantee, and its increase to
$200,000, since he would not have been able to meet his expenses
without it; and
- It makes no commercial or commonsense that the guarantee would be
increased without MM’s knowledge.
439. Mr. Peterson further submits:
- There is no evidence that the grant of the $6m loan to MM and LRM by
J&T Banka was anything but “an arm’s length and commercial loan
transaction”;
- This is supported by the appointment of a debt collection agency on 29th
December 2009 to recover the debt, and
- If the Crown’s case is sustainable, it would mean that senior banking
executives were complicit in the alleged bribery.
440. In response, the Crown pointed to:
- The fact that the agreement for sale provided for a deposit of $200,000
(more than MM’s annual salary of $152,829.11) and a further deposit
of $3.8m within 90 days;
- The $6m proceeds were remitted by J&T to TCM, who disbursed it for
MM’s benefit;
- MM and LRM did not purchase the house;
- LRM’s evidence was that she had changed her mind about purchasing
the house more than a month after the deposit was paid on 17th October
2006;
- The further deposit of $3.8m which was due within 90 days of the
acceptance of the agreement signed on 2nd October 2006, was not paid
