by MM indicating that as early as 31st December 2006, MM had decided
not to pursue the purchase.
FINDINGS OF FACT
113

441. The Court is unable to agree with Mr. Peterson’s submission that there is
no evidence that MHo had anything to do with the grant by J&T Banka of
the Amex card and the $6m loan, and that these transactions were bona fide
commercial transactions. In coming to this finding, the Court has taken into
account the following matters:
- While MHo was not a director or shareholder of J&T Banka, MHo
clearly had a good relationship with the bank through his company
Istrokapital, so much so that J&T Banka agreed to provide financing for
the project by a loan facility of $36m;
- There is evidence that by October 2005, MM had developed a personal
relationship with MHo, and travelled to Slovakia by private jet to
Slovakia in October 2005 to meet with MHo;
- It is highly unlikely that a politician in TCI should choose to apply to a
Slovakian bank, with whom, on the evidence, he had no prior
relationship, for an Amex Centurion card carrying guarantees of
$75,000 and $200,000 and later should be granted a loan facility of $6m;
- It is highly unlikely that a prudent banker would grant these facilities to
an applicant who on the evidence, was not an existing customer, and
whose income at the time was $152,829.11 per annum or approximately
$13,000.00 per month;
- The interest payment on the loan was $51,250 per month, about 4 times
MM’s monthly salary;
- MM had a choice with respect to the term of payment – one year, five
years or over five years. He chose one year, incurring a monthly
instalment of $500,000 plus interest at 10.25% per month;
- There is no evidence of any attempt by MM to repay the loan;
- Under the loan agreement, the principal and interest was due on 29th
April 2008;
- It was not until 29th December 2009 that J&T took steps to recover the
loan of $6m together with interest of $1,807,835.80 and the sum of
114

$415,064.64 owing on the Amex Centurion Card plus $26,496.75 in
interest.
442. The Court considers the role of Business Ventures Ltd (BVL) in providing
security to J&T Banka for the $6m loan to MM and LRM, to be significant.
BVL was registered on 17th January 2006. Chalmers Misick & Co., was the
agent from 12th January 2006 to 18th November 2006. No directors or
secretary were appointed until 18th November 2006, when Chalmers
Management Ltd and TCM were appointed. TCM’s directorship ceased the
same day. TCM was the sole shareholder from 18th November 2006 to 20th
February 2008 when Sun Beach Ventures Ltd acquired one share.
443. Salt Cay Golf Club Ltd (SCGL) was registered on 15th August 2006. Its
agent at the time of registration was Chalmers Misick & Co. There were no
directors appointed until 15th December 2007 when Windsor East Ltd and
Stephan Kral were appointed. The shareholders as at 15th December 2007
were BVL and HMDL, each holding 50 shares. BVL’s 50 shares were
transferred to SCEL on 14th May 2010.
444. As the Crown points out, it is curious that SCGL, which was to be granted
a 99 year lease of 238 acres of Crown land, was incorporated by TCM, and
not MSO, which was acting for MHo for the purposes of the Sale Cay
project.
445. As the Crown further submits, it makes no commercial sense that 50% of
the shareholding in SCGL should be held by BVL, a company in which
neither MHo, nor his associated entities had an interest. The 50%
shareholding in SCGL was used as security for the $6m loan granted by
J&T Banka. TCM signed the security document as Director of BVL on 27th
April 2007, when he clearly was not a director, as recorded in the Financial
115

Services Commission search results. Interestingly Stefan Kral, CEO of
several of MHo’s companies, witnessed TCM’s signature on the document.
446. From the evidence as outlined above, a clear and compelling inference
may be drawn that BVL was created by TCM, and used for the purpose of
providing security for the loan to MM and LRM. Why else would BVL, a
company which on the face of the FSC records had no connection with
MHo, be endowed with 50 shares in SCGL, actually 50% of its
shareholding?
447. The Court notes that BVL was registered on 17th January 2006, while
SCGL was registered on 15th August 2006. Both companies were registered
by Chalmers Misick & Co. The 99 year lease of 239 acres (approx.) to
SCGL was signed on 28th December 2006. The security was signed by
TCM on 27th April 2007. The sequence of events noted above, gives rise to
the clear inference that there was an element of preplanning in the creation
of BVL, its endowment with 50 shares in SCGL, and the use of the shares
in arranging the $6m loan from J&T Banka to MM & LRM.
448. From the matters outlined in paragraphs 435 to 447, the following clear
and compelling inferences are drawn by the Court:
- By October 2005, MM had developed a personal relationship with
MHo;
- MHo, through Istrokapital had a business relationship with J&T Banka,
which agreed to finance his Salt Cay project with a loan facility of
$36m;
- MHo used his influence with J&T Banka to cause the bank to issue the
Amex Centurion Card to MM, to increase the guarantee from $75,000
to $200,000, and to grant a supplementary card to LRM;
- Without the influence of MHo, it is extremely unlikely that an
established bank operating in Slovakia thousands of miles from TCI,
116

with whom, on the evidence, MM had no previous relationship, would
grant the Amex Card, and the loan of $6m, to a politician in the TCI,
earning approximately $13,000 per month, given the fact that the
monthly interest payment would be about four times his monthly salary;
- By 20th April 2007, the date of the application for the $6m loan, MM
and LRM were no longer interested in pursuing the purchase of a house
in California, USA;
- It was never intended that the loan should be repaid. The principal and
interest was due on 29th April 2008, yet the bank took no steps to recover
the debt until 29th December 2009, long after the Commission of Inquiry
was appointed, and
- The Amex card and the $6m loan granted to MM and LRM by J&T,
were engineered by MHo and were intended by him to cause MM to act
in a way that was corrupt and contrary to his obligations of honesty and
integrity.
ARLING ANSTALT (AA)
449. AA was set up by Markus Hasler in Liechtenstein on behalf of MHo on 9th
January 2006. On 2nd March 2006 MHo signed a declaration that he had
transferred AA to Brano Prielozny on 15th February 2006. On 28th August
2006, Markus Hasler, a director of AA approved a loan of $1m to MM. On
16th June 2008, the loan was increased to $2m. AA held an account with
J&T Banka. The loan account held at J&T was applied over a period of
approximately one year to pay for MM (and MCH’s) expenditure on their
Amex Cards.
450. Mr. Peterson SC relies heavily on the fact that the beneficial owner of AA
at the time of the loan was not MHo, but Brano Prielozny. He contends that
MHo had nothing to do with the loan. He referred to the evidence of Markus
Hasler, who had approved the $1m loan as Director of AA in support of this
contention.
117

451. The case for the Crown is that AA was a vehicle used by MHo to channel
funds to MM (and MCH) to pay for expenditure on their Amex cards. It
was never intended that these “loans” were to be repaid by MM.
452. In deciding the reasonable inferences to be drawn from the evidence, the
Court takes into account the coincidence of the following factors:
- AA is a company registered in Lichtenstein on 27th January 2006 by
Markus Hasler who clearly was an agent of MHo;
- MHo was the beneficial owner of AA;
- A little over a month later, MHo signed a declaration stating that he
founded the company, and had transferred it to Brano Prielozny on 15th
February 2006;
- MM was a politician in TCI, thousands of miles away, who on the
evidence had no previous connection with AA;
- MM met MHo at his home in August 2005, and later at his office, to
discuss MHo’s proposals for a development on Salt Cay;
- MM later travelled to Slovakia to meet with MHo in October 2005;
- The Salt Cay project was continuously expanding, requiring
negotiations with MHo and his associates for large acreages of Crown
land, and concessions;
- AA, which had no previous relationship with MM, a salaried politician
in a British Overseas Territory thousands of miles away agreed to grant
a loan of $1m to MM in August 2006, and increased it to $2m in June
2008;
- There is no evidence of repayment of the loan;
- There is no evidence of any steps taken by AA to recover the loan, and
- The loan was applied to pay off expenditure on MM’s Amex Card.
453. From the factors listed above, the Court draws the following inexorable
inferences beyond reasonable doubt:
118

- AA was a creature of MHo, and was used by him to further his own
purposes;
- The only connection that MM had with AA, on the evidence, was MHo,
with whom he had developed a personal relationship;
- It was never intended that MM would be required to repay the “loan”;
- The loan was in fact a device used by MHo, to provide a benefit to MM,
whose expenditure on the card could not be sustained by his income as
Premier.
HARBOUR MANAGEMENT DEVELOPMENT LIMITED (HMDL)
454. HMDL was registered in Cyprus on 28th August 2006. On 29th August
2006, MHo acquired the entire shareholding. HMDL entered into a
Termination Agreement with Ben Walkin, acting jointly with Caribbean
Investments Company Ltd (CIIC) on 29th September 2006, under which it
agreed to pay Walkin $3m, by an initial payment of $300,000, and
subsequent payments of $2m and $700,000.00.
455. The sum of $2m was transferred by HMDL on 10th October 2006 to
Chalmers Misick & Co. The sum of $700,000.00 was transferred by HMDL
on 11th December 2006 to Chalmers Misick & Co. Of the $2.7m paid by
HMDL, Walkin received only $1m. $300,000 was paid by MHo on 7th
August 2006, and $1m paid by TCM to Walkin, on 12th October 2006. The
remaining $1.7m was disbursed by TCM, as set out in paragraphs 277, 278
and 287 ante.
456. The Court infers from the payment of the $2.7m. in purported pursuance
of the Termination Agreement and the subsequent disbursement of $1.7m
of it for the benefit of MM by TCM that there was an intention by MHo to
benefit MM. The fact that TCM did not pay over $1.7m of the funds in
accordance with the Termination Agreement, but used the money to benefit
MM, supports a conclusive inference that MHo intended that these monies
119

were intended to be used to benefit MM. The Termination Agreement
clearly had a dual purpose – to pay off Walkin for his services to the Salt
Cay project, and to provide a vehicle for MHo to pass funds to MM.
457. Accordingly, this Court finds beyond reasonable doubt on the evidence
that the payment of $1.7m, was intended by MHo to influence MM in the
performance of his duties as Minister of the Crown.
458. On 15th January 2009, HMDL transferred $500,000 from its J&T Banka
account to Chalmers Misick & Co., with a remittance advice “for credit to
Business Ventures Ltd. Purchase Price SCGC/Purchase Price for share in
SCGC”. The payment was disbursed over the following 20 days by TCM.
459. On 14th May 2010, the BVL shares in SCGL were transferred to SCEL.
On the same day Windsor East Ltd ceased to be a Director, and Secretary,
leaving Stefan Kral as sole Director and Secretary of SCGL.
460. Mr. Peterson SC submits that the tribunal of fact cannot safely find that
the payment from HMDL was corrupt and was knowingly and intentionally
received as such. The Crown argues that if the charge held by J&T on the
shares was genuine, then why were the shares transferred to SCEL, which
was beneficially owned by MHo?
461. As noted earlier the Court has found that there was no apparent
commercial reason why BVL, an entity formed and controlled by TCM, was
made a 50% shareholder of SCGL, which later acquired a 99 year lease of
239 acres (approx.) of Crown Land. The shares were later used as security
for a $6m loan from J&T Banka to MM and his wife. Ostensibly J&T
Banka held the legal title in the shares as collateral for the loan, which was
never repaid. How then, does HMDL make a payment for these shares held
by J&T Banka on 15th January 2009, and the shares are later transferred to
120

SCEL about 5 months later? Why would MHo pay BVL (through HMDL)
for shares in SCGL, which, were mortgaged to J&T Banka, as security for
the loan of $6m to MM and his wife?
462. On its face, the transaction appears to be artificial. The question is,
however, does the evidence lead the court to the sure conclusion that MHo
(through HMDL) made the payment with the intention of influencing MM
to act in a corrupt manner?
463. As noted throughout this judgment, TCM was receiving payments from
MHo related entities and was disbursing them for MM’s purposes. There
was no rational basis for MHo to make payments for TCM’s personal
benefit. The disbursements of the $500,000 are depicted in chart 36. In his
evidence in chief, Mr. Glendinning was taken through the disbursements.
No connection was made by the witness to MM and the disbursements.
Accordingly, the Court is unable to draw any inference from the
disbursements, that MHo intended to benefit MM by the payment of
$500,000 though HMDL.
SEA HORSE RANCH
464. The payments made by TCM to Guzman and Associates for the acquisition
of Villa 127 Sea Horse Ranch are detailed in paragraph 351 ante. Of
particular interest is the transfer of $3m on 29th July 2008 from HMDL to
Guzman and Associates. The transfer was referenced “share purchase
price”. The undisputed evidence is that Villa 127 was purchased through a
share transfer from SHR Solar 127 S.A. which owned Villa 127 and Eastern
Township Investment Inc, which owned the shares in SHR Solar, to Partela
Investments Group, (the purchaser) which was beneficially owned by TCM.
The purchase price for the shares was $3.1m. The purchase price was
financed by the $3m transfer to Guzman and Associates by HMDL.
121

465. The Crown’s case is that TCM was no more than a conduit for MM,
receiving funds on his behalf and layering, hiding, and dealing with the
funds meant for MM (and MCH), and he was “the front” for the $3m
received from HMDL to purchase Villa 127 for MM’s benefit.
466. The Crown further relies on documents found at MM’s home on 28th
January 2014, which they submit connect MM to Villa 127 in a significant
way. These are:
- An envelope containing a statement dated 30th April 2010 from Sea
Horse Ranch detailing payment due for utilities plus interest due;
- Another statement dated 16th April 2010 for payments due;
- Phone bills dated 16th April 2010;
- A rental agreement for a property in Santo Domingo, commencing 4th
May 2009, which MM signed as guarantor and giving his address in
Providenciales TCI as well as Sea Horse Ranch, Villa 127, and
- An envelope containing an advertisement dated March 2008, for land
and villas for sale at Sea Horse Ranch.
467. Mr. Peterson SC points out that the statements and phone bills do not bear
MM’s name, and argues that they are not connected to him. Further the
brochure is something that anyone may pick up while at a resort and bring
home. The address given in the lease agreement may have been given
simply because MM might have been staying at Sea Horse Ranch at the
time he agreed to act as guarantor.
468. The inferences suggested by Mr. Peterson SC are not unreasonable, if the
items are considered separately. The statements for services to Villa 127
do not bear the name of the person billed while the phone bills are addressed
to TCM. The question arises, why would these statements and phone bills
be in the possession of MM, if he merely visited the Villa and had no
beneficial interest in it?
122

469. Interestingly, the brochure which advertises the plots of land and Villas
bears the date “March 2008”. Villa 127 is advertised for sale for $3.5m.
The share sale agreement for the acquisition of Villa 127 was dated 1st
August 2008 suggesting that MM’s interest in the property must have arisen
some time before it was actually acquired. Why would this brochure be in
the possession of MM, if he had no interest in the property beyond that of a
visitor?
470. The items found at MM’s home must be considered together with the other
evidence in the case, in particular, the role of TCM in receiving money on
his behalf and disbursing it for his benefit. The evidence of the payment of
$3m by HMDL to Guzman and Associates to acquire the shares in SHR
Solar, is not disputed. The question arises, who was intended to benefit
from the acquisition of Villa 127? TCM became the beneficial owner of
Portela Investments Group, which acquired the shares in SHR Solar.
471. HMDL was owned and controlled by MHo. There is no commercial
reason why MHo would seek to finance the purchase of a $3.1m villa for
TCM. Given his role as acting as a conduit for his brother MM, the obvious
and compelling inference to be drawn on the totality of the evidence, is that
TCM acquired Villa 127, as a front for MM, who was in a position to further
the interests of MHo in his Salt Cay investment.
ISTROKAPITAL
472. Istrokapital was incorporated in Cyprus on 1st February 2007. MHo was
a director. On 12th June 2008, Istrokapital transferred $800,000 from its
J&T Banka account to MSO. On 17th June 2008, $274,621.81 was
transferred from MSO Terrapin Investments funds to MM’s J&T Banka
account to pay off MM’s Amex Centurion Card and to pay J&T Banka for
previous payments made by them to cover MM’s Amex Card expenditure.
123

473. Mr. Peterson SC submitted that the witness Peter Glendinning was unable
to assist the Court with the reason behind the payment of $274,621.81 to
MM’s card, or whether it was or might have been connected to the sale of
an interest in land behind the Casablanca Casino. The response of the
Crown to this suggestion is that there is simply no evidence in the case of
any such transaction. On the face of it, there was a transfer of funds to MSO
from Istrokapital, followed by a payment of $274,621.81 from MSO to J&T
Banka to pay for expenditure on MM’s Amex Card.
474. The clear and compelling inference to be drawn from the evidence is that
Istrokapital, a company controlled by MHo, provided funds to his attorneys
MSO, and instructed MSO to apply part of these funds towards payment of
MM’s Amex card.
475. Based on the foregoing discussion, the Court finds beyond reasonable
doubt that the payments made by MHo or related entities were intended to
induce MM to act in a way that was corrupt and contrary to MM’s
obligations of honesty and integrity.
Question Four
476. The agreed route to verdict sets out this issue as follows:
- Were the payments knowingly and intentionally received by MM as an
inducement to act corruptly and therefore contrary to his obligations of
honesty and integrity as a Minister of the Crown?
477. This question focuses on the mens rea of the recipient of the payments.
There are two aspects of the mental element:
- The knowledge of the recipient and
- The intention of the recipient.
124

478. It is not disputed:
- that the PNP received donations totaling $600,000 from MSO, on behalf
of Terrapin Investments (an MHo entity) during the period December
2005 and January 2007, when MHo (and his related entities, were
actively engaged in negotiations for the acquisition of Crown Land, and
concessions from TCIG;
- That MM applied for and was granted an Amex Centurion Card by J&T
Banka with a guarantee of $75,000 later increased to $200,000,
approved by directors of the bank and a supplementary card was later
issued to his wife;
- That J&T Banka granted a $6m loan to MM and LRM, the proceeds of
which were wired to TCM, who disbursed the money for the benefit of
MM;
- That a loan of $1m was granted by AA to MM, and later increased to
$2m, the proceeds of which were used to service MM’s Amex Card;
- That the sum of $274,621.81, was transferred by MSO from MHo’s
Terrapin client account to pay MM’s Amex expenditure on 17th June
2008, and
- That $3m was paid by HMDL to Guzman and Associates for the
acquisition of Villa 127, Sea Horse Ranch.
479. Mr. Peterson SC has submitted that there is no evidence that MM knew of
the guarantees of $75,000 and $200,000 signed by directors of J&T Banka.
The Court considers that this is highly unlikely, Bankers are not likely to
grant such accommodations, unless they are requested by the client, or as in
this case, they are supported by a customer who has the resources and
influence to persuade the bank to grant the facility.
480. The clear and compelling inference to be drawn on the evidence, is that
MM had knowledge of the payments. With respect to the payments made
directly to Chalmers Misick & Co., the inference is clear: How else would
125

TCM know how to make disbursements, which were undoubtedly for MM’s
or LRM’s benefit? The payments made were for specific amounts to
specific payees. The inexorable inference is that MM and/or LRM provided
the details of the intended payees and the exact amounts to be paid.
481. The question that has to be considered is whether MM received these
benefits and payments knowingly and intentionally as inducements for him
to act corruptly.
482. In deciding this issue, the Court takes into account:
- MM did not disclose in his declaration to the Registrar of Interests, the
trip to Slovakia, the grant of the Amex Card, and his loans from J&T
Banka and AA;
- The grant of requests from MHo for increasing acreages for Crown
Land;
- The valuation of Crown Lands conveyed or leased, and the actual prices
agreed by Cabinet, on the recommendation of MM;
- The concessions granted to MHo or his related entities;
- The grant of Belongership status to MHo, and the discounts granted on
the sale of Crown Land;
- The reversal of the decision to refuse planning permission for the barge
dock, and
- The grant of rights to manage the port and airport.
483. In considering these matters the Court bears in mind the submissions of
Senior Counsel that:
- The investments in Salt Cay were risky, involved substantial sums of
money, and were extremely important for the development of the island;
- The concessions that were granted were within the statutory limits
imposed by the Encouragement of Development Ordinance;
126

- The concerns expressed by RT and RBK were in fact their opinions
only;
- The decisions taken by MM were a matter of political judgment,
balancing the need for development of Salt Cay against perceived
advantage for the investor, and
- The urgency to ensure that the proposed investment was not lost,
required the direct engagement of the TCIG with the developer in order
to avoid bureaucratic delay, and the use of Oral Mentions.
484. In deciding the intention of MM in receiving the financial benefits from
MHo, the Court must weigh in the balance inter alia:
- The reduction in the purchase price of 41 acres of Crown Land from
$9.5m to $4.25m which included a 50% Belonger discount, and a further
reduction of $500,000;
- At the time of the Cabinet decision on 23rd August 2006 MHo was not
a Belonger. He became a Belonger on 17th November 2006, when the
Certificate of Belongership was signed by the Governor;
- The query on this issue from Glenda Clarke of the AG Chambers, drew
a sharp rebuke from McH;
- The lease of 99 years at $1 per acre per year of 238.72 acres of land
valued at $7.76m by Mr. Hoza;
- The decision of Cabinet on 11th April 2007, (on a paper presented by
McH) to sell 122.14 acres of Crown Land for $6.102m, without a
valuation of 100 acres, at an arbitrary value of $50,000 per acre;
- The 100 acres was subsequently valued by Mr. Hoza at $12.932m;
- The agreement of Cabinet on 29th August 2007 to grant a 99 year lease
of a further 637.15 acres of Salt Cay;
- The reversal of the refusal of planning permission for the construction
of a barge dock, following a heated meeting at MM’s office on 9th
January 2009, at which Clyde Robinson, the Director of Planning was
subjected to obscene verbal abuse by McH;
127

- The practice of MM (and McH) to inform MHo promptly of favourable
Cabinet decisions;
- The rejection by MM of concerns raised by RT and RBK as to the extent
of concessions, acreages granted, and the concession granted to the
developers to manage the airport.
485. While all these decisions were being made by the Cabinet, on
recommendation by MM (and McH), payments were being received by the
PNP and MM as follows:
- $650,000 to the PNP;
- A grant of an Amex Centurion Card to MM and LRM, on which
expenditure of $4.881m was incurred;
- The grant of a $6m loan from J&T Banka to MM and LRM;
- The grant of a $1m loan to MM, increased to $2m by AA, used to pay
for the expenditure on the Amex Card;
- There was no attempt to repay these loans;
- These loans were not disbursed directly to MM;
- There was no disclosure to the Registrar of Interests of any of the
facilities received by MM;
- The payment of $3m from HMDL to Guzman and Associates on 29.7.08
to acquire Villa 127, Sea Horse Ranch, and
- The transfer of $274,621.81 from MSO Terrapin Investments Client
Account to pay J&T Banka for expenditure on MM’s Amex Card, on
17th June 2008.
486. It was open to MM, if he so desired, to give evidence to explain the receipt
and disbursement of funds directly or indirectly connected to MHo, or his
entities. He elected, as is his right, not to give evidence or to call witnesses.
The Court draws no adverse inferences from his election. The consequence
of his decision, however, is that the Court has received no evidence from
128

MM or any witness to explain the payments, and is left to draw reasonable
inferences from the evidence provided by the Crown.
487. The Court has considered the submissions of Mr. Peterson very carefully.
There can be little doubt that the investment in Salt Cay was likely to bring
substantial benefit to the development of the island. It was an investment
that a responsible government would be inclined to support with a high level
of enthusiasm, and would justifiably take steps to avoid possible
bureaucratic delays. All this would of course be commendable if MM was
motivated by the interests of TCI, and not his own enrichment.
488. The evidence of the benefits received by MM from MHo or his entities is
not controverted. MM received benefits estimated by the Crown to be worth
$13,281,241.00. The fact that the intended investment would substantially
benefit the economy of Salt Cay and TCI, does not in any way detract from
the fact that MM received these benefits from an investor who was actively
engaged in negotiations with TCIG, and who was being granted generous
concessions and reduced prices on very large acreages of land, substantially
below valuations carried out by the Chief Valuation Officer for TCIG.
489. From all the circumstances set out above, the Court comes to the clear and
compelling inference that MM knowingly and intentionally received the
benefits and payments as an inducement for him to act corruptly, and
therefore contrary to the obligations of honesty and integrity which he had
as a Minister of the Crown.
490. The Court makes this finding beyond reasonable doubt, bearing in mind
that if there are other inferences open to the Court which weaken the
Crown’s case, or are inconsistent with guilt, the Court must come to a
verdict of not guilty. The Court found no such inferences.
129

Conclusion
491. It follows that this Court finds MM guilty on Count 2. In coming to this
verdict, the Court expressly takes into account the propensity limb of the
good character direction as laid down in Vye.
THE CASE AGAINST MCALLISTER HANCHELL – COUNT 2
492. The case for the Crown is that between 1st January 2005 and 30th April
2009, McH accepted inducements directly or indirectly from MHo or his
related and connected entities by unlawful corrupt payments or other
rewards such as credit, while he was a Minister of TCIG.
493. The inducements which McH received through MHo in connection with
the Salt Cay project are alleged to be:
- The grant of an Amex Centurion Card to himself and subsequently to
his wife, through J&T Banka;
- The disbursement of funds by TCM from the receipt of the $6m loan
on behalf of MM in May and June 2007, used to make payments for the
benefit of McH as follows;
- $450,000 to South Point Ltd, for the construction of McH’s house by
Arlington Musgrove, controller of Jaca Ltd;
- $77,500 as a deposit towards McH’s Golf Course Condominium, and
- $368,241.02 to Saunders and Co. marked “Hanchell Loan repayment &
interest”;
- $40,564 paid towards expenditure on McH’s Amex Card;
- $100,000 by cheque to Hyd Ltd per client instructions deposited into an
FCIB account in the name of McH and his wife.
494. On behalf of McH, Mr. Lynch KC submitted:
- McH was a successful businessman, who through his companies, AL
Services and Caicos Oil, earned a personal income of $6.5m between
130

2004 and 2009 (less $900k borrowed from Saunders & Co., which he
repaid);
- Being a man of substantial means, McH did not need to take bribes;
- McH’s Amex cards showed expenditure of $1.15m between April 2007
to August 2008, which was “unremarkable” given his business interests;
- He borrowed “prolifically”, often between friends or trusted business
associates “on a handshake”, that is, without the need for written
agreements;
- Any money that was provided to McH, that the Crown alleges was a
bribe, was a legitimate loan that had nothing to do with Salt Cay (or
West Caicos);
- Any concessions granted were well within the perimeters set out in the
legislation and Crown Land Policies; in fact much larger concessions
could have been offered;
- The “hands on” approach of McH was as a direct result of issues with
the public service and government departments that created delays, and
did not operate with the expediency and efficiency required by
developers seeking to invest large sums of money in the islands.
495. Mr. Lynch KC further pointed to his client’s good character, which the
Court expressly acknowledges, and takes into account in its deliberations.
496. The following general points were also highlighted by Mr. Lynch SC:
- The Ministerial code of conduct was unrealistic, in that it required
divestment of any outside interests or directorships which may conflict
with ministerial duties;
- In particular Mr. Lynch KC referred to the 1998 code, which did not
have the force of law, but expressly proscribed the acceptance by
Ministers of any kind of favor from persons with whom they were
negotiating “contractual or proprietary or pecuniary relations” with
TCIG, or acceptance of gifts of substantial value from members of the
131

public; Mr. Lynch’s view was that this requirement ignores local
custom;
- The Register of Interests requires the declaration of gifts; it was not
necessary to declare loans;
- RT took up his post with “an agenda” to obtain enough material to
support initiating a Commission of Inquiry (COI); the FCO chose to
entrap Ministers into making bad decisions to justify this agenda,
instead of instructing RT not to sign off on any substantial land
transaction without an independent report on viability and value for
money, and
- The public service including the AG’s office was inefficient, slow and
under-resourced, prompting Ministers to take direct action to expedite
processes, sometimes bypassing established protocols.
497. Mr. Lynch KC focused particularly on Clyde Robinson (CR), the Director
of Planning, as a civil servant who was clearly obstructive to the reasonable
plans of the TCIG. His submission was that CR’s approach to the barge
dock on Salt Cay was rigid, in that he could have granted conditional
approval. His failure to do so undermined and delayed a “critical”
government project.
498. Mr. Lynch KC, referred to the evidence of RBK and Clayton Been (CB),
who both attested as to the inefficiency of the public service, and submitted
that, as a result, direct interaction between Ministers and developers was “a
known, accepted, and practically necessary feature of the investment
landscape and was not inherently improper or contrary to the established
procedure in the TCI”.
499. On the other hand, RT’s evidence that “in principle” TCInvest and AGC
should take the lead in negotiations was, it was submitted, a “potential ideal
132

or theoretical workflow”, which contrasted sharply with the practical
realities. RT was not a businessman, and had an agenda.
500. Mr. Lynch KC sought to debunk the idea that oral mentions were used by
Ministers to circumvent proper procedure and expedite matters without
adequate prior scrutiny by the Cabinet. It was submitted that the oral
mention process did not inherently prejudice proper consideration, since the
process did not prevent discussion by members, and the Governor had the
power to request further information, and to defer or veto the
recommendation.
501. With respect to the concessions granted in respect of the Salt Cay project
Mr. Lynch KC emphasised the remoteness of the island, the competing
environment where there were more developed and better-known islands,
and the need to make the investment more attractive by providing
sweeteners or incentives. In addition there were the benefits to Salt Cay in
terms of infrastructure development, community benefits and employment
and economic activity on the island.
502. Mr. Lynch KC further pointed out that McH did not become Minister for
Natural Resources until February 2006. The grant of the 99 year lease of
222 acres to MHo for the development of a golf course was a clear benefit
for the development of a remote island. It was a matter of political judgment
at what price the TCIG sells the land and grants concessions balancing the
risks and difficulties the developer was undertaking.
503. The Crown submits that there is “no honest commercial explanation as to
why McH received the payments from TCM, through disbursements to
satisfy McH’s debts. The Crown further submits that “the hidden financial
relationship” in respect of the Amex card, together with the receipt of
monies through TCM point to a corrupt relationship with the developers.
133

THE LAW
504. The agreed directions with respect to the law of bribery have already been
set out at paragraphs 157 to 160 ante. They are repeated here.
DISCUSSION
505. The discussion is framed around the 4 questions formulated in the agreed
Route to Verdict (RTV).
Question One
506. The first question posed is whether MHo and/or related entities, directly
or indirectly made payments to McH.
507. McH has not denied the disbursement of the funds applied by TCM
towards payment of McH’s expenditure and debts. Nor has McH denied
the receipt of the funds, or the Amex cards received by him and his wife.
508. McH submits that the disbursements made by TCM were in fact a loan
from MM to McH.
509. The Crown has emphatically submitted that there is absolutely no
evidence, documentary or otherwise that the disbursements by TCM to
McH were, in fact, loans from MM to McH. The assertion that the payments
were loans from MM, arose only in the submissions of Mr. Lynch KC.
There is no evidence to support the submission.
510. The grant of the Amex Centurion Card to McH and his wife, also appears
to be directly traceable to MHo through his relationship with J&T Banka.
There is no evidence of any prior banking relationship between McH and
J&T Banka, a bank thousands of miles away from TCI, in Slovakia with
which MHo appears to have had an influential relationship, so much so that
134

J&T Banka agreed to grant a loan of $36m to finance the Salt Cay project.
The only plausible link, on the evidence, between McH and J&T Banka, is
MHo, with whom the TCIG was engaged in active negotiations, and for
whom McH appears to have shown appreciable enthusiasm in furthering his
interests.
511. Accordingly, the Court concludes that the monies used by TCM to pay for
expenditure and debts on behalf of McH, and the grant of the Amex
Centurion Cards to McH and his wife, are directly linked to MHo through
his relationship with J&T Banka.
512. The answer to question one is therefore in the affirmative.
Question Two
513. There is no issue that McH was a Minister of the Crown when he received
payments from MHo or related entities.
Question Three
514. The issue is whether MHo or his related entities made the payments to
induce McH to act corruptly and contrary to his obligations of honesty and
integrity.
515. The question focuses on the intention of MHo in facilitating the issue of
the Amex Centurion Cards to McH, and the $6m loan from J&T Banka from
which approximately $900,000 was disbursed for the benefit of McH.
516. The undisputed evidence is that at the time that these facilities were
provided to McH, he was the Minister of Natural Resources, and MHo was
actively engaged with TCIG in negotiations for ever increasing acreages of
Crown land and concessions such as reduced stamp duty and import duties,
135

and discounts on the purchase price and leases of large tracts of Crown
Land.
517. McH became Minister of Natural Resources from 6th February 2006. His
portfolio included the disposition of Crown Lands. He was therefore in a
position to facilitate MHo’s requests for large acreages of Crown Land.
Dispositions of Crown Land to MHo’s companies included:
- on 23rd August 2006, Cabinet agreed to grant an option to purchase 41
acres of Crown Land valued at $9.5m, at a discount of 50% for Belonger
interest, and a further discount of $500,000.
- on 29th November 2006, Cabinet agreed to the sale of 41.5 acres with
Belonger discount, and a 99 year lease of 238.72 acres of Crown Land
at an annual rent of $1 per acre;
- on 11th April 2007, on a Paper introduced by McH, Cabinet agreed to a
sale of 22.14 acres together with 100 acres at $50,000 per acre; the 100
acres was subsequently valued at $12.932m. by the Chief Valuator; the
following day, McH’s application for his Amex card was approved by
J&T Banka;
- on 20th August 2007, MHo wrote to MM, copied to McH seeking a 99
year lease of a further 637.15 acres of Crown Land;
- on 29th August 2008, on an Oral Mention raised by MM, Cabinet
approved the proposal;
- on 9th January 2008, on an Oral Mention raised by McH, Cabinet agreed
to an outright sale of 3.1 acres at an open market value of $160,000 per
acre; nearby parcels were sold for approximately $250,000.00 per acre.
518. Apart from the concessions requested, MHo also sought and was granted
exclusive rights to manage the port and airport for 20 years.
519. Mr. Lynch KC has submitted on behalf of McH that whatever monetary
benefits McH received were not gifts (which would require disclosure), but
loans from MM. Counsel invited the Court to draw this inference from the
136

evidence, submitting that McH was independently wealthy and did not need
to solicit or accept bribes, and in fact was constantly taking loans to finance
his investments, which he consistently repaid.
520. The Court is unable to draw the inference suggested by Counsel. There is
no evidence, oral or documentary from which the inference arises. The fact
that McH was independently wealthy does not negate the possibility that
McH was offered and accepted bribes from an investor with whom the
TCIG was engaged in active negotiations, and with whom McH was in
communication, informing MHo promptly of Cabinet decisions. In addition
it does not follow from the fact that McH regularly took loans to finance his
investments and lifestyle, that the monies received indirectly through TCM,
were in fact loans and not bribes intended by MHo to influence him to make
decisions that were advantageous to MHo.
521. From the evidence outlined above, and the forgoing discussions, the Court
draws the compelling and irresistible inference that the facilities extended
to McH (and MM) and the benefits and payments accruing to McH as a
result, were intended to induce McH to act in a way that was corrupt and
therefore contrary to his duty to act honestly and with integrity in the
performance of his duties as a Minister.
Question Four
522. This question involves the knowledge and intention of McH in receiving
the inducements to act corruptly. It raises the issues:
- Did McH know that the facility of the Amex Cards, and the payments
made for his benefit through TCM, were inducements from MHo to
influence him to act corruptly, and
- Did McH intend to receive the facility and payments as inducements or
rewards for him to act corruptly?
137

523. There can be little doubt that McH must have known of the participation
of MHo in the grant of the Amex Card to him, and the disbursements made
by TCM from the grant of the $6m loan to MM and LRM. The likelihood
of McH applying to a bank thousands of miles away in Slovakia for a
premier credit card and being granted such a facility without having any
previous banking relationship with J&T Banka, is extremely remote. There
is simply no evidence in the case of McH having any previous relationship
with J&T Banka. The evidence is that MHo, through Istrokapital, had an
undoubtedly favourable relationship with J&T, so much so that J&T agreed
to lend him $36m to finance his Salt Cay investment. On the evidence, the
only plausible link between McH and J&T Banka, was MHo, with whom
McH was in communication. The grant of the Amex Card to McH was
approved by J&T Banka on 12th April 2007, just a day after McH had
presented a paper to Cabinet for the sale of 22.14 acres, and a further 100
acres of Crown Land at $50,000.00 per acre at a gross undervalue. McH
had communicated the Cabinet decision on the same day it was made by
letter to MHo.
524. The disbursements made by TCM for McH’s benefit on 15th May 2007,
the day after the $6m loan to MM and LRM was disbursed to Chalmers
Misick & Co., are not denied by McH. Mr. Lynch KC suggests that the
disbursements, though broken into separate transactions, were in fact part
of a loan from MM to McH. The $6m loan from J&T Banka to MM and
LRM, the Court has found, was orchestrated by MHo to pass corrupt
payments to MM to influence him in facilitating grants of Crown Land and
favourable concessions to MHo and his related companies. A clear and
compelling inference arises from the disbursements to McH from the $6m,
that the loan was intended to provide financial inducements to McH as well,
who as Minister of Natural Resources was well positioned to facilitate
grants of Crown Land and concessions.
138

525. Clearly, McH was well aware of the receipt of the funds by TCM, and
must have provided detailed instructions to TCM to disburse the funds to
specified payees in specified amounts.
526. The issue is whether McH received the benefit of the Amex Cards for
himself and his wife, as well as the payments, intentionally knowing that
they were corrupt inducements by MHo intended to influence him to act in
a way that was contrary to his obligations of honesty and integrity as a
Minister.
527. To address this issue the court must consider the conduct of McH with
respect to MHo and the Salt Cay Project. The conduct of McH displays a
high level of enthusiasm to facilitate MHo and to accelerate Cabinet
consideration of the project, and implementation of Cabinet decisions.
Examples of this enthusiasm are:
- McH’s recommendation to Cabinet to sell 100 acres of Crown Land to
MHo, at an undervalue, without the benefit of a current valuation;
- McH was a referee for MHo’s application for Belongership status,
impliedly attesting that he had known MHo for at least 3 years, while
the evidence is that on the date of the application, McH would not have
known MHo for 3 years;
- McH’s sharp rebuke of Glenda Clarke, who pointed out that at the time
Cabinet approved a Belonger discount of 50% for the sale to MHo of
41.5 acres of Crown Land on 23rd August 2006, MHo did not have
Belongership status, which was granted on 17th November 2006;
- On 6th June 2007, 5 days after he received a proposal from Stefan Kral
to build a port on Salt Cay and offset the cost from the purchase price
of 122 acres, McH raised the proposal by Oral Mention at Cabinet, and
secured the approval of Cabinet for the proposal together with
139

concessions for cargo dues, dock dues, and other duties to be suspended
during construction;
- On 8th June 2007, McH wrote to the Directors of Salt Cay Resort and
Spa, advising of the Cabinet decision;
- On 9th January 2008, McH raised by Oral Mention the request by
representatives of Salt Cay Resort and Spa to purchase 3.1 acres, at an
open market value of $51,612.90 per acre, about 20% of the price at
which nearby plots had been recently sold, and without a valuation;
McH further represented that the land was free from encumbrances,
despite an existing lease in favor of Earl Ingham; Cabinet approved the
sale; and
- McH wrote to Stefan Kral on the same day advising him of the decision.
528. Perhaps the most egregious example of McH’s enthusiasm in furthering
MHo’s interests is to be found in the controversy surrounding the refusal of
planning permission for the port barge dock. Clyde Robinson (CR) the
Director of Planning recommended to the Physical Planning Board (PPB)
that planning permission should be refused on the basis that the location had
been shifted and the new location did not take into consideration the
environmental impact on one of the most important historical sites in the
TCI.
529. On 17th December 2008 the PPB accepted CR’s recommendation, the
Chairman expressing concern about the arrogance of wanting to place an
industrial dock in the middle of the town without regard to Salt Cay’s
heritage. The outcome of the meeting was communicated to McH the same
day.
530. On 9th January 2009, CR was invited to a meeting by McH at the office of
the Premier. Some members of the PPB were present as well as Stefan Kral,
MM, and McH. The meeting became heated, with CR and McH becoming
140

involved in a confrontation at which McH obscenely abused CR. The PPB
convened an extraordinary meeting the same evening, and reversed its
decision.
531. From the evidence it appears to be undisputed that the public service was
inefficient, and their slowness to act and make decisions promptly, resulted
in bureaucratic delays. It was submitted that the lethargy of the public
servants posed a risk to the TCI receiving much needed investment. Due to
the glacial approach of the public service, the Ministers had to take a hands-
on approach and deal directly with investors, in order to accelerate the pace
of much needed investment in the TCI.
532. The Court acknowledges the importance of inward investment in the TCI,
especially in a remote and underdeveloped island such as Salt Cay. One
can easily understand the enthusiasm of the Premier and McH in doing what
they considered necessary to attract and facilitate substantial investment in
the Salt Cay.
533. Mr. Lynch KC submits that CR is a prime example of a public servant who
was being obstructive, and was guilty of undermining the planning process.
On the evidence before this Court, CR was merely doing his job and carried
out his duties with professionalism in the interest of the people of TCI. The
evidence is clear that the location of the port barge had been shifted from
the location agreed in the DA, and was likely to negatively impact an
important historical site. There is no evidence of mala fides on the part of
CR in making his recommendation to the PPB to refuse planning
permission. There was also no justification for the abuse he received at the
meeting at MM’s office. In fact, the heated discussions in which MM and
McH engaged, and the inexplicable presence of Stefan Kral, point to an
unhealthy involvement of MM and McH with MHo and his associates. To
be clear, this Court makes no finding as to the correctness of the decision to
141

refuse or grant planning permission. The relevance of the issue lies in the
conduct of McH (and MM) and the lengths they were prepared to go to
accommodate requests made by MHo.
534. Mr. Lynch KC further submitted that the Governor (RT) arrived on TCI
with an agenda. This agenda was to obtain enough material to support
initiating a Commission of Inquiry. RT could have stopped some of the
enterprises, but he chose not to do so in order to obtain evidence. The
intention was to entrap Ministers into making bad decisions to satisfy his
agenda to find a way to order a COI.
535. The obvious flaw in the submission is the premise on which the FCO and
RT would have an agenda to entrap Ministers into wrongdoing and so
provide a basis for establishing a COI. It is not clear why the FCO or RT,
would embark on a campaign to entrap Ministers into wrongdoing. There
is no evidence before this court that RT or the FCO was aware that McH (or
MM) were receiving money and benefits from MHo. If they had this
evidence, the obvious course of action would be to involve the police and
the DPP. What did the FCO or RT have to gain by entrapping Ministers in
wrongdoing? There is absolutely no evidence of any such action on the part
of the FCO or RT. There is no evidence whatsoever that RT or the FCO
were aware of the receipt of benefits by McH (and MM) from MHo, or any
evidence whatsoever that RT or the FCO facilitated the receipt of these
benefits. The Court finds no merit whatsoever in the submission.
536. Mr. Lynch KC further submits that the concessions granted to MHo were
within the limits of the Encouragement of Development Ordinance, and fell
short of those requested by MHo or his subordinates. This is undoubtedly
true. While generous, as attested to by RT and RBK, the concessions were
within the statutory limits.
142

537. It does not follow from this that the payments and benefits received by
McH, through MHo, were not bribes intended to influence him in the
performance of his duties as Minister. Whether or not the concessions could
have been even more generous, there is no doubt that they were in fact
generous, as confirmed by the evidence of RT and RBK, which the Court
accepts.
538. As already indicated, the Court has found that the grant of Amex Centurion
Cards to McH and his wife was clearly orchestrated by MHo, as was the
$6m loan to MM and LRM, out of which disbursements amounting to close
to $1m were made by TCM on the instructions of McH. These payments
were not part of a loan by MM to McH as suggested by Counsel. They were
clearly payments to McH, by MHo, to influence him or reward him for
generous discounts on the purchase price of Crown Lands, and generous
concessions. These payments made by TCM for McH’s benefit should have
been disclosed by McH in his annual Declaration to the Registrar of
Interests. The non-disclosure of such large payments must have been
deliberate, supporting the inference that they were corrupt payments.
539. From the evidence and the discussion above, this Court finds beyond
reasonable doubt that McH knowingly and intentionally received these
benefits and monies as inducements for him to act corruptly and therefore
contrary to his obligations of honesty and integrity as a Minister. The Court
therefore finds McH guilty as charged on the Second Count.
540. In coming to this conclusion the Court expressly takes into account the
propensity limb of the Vye direction, that is it is less likely that a man of
good character such as McH would commit the offence of bribery, having
maintained a clean record as a man of mature years.
COUNT 3
143

541. Count 3 involves both MM and McH. They are charged that between 1st
November 2006 and 1st May 2008, they accepted inducements directly or
indirectly from the Logwood Development Company Ltd (LDCL) and
connected entities (including Harold Charles (HC), Alan Lisenby, Matt Mc
Donald and Scott Boatman) by unlawful corrupt payments or other rewards
in the form of cash, credit, entertainment and other advantages), so that they
would act in a way that was contrary to the ordinary rules of honesty and
integrity expected of Ministers of the Crown.
The Prosecution Case – Facts
542. The island of West Caicos is 11 square miles and has been uninhabited for
over a century.
543. LDCL was incorporated on 22nd December 1997 by Caribbean
Management Services Ltd. Among its Directors were Mako Investments
Ltd. from 9th November 1999, Alan Lisenby from 22nd December 2010,
David Hartshorn from 16th December 1998, Desmond Quant and Douglas
Missick and Earl Ingham from the same date, R Matt Mc Donald from 22nd
December 2010 and W. Scott Boatman from 22nd December 2010. All of
these directors were shareholders with the exception of Matt Mc Donald,
Scott Boatman and Alan Lisenby. Southhaven Limited was a shareholder
from 23rd December 1997 to 16th December 1998. The directors and
shareholders were at various times associated with the Logberg Group and
Southhaven Ltd.
544. On 27th November 2001 a Development Agreement (DA) between TCIG
and LDCL was signed.
545. Logwood Hotel Development Co. Ltd (LHD) was incorporated in TCI on
15th April 2003 by Caribbean Management Services Ltd. Its Secretary was
Southaven Ltd. Its Directors included at various stages Caribbean
144

Management Services Limited, Alan Lisenby, David Hartshorn, Earl
Ingham, Jonathan Siegel, Joseph Flannery, Karim Alibhai, Matt Mc Donald
and Mako Investments Ltd.
546. Mako Investments Ltd was registered on 4th February 1999 by Logberg
Corporate Services Ltd. Its sole Director was Logberg Directors Ltd, and
the Secretary was Logberg Secretaries Ltd. The sole shareholder was
Logberg Management Ltd.
547. The shareholders in LHD from 2nd July 2005 were Genib-Caicos Limited
Partnership as to 1.5m shares, Westside Development Ltd as to 7.5m shares
and LHDC Holding as to 1.5m shares. The company was struck off the
Register on 25th September 2013.
548. On 22nd April 2003 a DA was signed between the Crown, the TCIG, LHD
and LDCL.
549. West Harbour Development Co. Ltd. (WHD) was incorporated on 13th
June 2003. Its Secretary was Southaven Limited. Its Directors included
Mako Investments Ltd, Alan Lisenby, David Hartshorn, Earl Ingham,
Jonathan Siegel and R Matt Mc Donald. Its shareholders were LDCL and
LHDC Holdings Ltd.
550. Harold Charles (HC) received payments from LHD and LDCL, including
a monthly payment of $5,000.00 as “consultancy” fees.
551. On 20th June 2005 Chalmers Management Ltd was incorporated. Its
Directors were Caribbean Management Services Ltd for one day, TCM and
Tarsha Hall. Its Secretary was Southaven Ltd (for one day), and Joyanna
Misick (TCM’s wife) from 21st June 2005 to 21st June 2011, when she was
145

replaced by Tarsha Hall. Its shareholders were TCM, Joyanna Misick and
Chalmers Management Ltd.
552. Windsor East Ltd. was incorporated on 20th June 2005. Caribbean
Management Services Ltd, and Southaven Ltd were respectively Director
and Secretary for one day – 21st June 2005. TCM and his wife Joyanna
were Directors, Joyanna also being Secretary from 21st June 2005 until 6th

2008. Southaven Ltd held 1 share on 21st June 2005, for one day. TCM
held 1 share, while his wife Joyanna held 2 shares. The company was struck

553. West View Development Ltd. (West View) was incorporated on 23rd
November 2007, by Chalmers Management Ltd. Its Directors were
Chalmers Management Ltd. and Windsor East, which was also Secretary
and sole shareholder.
554. International Transfer Company Ltd (ITC) was incorporated on 27th
September 2005. Harold Charles (HC) and Greg Aaron Marshall were
Directors until 6th April 2010, when they were replaced. Marshall was also
Secretary up to 6th April 2010. Harold Charles, Mallory McComish and
Henry Mensen were shareholders up to 6th April 2010.
555. At an ExCo meeting on 1st March 2006, on a Paper presented by the
Deputy Chief Minister on behalf of MM, ExCo approved in principle,
subject to a valuation being done, the transfer of an additional 29.52 acres
of Crown Land to LDCL on payment of the freehold purchase price as
prescribed in the DA.
556. On 5th July 2006, $100,000 was paid by cheque drawn on MM’s BB
account to McH’s FCIB account.
146

557. On 16th October 2006, Norman Saunders Jnr. applied to FCIB for an
account to be opened in the name of “South Caicos 2007 PNP Campaign”.
The account was opened on 20th October 2006. The sole signatory on the
account was McH.
558. The South Caicos account received the following deposits:
- $83,000 from HC by cheque dated 20.11.06;
- $83,000 from HC by cheque dated 30.11.06;
- $1,000 from Norman Saunders by cheque dated 15.12.06;
- $75,000 from Caicos Management and Development Ltd (CMDL) by
cheque dated 19.12.06;
- $75,000 from CMDL by another cheque dated 19.12.06;

559. The South Caicos FCIB account had the following debits:

- $10,000 cheque dated 18.01.07 deposited to McH’s FCIB account the
same day;
- $60,000 cheque dated 10.01.07 deposited to McH’s FCIB account on
09.01.07;
- $12,000 by cheque dated 18.01.07 in favour of Raymond Monpremier;
- $5,000 by cheque dated 19.01.07 to Emily Saunders;

- $10,000 by cheque dated 05.02.07 to Raymond Monpremier;

- $17,000 cash withdrawal on 14.02.07;
147

- $7,000 by cheque dated 23.03.07 to Raymond Monpremier, and
- $6,000 by cheque dated 23.03.07 to Raymond Monpremier.
560. On 26th October 2006 Cabinet approved the mutation of parcel 70100/44
on West Caicos to create three parcels of 15.0, 3.0 and 1.5 acres, on a paper
presented by McH as Minister of Natural Resources. On the same day,
Cabinet approved the freehold transfer of 29.52 acres of Crown Land to
LDCL, on an Oral Mention presented by MM.
561. On 1st November 2006 on a Paper introduced by McH, Cabinet approved
the mutation of Parcel 70100/44 to create a parcel of 25.2 acres, and granted
approval for the sale of the 25.2 acres to LDCL for the construction of an
industrial roro ramp and facilities for tourist related activities, at $30,000
per acre in accordance with the DA.
562. On 17th November 2006, LHD made a payment of $250,000 to Harold

- $83,000 by cheque dated 20.11.06 to Chalmers & Co., and
- $83,000 by cheque dated 20.11.06 to “PNP South Caicos Campaign
2007”.
563. On 21st November 2006, Chalmers & Co. paid $31,025.53 (plus $95 wire
transfer fee) to J&T Banka towards MM’s Amex Card.
564. On 24th November 2006 LHD made a further payment of $250,000 into
HC’s FCIB account.
565. By cheque dated 30th November 2006, HC paid $33,000 to Belize Bank,
which was credited to Chalmers & Co.’s account the following day. A
cheque dated the same day in the sum of $83,000, drawn by HC was
credited to the PNP South Caicos account on 1st December 2006.
148
