The Case Against Michael Misick on Count 3
612. MM is charged with the offence of bribery. The Crown alleges that
between 1st November 2006 and 1st May 2008, MM accepted inducements
directly or indirectly from the Logwood Development Company Ltd. and
related and connected entities (including Harold Charles, Alan Lisenby,
Matt Mc Donald and Scott Boatman) by unlawful corrupt payments or other
rewards, while serving as a Minister of the Crown.
613. The facts on which the Crown lies, have been set out in some detail, and
repetition of them will be avoided. The Crown has led evidence in support
of these facts, which has not been controverted by evidence given by MM
or any witness called on his behalf. As he is entitled to do MM has elected
not to give evidence, or to call witnesses. The Court draws no adverse
157

inferences from MM’s election, when called upon at the close of the
Crown’s case.
614. MM has stood upon his right to require the Crown to satisfy its burden to
prove its case beyond reasonable doubt as he is entitled to do. Through
Counsel, MM has sought to highlight what he perceives to be weaknesses
in the Crown’s case, and has through his Counsel’s submissions, drawn
attention to circumstances and inferences, which it is submitted, weaken or
destroy the prosecution case.
Defence Submissions and Crown’s Response
615. Mr. Peterson SC has submitted that the “Letter Agreement” signed by HC
and Alan Lisenby on 28th March 2007, was an “apparent agreement”
between two persons who are not before the Court, and who have not given
evidence. Further there is no evidence that the document was ever seen by
MM, or that he was aware of its existence or contents. On its face, the
document is an apparent agreement between the two signatories, which has
no evidential value in relation to MM’s knowledge of the arrangement. The
same submissions are repeated in relation to the later Letter Agreement of
25th January 2008.
616. The Crown took a different view of the transaction. It is submitted that
the letter agreement was no more than a device created by HC and
Logwood, and/or its related companies to raise a loan from Belize Bank to
pay ITC for a non-existent interest in the land, $2m of which were
distributed for the benefit of MM (and McH).
617. Mr. Peterson SC concedes (at paragraph 245) of his closing submissions
that the tribunal of fact may “reasonably and properly” infer from the West
Caicos Chart evidence, that HC made a number of payments to TCM, some
of which were applied for MM’s direct or indirect benefit. However, Senior
158

Counsel argues that the evidence does not permit a properly directed
tribunal of fact to infer beyond reasonable doubt that MM knew that this
money originated from the West Caicos Developers.
618. Mr. Peterson SC further submits that a properly directed tribunal of fact
cannot infer that any of the HC payments to TCM which were applied for
MM’s benefit were bribes or intended as such by HC, and were knowingly
and intentionally received as such by MM. The issue of MM’s knowledge
and intent in receiving the payments are expressed to be a critical issue for
MM’s defence.
619. Mr. Peterson SC further argues in relation to a payment of $83,000 to
Chalmers & Co, that there is no evidence from which a properly directed
tribunal of fact could infer so that he is sure that MM knew that the money
received from HC had originated from LHD as opposed to HC himself.
620. The Crown responds that there is simply no evidence of any commercial
relationship between MM and HC to sustain any suggestion that MM might
have believed he had received $83,000 from HC personally. The Crown
points out that the $83,000 was the first of several payments from HC that
lack any commercial logic, rationale or explanation. Further, the Crown
argues that it is inconceivable that MM could have received the funds
without any knowledge as to their source. In fact, the Crown suggests that
the $83,000 appears to be a three way split of the $250,000 emanating from
LHD, between MM, McH, and HC as “middleman”.
621. With respect to the payment of $1m from Henry Mensen to HC on 14th
November 2007, Mr. Peterson SC submits that the fact finder is being
invited to speculate or guess as to the reason for the payment and its
subsequent transfer from HC to the Chalmers & Co BB account from which
MM concedes that $300,000 was applied to his Amex Card. However, Mr.
159

Peterson SC argues that the transfer to MM’s Amex Card was met from
TCM’s overdraft facility, and this was “essentially a banking arrangement
between TCM and Belize Bank, about which the tribunal of fact has heard
no evidence. Again, it is submitted that the fact finder is being asked to
speculate without evidence that can support reasonable inferences.
622. With reference to Chart 8, which shows the distribution of the $1m
received from Henry Mensen on 14th November 2007, Mr. Peterson SC
poses the questions:
(i) where is the evidence that any of the payments in transactions 1 to
5, emanated from any of the parties to the Logwood Development
Agreements (DAs) at this point in time, and
(ii) even if there is such evidence, where is the evidence that MM knew
any of these payments had come from any of the parties to the DAs,
rather than from HC himself?
623. Mr. Peterson SC further points out that, while Matt Mc Donald was a
director of HT Ltd. in April 2007, which made payments to HC in December
2007 and January 2008, HT Ltd was not a party to the Logwood DA, nor
was Matt Mc Donald a company officer or shareholder in any of the West
Caicos development companies in November 2007 when Henry Mensen
“advanced” $1m to HC. However, Mc Donald had been a director of
Logwood Hotel Development Co. Ltd. (LHD) from 1st October 2004 to 30th
June 2005. Matt Mc Donald was also clearly associated with Westco
Development Group Ltd, evidenced by the fact that he signed a loan
agreement for $3.1m with Belize Bank in March 2008, as a Director of
Westco.
624. In respect of the payment of $500,000 from Skyking to Chalmers & Co on
31st December 2007, and the subsequent disbursements (one of them a
160

payment of $500,000 to MM’s Amex Card), Mr. Peterson SC poses
questions:
(i) how could a properly directed tribunal of fact be sure that the
$500,000 transfer to MM’s Amex Card was met from the $500,000
payment from Skyking to Chalmers & Co., and
(ii) how can a properly directed tribunal of fact be sure that MM knew
that HC’s payment made from his own company Skyking, was in
any way connected to the West Caicos developers? Senior Counsel
submits that the fact finder is being invited to engage in speculation.
625. Senior Counsel further submits that the ITC cheque for $700,000 dated
10th April 2008 paid into the Chalmers & Co FCIB account became
comingled with other funds in the account. In addition, cheques for
$100,000 and $200,000 were drawn on the FCIB account and paid into
Chalmers & Co Belize Bank account, before the ITC cheque was deposited
into the FCIB account. The $300,000 paid by the 2 cheques into the BB
account were subsequently used to pay disbursements benefitting MM (and
McH). Senior Counsel poses the question: How can the Crown prove that
the $300,000 disbursements by TCM (shown in Charts 12 and 13) were part
of the ITC payment?
626. Mr. Peterson SC summarises his arguments on the disbursements by TCM,
by posing the question: how could a properly directed tribunal of fact
conclude on the evidence so as to be sure, that MM knew that the
disbursements (shown on Charts 12 and 13), were being met from proceeds
of Westco’s Belize Bank loan, as opposed to monies from HC himself,
and/or other funds belonging to MM which were being held, managed and
disbursed by TCM on his behalf?
627. In response to these submissions, the Crown contended:
161

- The Crown does not invite speculation nor does it seek any inference to
be drawn from the $1m payment from Henry Mensen to HC. Its only
relevance is that it enabled HC to make an almost immediate transfer of
$1m to Chalmers & Co;
- with respect to MM’s submission that there is no evidence that the
payments emanated from any of the parties to the Logwood DAs, the
Crown pointed to the evidence of the forensic financial investigator Paul
Farrell, that HT Ltd was “under the Logwood umbrella….part of the
development companies on West Caicos”, and so Matt Mc Donald did
have a close connection to the Logwood development companies as a
director of HT Ltd, whose role was to pass money to HC for the ultimate
benefit of MM (and McH);
- in response to MM’s submission that the $300,000 was applied to MM’s
Amex card from the overdraft facility on the Chalmers & Co account
pursuant to a banking arrangement between TCM and Belize Bank, on
which the court had heard no evidence, and so the court was being
drawn into speculation and guesswork, the Crown responded that
payments made to MM (and McH) dramatically increased the overdraft
which was only cleared by the $1m cheque from HC, which TCM
deposited the day before;
- in response to the questions posed by MM with respect to the $500,000
payment to MM’s Amex card, and MM’s knowledge of the source of
the payment, the Crown responds that the question suggests that MM, a
politician on a relatively modest salary, repeatedly benefited from
substantial sums of money without any knowledge as to their source or
purpose. The Crown submits that it is a matter of inference, not
speculation, that in the absence of any honest commercial explanation,
MM was aware that the funds derived from the West Caicos developers;
- the Crown posed its own question: what non-corrupt purpose did HC
have for making payments to MM (or McH)? None was suggested, and
162

the undisputed evidence was that HC received the money from sources
connected to the West Caicos Developers;
- It is inconceivable that MM received over $2m unaware as to its source.
There is no evidence of what MM believed, or that the funds were
received from HC personally, or alternatively belonged to MM, and
were managed and disbursed on his behalf by TCM, in the absence of
any evidence of a lawyer/client relationship between TCM and MM.
628. With respect to the donations made by LHD to the PNP, Mr. Peterson SC.
submitted:
(i) That the prosecution has adduced no evidence to prove that any of
this money was subsequently paid to MM or applied for his benefit,
and
(ii) A properly directed tribunal of fact could not discount or reject
completely out of hand the inference that the payment of $100,000
made on 29th March 2007 by LHD, directly to the PNP bank account
was a political donation made shortly after the February 2007
elections.
629. Mr. Peterson SC made the following submissions with respect to the
purchase of 1.3 acres of land by Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd:
(i) a properly directed tribunal of fact cannot safely conclude on the
evidence that MM knew HC had borrowed $330,000 from HT Ltd
to purchase the land;
(ii) there is no evidence of the issue of a share certificate to MM with
respect to Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd; the FSC search document in
fact reveals that Windsor East Ltd became the shareholder;
(iii) there is no evidence that MM became a director;
(iv) there is no evidence that MM became a shareholder by virtue of any
secret deed of trust, and so became beneficial owner of the land, and
163

(v) there is no evidence that the land purchase was funded by any of the
Logwood developers.
630. The Crown submits that the unregistered resolutions signed by HC, clearly
show his intention that the shareholding in Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd,
should be transferred to MM. This course was abandoned since the
registration of MM as shareholder would raise questions about the
transaction. In the Crown’s submission, TCM used his corporate entities to
conceal or disguise MM’s ownership.
Discussion
631. Count 3 of the indictment charges MM with the offence of bribery, in that
he corruptly accepted inducements from the developers of the West Caicos
project.
632. The facts have been set out in detail, and the Court will do its best to avoid
repetition. No evidence has been led to controvert that led by the
prosecution. The facts have not been disputed by the defendant, although
submissions have been made on his behalf, which seek to invite the Court
to refuse to make inferences from the evidence that the Crown has invited
the Court to make.
633. The parties have helpfully agreed legal directions, which the Court adopts
and will apply. The legal directions are as set out earlier in this judgment.
634. The questions set out in the agreed route to verdict will form the basis of
the discussion.
Question One
635. The first question is: Did Harold Charles and/or related entities, directly
or indirectly make payments to MM?
164

636. The fact of the making of the payments and their receipt, has not been
disputed. Questions were raised by MM, through his lead counsel as to
MM’s knowledge of the source or origin of the payments, but the fact of the
payments and their receipt into the accounts of Chalmers & Co and their
disbursement has not been disputed. The issue of the knowledge of MM is
a matter which will be better examined in relation to question 4.
Question Two
637. The second question posed is whether MM was a Minister of the Crown
when he directly or indirectly received payments. There is no issue that he
was.
Question Three
638. The third question relates to the intention of Harold Charles and/or the
related entities in making the payments. Were the payments made in order
to induce MM to act in a way that was corrupt?
639. The Crown alleges that the payments made by HC to TCM, for the benefit
of MM amounted to $2,706,000. The payments made to the PNP amount
to $225,000. The Crown further alleges that the parcel of land acquired by
Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd, through the agency of HC, was also intended
to benefit MM.
640. Mr. Peterson SC has submitted that the payments to the PNP were political
donations which were received directly into the PNP bank accounts, and
from which there is no evidence that MM benefited directly. The Court
notes that the donations were by no means insubstantial. It does not follow
from the labelling of the payments as “political donations” that the
payments were not intended to influence MM, the political leader of the
165

party, from acting in a way that was corrupt and to the benefit of the
developers.
641. With respect to the parcel of land acquired by Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd,
Mr. Peterson SC has relied heavily on the fact that the resolutions signed by
HC on 22nd July 2007, having been the sole director and shareholder for one
day, were not registered and the official record of the Financial Services
Commission does not reflect any connection with MM as director or
shareholder. While this is undoubtedly correct, the Court must draw the
appropriate inferences from the undisputed facts.
642. Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd. was incorporated on 20th July 2007. The sole
director and shareholder on 21st July 2007 was HC. HC signed resolutions
on 22nd July 2007, purporting to appoint MM as director, and transferring
his share to MM. HC ceased to be director on 22nd July 2007, and was
replaced by Chalmers Management Ltd and Windsor East Ltd, also a TCM
entity. Windsor East Ltd also became the sole shareholder on the same day.
643. While it is apparent that the resolutions were not registered, and the share
transfer from HC to MM was not effected, the question remains for the
Court to determine what reasonable inferences are to be drawn from the
undisputed fact of the existence of the resolutions. What are the inferences
to be drawn as to the intention of HC in signing the resolutions, after only
one day of HC being sole director and shareholder and two days after the
company was incorporated?
644. The evidence clearly establishes that HC was paid as a “consultant” by
Logberg Corporation and was used by the developers to distribute money
through Chalmers & Co to MM (and McH). Leeward Highway 2008 Ltd.
was undoubtedly created to hold the legal title of the parcel of land which
was adjoining the Lisa Raye McCoy Cinema Complex on the Leeward
166

Highway. The signed resolutions dated 22nd July 2007, though unregistered
without doubt, evince an intention to make MM sole director and
shareholder of Leeward Highways 2008 Ltd., and so becoming in effect the
beneficial owner of the parcel of land. The Court is unable to find any other
inference from the undisputed facts, which is inconsistent with the Crown’s
case.
645. The issue arises: why were the resolutions signed by HC not registered,
and why was a share transfer from HC to MM not executed? The answer is
quite obvious. If the resolutions were put into effect, the title to the land
could be easily traced to MM as sole shareholder, and further investigation
would reveal the agency of HC and his connection with the Logberg
developers.
646. The Court returns to the issue raised by Question Three. Were the
payments intended to induce MM to act in a corrupt manner?
647. The evidence shows that as the project expanded the developers were
requesting more land, so much so that on 31st May 2006, Clyde Robinson
(CR) the Director of Planning expressed his concern in an email to McH
that the Crown Land already issued to LDCL formed a ring around the entire
island of West Caicos, in spite of provision in the Development Plan for
land to be set aside as Crown Land Reserve. CR recommended to McH that
the Planning Department should begin planning of these lands to make them
available to Belongers over a specified period to prevent West Caicos from
becoming a private island. CR received a sharp response from McH, who
expressed concerns at his “written assumptions of the government
intentions” and reminded him of “government prerogative”.
167

648. The Crown further relied on the evidence of CR that the usual processes
for applications of planning permission were not followed and that the
developers were allowed to choose the approach they favoured.
649. The tribunal of fact must consider the circumstances that are relevant to
the question of the intention of the developers. These include:
- the size of the payments made through HC;
- the timing of the payments, in the context of requests made, papers and
oral mentions presented to Cabinet, and decisions made;
- the indirect and sometimes disguised manner in which the payments
were made through HC to TCM;
- the disbursement of the payments upon receipt, and the speed at which
the payments were disbursed;
- the apparent willingness to accommodate requests made by the
developers for the drawdown of parcels of Crown Land and mutations
of parcels in line with requests made by developers, and
- the use of the “Letter Agreements” to create an artificial interest in the
land in favour of ITC so as to provide a legal basis to pass funds to ITC
for distribution through HC to TCM, and
- the creation of Leeward Highways 2008 Ltd to acquire the parcel of land
for the benefit of MM.
650. The co-existence of these circumstances and their combined effect lead the
Court to the inexorable and compelling conclusion beyond reasonable doubt
that HC and/or related entities, intended to induce MM to act in a way that
was corrupt and contrary to his obligations of honesty and integrity as a
Minister of the Crown.
Question Four
651. The remaining question is whether the payments were knowingly and
intentionally received by MM as an inducement for him to act corruptly.
168

652. MM has submitted through Senior Counsel that:
(i) there is no evidence that he knew of the Letter Agreement of 28th
March 2007, between ITC and West Harbour Development
Company Ltd. (WHDC), or the further Letter Agreement dated 28th
January 2008 between ITC and Matt Mc Donald on behalf of
Westco Development Group Ltd. (Westco);
(ii) there is no evidence from which the tribunal of fact could safely and
reasonably infer that MM knew that the funds HC paid to TCM, had
come from persons or entities associated with the West Caicos
developers, as opposed to HC himself;
(iii) a properly directed fact finder cannot conclude on the evidence so
as to be sure that MM knew that the disbursements shown on Charts
12 and 13, were being met from proceeds of a loan by Westco from
Belize bank.
653. The issue as to MM’s knowledge of the Letter Agreements, or the BB loan
to Westco are irrelevant to the issue of his knowledge as to whether the
payments received by TCM through HC, were corrupt payments,
knowingly received by him as such. How the monies were raised by the
Logberg developers for transmission through HC to TCM, or MM’s
knowledge of the existence and content of the Letter Agreements, is not
relevant to the issue as to MM’s knowledge or intention in receiving the
monies.
654. As to the issue as to whether the fact finder can infer that MM knew that
the payments originated from the developers as opposed to HC himself, the
Crown points out that there is simply no evidence of any commercial
relationship between MM and HC so as to sustain any suggestion that MM
might have believed he had received $83,000 or any funds from HC
personally.
169

655. The Crown submits that the payments from HC lack any commercial logic,
rationale or explanation, and it is inconceivable that MM could have
received these funds without any knowledge as to their source.
656. In deciding the issue as to MM’s knowledge and intention in receiving the
monies, the Court considers the following circumstances:
- MM as head of Government, was actively engaged in negotiations with
the West Caicos developers;
- there is no evidence of any financial transactions or relationship
between MM and HC;
- there is evidence of an eagerness to accommodate requests of the
developers and a willingness to be flexible in accommodating requests
in relation to planning issues;
- from funds received from HC, TCM was able to disburse funds to make
payments for specific payees for specific amounts to specific accounts;
- these payments could not be made without specific instructions from
MM;
- the disbursements were made immediately upon receipt of funds from
HC by TCM, or shortly thereafter; and
- the obvious inference is that MM was aware of the receipt of the funds
and gave detailed instructions for their disbursement.
657. The Court concludes that the compelling and inevitable inference to be
drawn from all the circumstances of the case, is that MM was fully aware
of the payments, and the source of the payments, and received the payments
intentionally as inducements for him to act corruptly and therefore contrary
to his obligations of honesty and integrity as a Minister of the Crown. The
Court makes this finding beyond reasonable doubt, expressly taking into
account the previous good character of MM. Accordingly, the Court finds
MM guilty on the Third Count.
170

The Case Against Mc Callister Eugene Hanchell on Count 3
658. McH is charged on the third count, together with MM, that between 1st
November 2006 and 1st May 2008, he accepted inducements directly or
indirectly from the Logwood Development Company and related and
connected entities (including HC, Allan Lisenby, Matt Mc Donald, and
Scott Boatman) by unlawful corrupt payments or other rewards, contrary to
the ordinary rules of honesty and integrity expected of a Minister of the
Crown.
659. The evidence on which the Crown relies to prove the case against McH
has already been outlined. Care will be taken to avoid repetition, which is
unavoidable in some instances in view of the legal requirement to consider
McH’s case separately on each count.
660. The case for the Crown is that McH accepted payments from the Logberg
developers in the aggregate sum of $2,116,000. The thrust of the defence,
(which will be examined shortly) is that all the payments that were received
by McH were in fact loans, and not bribes.
661. The agreed law of bribery has been set out in some detail earlier in this
judgment and will be applied as before, to the case against McH on Count
Three.
662. McH, as he was entitled to, elected to give no evidence from the witness
box, or to call witnesses in his defence. On his behalf, Mr. Lynch KC has
made oral and written submissions on the evidence with a view to
persuading the court that the Crown has failed to prove the case against
McH to the criminal standard.
171

663. The Court acknowledges that Mr. Lynch’s KC general submissions with
respect to the Governor’s agenda, the inefficiency of the public service, the
use of oral mentions, concessions and Crown Land policies, which were set
out in the court’s consideration of McH’s case on the second count, are
intended to apply to the third count as well. These submissions are not
repeated here, but are considered by the Court in relation to Count Three.
The submissions of McH which relate specifically to Count Three will now
be set out.
664. In respect of the two cheques of $83,000 paid in November 2006 into the
South Caicos PNP Campaign 2007 account, Mr. Lynch KC submits that
there is no evidence to suggest that McH had any idea “where this money
came from other than HC, who was a “wealthy business man and friend”.
In other words, McH is saying through his Counsel that he did not know
that HC had received two transfers of $250,000 each from LHD in
November 2006.
665. The Crown responds that there is simply no evidence of any commercial
relationship between McH and HC, or any evidence that he was a friend of
McH. The Crown further pointed to the size of the donation ($166,000),
from one person for a very small constituency, and the fact that the first
cheque for $83,000 was deposited on the same day the account was opened
by McH, who was the sole signatory. The Crown further noted that McH
paid himself sums of $10,000 and $60,000, and paid $50,000 to a building
firm which was constructing his house, payments which on their face do not
appear to be related to election expenses. The Crown further argues that
given the circumstances of the opening of the account, the disbursements
for the benefit of McH and his sole control of the account, McH must have
been aware of the source of the funds.
172

666. Mr. Lynch KC further submits that there is no evidence that McH knew,
met or ever communicated with Henry Mensen (HM) or that he knew that
Mensen loaned HC $1m in November 2007. Further, there is no evidence
that McH knew Twa Marcellin and Wolf (Attorneys for HT Ltd. and
Logwood), and no evidence that McH knew that HT Ltd. loaned HC
$700,000 in 2007. It is conceded however that $1m was sent by HC to
Chalmers & Co, of which $400,000 was sent by Chalmers & Co to J&T
Banka two days later to pay expenditure on McH’s Amex Centurion Card.
Mr. Lynch KC poses the question: “In the absence of any allegation against
HM (and HC) why is this not simply a loan as opposed to the more sinister
connotation that it was a bribe for something to do with Logwood and West
Caicos?”
667. With respect to the payment of $2,510,000 to ITC, as depicted in Chart 10,
McH (through Mr. Lynch KC) denies knowledge of transactions above that
line, suggesting that he was not aware that the money came from the
proceeds of a loan of $3.1m obtained by Westco from Belize Bank. He,
however, appears to accept that he was aware of the payment of $1m by
ITC to Saunders & Co. on 10th April 2008, (the same day the cheque for
$2.51m was paid into ITC’s account at TCI Bank).
668. Mr. Lynch submits that to prove the offence, the Crown must show the
“Bribee” knew the origin of the funds and that it was being paid for the
purpose of influencing his actions. Since there is no direct evidence of that
fact, it requires “an irresistible inference” to be made on the evidence that
McH knew the source. Mr. Lynch KC submits that there is no such
evidence.
669. Mr. Lynch KC poses the questions: “If there was a need to bribe McH,
why use a conduit? Why involve another party at all? Just pay him
directly”.
173

670. The case for the Crown (as depicted in Chart 15) is that the sum of
$840,581.18 was applied (from the $1m payment received from ITC) to pay
off 4 loans that McH held with Saunders & Co. It is not disputed that McH
borrowed regularly from Saunders & Co. My Lynch KC contends that the
figure of $840,581.13 is wrong, and that the correct figure is just shy of
$985,000. The Crown argues that Mr. Lynch KC is wrong. Nothing
significant turns on the exact figure. Both parties appear to agree that the
$1m payment from ITC was used to liquidate loans held by McH at
Saunders & Co.
671. With respect to the payment of $125,000 on 17th April 2008 by Chalmers
& Co. to J&T Banka account in the name of McH (which was paid out of
the $700,000 received by Chalmers & Co. from ITC, being part of the
$2.51m received from ITC), Mr. Lynch contends that the sum of $125,000
must be wrong since the Amex payment for April 2008 was $76,353.
672. In response, the Crown points out that the payments were made into an
account at J&T Banka, which guaranteed the Amex Card. From this
account funds were applied by J&T towards payment for expenditure on the
card. The sum of $125,000 was paid out of the $700,000 received by
Chalmers & Co., to McH’s account at J&T and subsequently applied to
expenditure on the card. Whatever the Amex bill was for the month of April
2008, the payment of $125,000 was for McH’s benefit.
Discussion
673. The discussion will be framed by reference to the 4 questions posed in the
agreed route to verdict.
Question One
174

674. The first question is a question of fact: Did HC and/or related entities
directly or indirectly make payments to McH.
675. The fact of the payments made through HC and related entities (notably
ITC) are not disputed. McH raises an issue as to the nature of the payments.
He contends that the payments were not bribes. They were in fact loans to
McH, presumably granted to McH by HC and ITC.
676. Whether or not the payments were loans is a question of fact. In deciding
the issue the court, as the tribunal of fact, considers the following matters:
- there is no oral evidence given from the witness box of any oral
agreement between McH and HC (or ITC) or between McH and MM,
for any loan;
- there is no oral evidence of any terms of agreement for the repayment
of any loan;
- there is no oral or documentary evidence of any repayment of any loan;
- there is no evidence of any written agreement setting out the amounts of
any loans, their disbursement and the terms for repayment including
dates for repayment and interest payments, including rates of interest to
be applied;
- there is no evidence of repayment of any loans from HC or ITC, by
McH;
- there is no evidence of any commercial arrangement or agreement
between HC or ITC, and McH;
- there is no evidence that McH and HC were friends or business
associates, who by virtue of a close business or personal relationship,
would enter into agreements for the advancement of large sums of
money, “on a handshake”.
175

677. In short, there is simply no evidence before this Court that supports the
suggestion of defence counsel, that the payments received by McH were
loans.
678. The court is unable to infer from the evidence that the payments made by
HC and ITC were in fact loans to McH. The Court finds beyond reasonable
doubt that the payments were not loans.
Question Two
679. The second question raised in the route to verdict is whether McH was a
Minister of the Crown at the time he received the payments. There is no
dispute that he was.
Question Three
680. The third question raises the issue of the intention of the developers in
making the payments to McH through HC and/or related entities. Were the
payments intended to induce McH to act in a way that was corrupt and
contrary to his obligation to act honestly and with integrity?
681. The Crown alleges that the developers made payments of $2,116,000 to
McH indirectly, through HC and/or related entities. Payments amounting
to $166,000 were also made to an account controlled by McH set up
ostensibly for McH’s 2007 election campaign.
682. As pointed out earlier in this judgment, there was a clear connection
between HC and the Logberg developers who hired him as a “consultant”
on a retainer of $5,000.00. The evidence also establishes that HC received
monies from entities controlled by the Logberg developers, which were then
distributed through HC and ITC to Chalmers & Co to be applied for the
benefit of McH (and MM). In McH’s case, ITC paid $1m directly to
Saunders & Co to assist in liquidating loans taken by McH.
176

683. The evidence further established that the Logberg developers were
requesting more Crown Land as the project expanded, so much so that
concerns were expressed by the Director of Planning that they were
threatening to make West Caicos a private island to the detriment of the
development plan which provided for distribution to Belongers. The
developers were also seeking to circumvent planning procedures, as
suggested by the evidence of CR.
684. In deciding the issue of the intention of the developers in making the
payments, through HC and/or related entities, the Court takes into account
the following circumstances:
- the size of the payments made through HC;
- the timing of the payments, in the context of requests made, and papers
and oral mentions presented to Cabinet, and decisions made;
- the disbursements of the payments upon receipt, and the speed at which
the disbursements were made;
- the apparent eagerness to accommodate requests made by developers
for drawdowns of parcels of Crown Land, and mutations of parcels;
- the use of the ‘Letter Agreements’ to create an artificial interest in the
land in favour of ITC, so as to provide a legal basis to pass funds to ITC
for distribution to TCM and Saunders & Co, for the benefit of McH, and
- the fact that McH was the Minister of Natural Resources, responsible
for the distribution of Crown Land, and the Department of Planning
Permission.
685. The fact of the payments, the origin of the payments, and their
disbursement for the benefit of McH (and MM) together with the factors set
out in paragraph 684, all lead in one direction to the inexorable conclusion
that the intention of the Logberg developers in making the payments was to
177

induce McH to make decisions and to act in a way that was corrupt and
contrary to his obligations of honesty and integrity.
686. Mr. Lynch KC perhaps unwittingly crystallised the issue when he asked:
If there was a need to bribe McH, why use a conduit? Why not pay him
directly?
687. This is precisely the point. Why did the developers find it necessary to
pass funds through HC, even creating the letter agreements to endow ITC
with an artificial interest in land, for which monies were then passed to ITC
for distribution to TCM. It is the use of these indirect agencies and devices
in order to make payments for the benefit of McH, that leads to the
irresistible inference that the payments were corrupt and intended to induce
McH to act corruptly.
688. The answer to Question 3 is clearly in the affirmative.
Question Four
689. The fourth question is: Were the payments knowingly and intentionally
received by McH as an inducement for him to act corruptly?
690. This question raises the issue of the knowledge and intention of McH when
he received the payments. Through his lead counsel, McH submits that there
is no evidence that McH had any knowledge:
- that the two cheques drawn on HC’s FCIB account for $83,000 paid into
his South Caicos PNP campaign 2007 account, were paid out of 2
transfers of $250,000 each paid by Logwood Hotel Development
Company Ltd. (LHDL) to HC;
- that the $1m paid by HC to Chalmers & Co. (out of which $400,000 was
used to pay McH’s Amex Card) was part of a $1m loan from Henry
Mensen to HC;
178

- that the $1m paid by ITC to Saunders & Co. on 10th April 2008, was
part of a payment to ITC in the sum of $2.51m made by Westco out of
a loan of $3.1m from Belize Bank, and
- that the $400,000 cheque dated 5th December 2007 paid to Saunders &
Co by HC (and used for the purchase of Unit 5 The Enclave by McH)
was paid out of a loan from HT Ltd in the sum of $500,000, paid to HC
out of the client account of HT Ltd held by Twa Marcellin Wolf.
691. Mr. Lynch KC submits that McH had no idea where these monies came
from other than from HC, who was a “wealthy business man and friend”,
Mr. Lynch KC has further submitted that the funds received through HC
were loans, not bribes.
692. McH as he was entitled to do, elected to give no evidence or to call
witnesses in his defence. This is undoubtedly his right, and the Court draws
no adverse inferences from the exercise of his right. However, it means that
the submissions of counsel are not supported by any evidence. In particular:
- there is no evidence of any commercial or personal relationship between
McH and HC;
- there is no evidence of any loan agreement, whether oral or written
between McH and HC, and
- there is no evidence of repayment of any loan by McH to HC.
693. By contrast McH took out several loans with Saunders & Co, from East
Harbour Nominees, for smaller sums, which are well documented, in terms
of written agreements, terms of repayment, interest rates and fees
applicable.
694. Mr. Lynch KC, suggests that there was no need for documentation since
they were short terms loans. Once more there is no evidence of short-term
179

loans. But if they were short-term loans, then one would expect to see on
the charts, funds flowing from McH back to HC. There is no such evidence.
695. In the absence of any evidence of a commercial or personal relationship
between McH and HC, or any evidence of any loan agreement, oral or
otherwise, the Court is left with the evidence of payments made through HC
and ITC ultimately applied for the benefit of McH.
696. These payments are not to be viewed in isolation. The context of the
payments is important. McH was the Minister of Natural Resources
engaged in active negotiations with the developers. He was in a position to
facilitate applications for additional Crown Land requests for mutation of
parcels, and applications for planning permission. The evidence reveals his
enthusiasm in facilitating these matters when requests were made by the
developers, even rebuking the Director of Planning when he expressed
concern over the granting of further Crown Land on West Caicos to the
developers.
697. In the absence of any evidence of a commercial or personal relationship
with HC, as an experienced and as Mr. Lynch KC submitted, highly
successful businessman, surely McH must have been aware of the source of
these large sums of money from which he clearly benefited. The clear
inference arises as well, that McH must have given instructions for the
disbursement of the payments for his benefit. How else would TCM know
the details in order to effect the disbursements?
698. If this was not the case, it would follow that TCM paid $400,000 towards
McH’s Amex card, just out of the blue. ITC made a $1m payment to
Saunders & Co. to pay off his loans, without any communication from McH.
HC paid Saunders & Co. $400,000 to complete the purchase of Unit 5 The
Enclave, without any communication from McH. The obvious and
180

irresistible inference from these transactions is that McH (or someone acting
on his instructions) was in active communication with HC, conveying
instructions for the application of the funds.
699. It follows from the inference of communication with HC, and the absence
of any commercial relationship between them, that McH must have been
aware that the payments were not being financed by HC himself, but were
being financed through another source. Two further inferences arise from
this:
- McH knew the source of the payments or
- McH did not know the source, but willfully turned a blind eye as to the
source of the payments.
700. The Court finds that McH must have known the source of the funds. The
fact of the payment of $1m from ITC to Saunders & Co is undisputed. The
obvious inference to be drawn from that fact is that McH knew in advance
that payment was going to be made by ITC, knew the amount of the
payment, directed the payment to Saunders & Co., and instructed Saunders
& Co. as to how the payment was to be applied.
701. The same inferences arise from the payment of $400,000 from HC to
Saunders & Co., used to complete the purchase of Unit 5 The Enclave. McH
must have been aware through communication with HC, that the sum of
$400,000 was to be paid, directed HC to pay the money to Saunders & Co.,
and instructed Saunders & Co. as to how the money was to be applied. In
the absence of communication between McH and HC, and instructions from
McH to HC and Saunders & Co., the transactions could not have been
accomplished.
702. Mr. Lynch KC has submitted that there is no evidence that McH knew or
could have known of the ultimate source of the funds which were channeled
181

through HC, for example – the loans from Henry Mensen, HT Ltd., and
Belize Bank to Westco, and the letter agreements.
703. The Court has drawn inferences from undisputed facts that McH must have
been in communication with HC before HC made payments to Saunders &
Co. There is no direct evidence of these communications, or their exact
content. There is no evidence that HC informed McH as to the source of
the funds, or even that HC himself was aware of the loans from Henry
Mensen, HT Ltd and Belize Bank to Westco. However, it is not necessary
in this case for the Crown to prove that McH knew about the ultimate
sources of the funds that were channeled through HC to McH. It is
sufficient for the Crown to prove that McH knew that the funds which HC
passed to Chalmers & Co and Saunders & Co. for his benefit, came from
the Logberg developers.
704. The Court finds it inconceivable that McH was not aware that the funds
received through HC (and ITC) were provided by the Logberg developers.
The Court considers that the circumstances of the case point to the
inevitable conclusion that McH was aware that HC was channeling funds
on behalf of the Logberg developers. These circumstances include:
- there is no evidence of any commercial or personal relationship between
McH and HC;
- these are substantial payments made by HC (and ITC) to Chalmers &
Co. and Saunders & Co. which were disbursed for McH’s benefit;
- McH must have been in communication with HC, directing him as to
where the funds should be directed;
- McH must have been aware in advance that payments were to be made,
in order to provide for the receipt of the payments, (for example the
opening of the PNP South Caicos Campaign account);
- McH was the Minister of Natural Resources actively engaged in
negotiations with the Logberg developers;
182

- McH was an active, and (as suggested) astute businessman; he must
have known that HC himself was not funding the payments;
- as an astute businessman, he must have known who was supplying the
funds to HC, and if not, as an astute businessman and a responsible
Minister of the Crown, he would have taken steps to ascertain the
identity of the provider;
- if he did not know the provider, surely he would have asked HC himself,
with whom he must have been in regular communication;
- as a responsible Minister, and a man of considerable means who did not
need to accept bribes, as submitted, he would not have accepted these
substantial payments without satisfying himself as to the identity of the
provider, and
- if the payments were in fact loans provided by HC, (of which there is
no evidence) as an honest and responsible Minister, surely, he would
have disclosed them in his annual declaration.
705. The Court finds on the evidence beyond reasonable doubt that McH
knowingly and intentionally received the payments as an inducement for
him to act corruptly contrary to his obligations of honesty and integrity as a
Minister of the Crown.
706. Accordingly, the Court finds McH guilty on the Third Count. In doing so,
the Court expressly takes into account that McH is a man of previously good
character, and is entitled to the benefit of the propensity limb of the Vye
direction.
Counts 4-7 – Money Laundering
707. These counts relate to TCM, who is accused of concealing or disguising
the proceeds of the criminal conduct of MM and McH between 1st August
2003 and 31st August 2009.
183

708. It is the contention of the Crown that during this period TCM received into
his client accounts bribes totalling $14,666,000 for MM and $1,623,290 for
McH, which he distributed for their benefit.
709. Originally, the indictment in respect of the money laundering charges were
the subject of two counts – Count 4 in respect of MM and Count 5 in respect
of McH. However, following the written and oral closing submissions of
Mr. Smith KC, which the Court accepted, the Crown was permitted to
amend the indictment splitting the time frame of 1st August 2003 to 31st
August 2009 into two periods: 1st August 2003 to 8th October 2007 and 7th
October 2007 to 31st August 2009. Counts 4 and 6 on the amended
indictment relate to the allegations against TCM of concealing and
disguising the proceeds of criminal conduct of MM, while Counts 5 and 7
relate to the concealing and disguising of the proceeds of criminal conduct
of McH. Each count will be considered separately.
Defence Submissions
710. TCM, as he is entitled to, exercised his right not to testify at the trial, and
not to call any witnesses to give evidence on his behalf. The Court draws
no adverse inferences from his exercise of the right to call no evidence and
leave the burden on the Crown to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt.
711. Mr. Smith KC, in his written and oral submissions, sought to persuade the
Court that the Crown had not discharged its burden to the requisite standard.
In particular, Mr. Smith KC submitted:
- that there is no evidence of concealing or disguising the funds;
- the nature, source and location of the funds were known;
- the movement and disposition of the funds were clear and easily
gleaned, as represented in the charts;
184

- there is no evidence of any circuitous routing of the funds to shell
companies or to entities, where the ownership was layered or otherwise
concealed, and
- the disposition of the funds reflected their ownership.
712. From these submissions, it is implicit that, Mr. Smith KC, on behalf of
TCM, accepts the receipt of the funds by his client, and is not disputing that
the funds were intended for the benefit of MM and McH. It is expressly
submitted that “the disposition of the funds reflected its (sic) ownership”.
713. Mr. Smith KC further submits that in assessing whether TCM acted to
conceal or disguise the criminal proceeds of MM or McH, focus should be
placed on the absence of any proven definitive reason for TCM’s actions.
It seems to the Court, that in the absence of any direct evidence on this issue,
this is a matter of inference to be gleaned from the evidence of TCM’s
actions in relation to the funds, and the surrounding circumstances.
714. Mr. Smith KC further argues that there was undisputed evidence that it
was the practice of lawyers to receive funds into their client’s accounts and
distribute them on their client’s instructions. Mr. Smith KC further points
out that before the enactment of the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance 2007
there were no regulations regulating the receipt and distribution of funds by
lawyers on behalf of their clients.
715. Similarly, in respect of political donations, Mr. Smith KC points out that
there was no legislation regulating the receipt and handling of political
donations before 28th August 2012, when the Political Activities Act was
passed.
716. Mr. Smith KC directed his attack on the Crown’s case, with specific focus
on the Crown’s burden to show that the funds that came into TCM’s client
185

accounts were the proceeds of criminal conduct on the part of MM and
McH. Mr. Smith KC contends that the Crown has failed to satisfy this
burden, or to prove to the criminal standard that TCM knew or had
reasonable grounds to suspect that the funds were in fact criminal proceeds.
Crown’s Submissions
717. The Crown submits that if the Court finds MM and McH guilty of counts
1-3, then the funds paid to them and received by TCM would necessarily be
proceeds of criminal conduct. It follows that TCM’s submission that there
is no evidence that the funds received by TCM were proceeds of crime, is
untenable, since the Court would have made a finding of fact that the funds
were in fact bribes intended for MM and McH.
718. The Court having found MM and McH guilty of bribery on Counts 1-3,
the Crown submits that the only question that remains for the Court to
consider would be whether TCM knew or had reasonable grounds for
suspecting that the money, in whole or in part, directly or indirectly,
represented the proceeds of criminal conduct, and whether he concealed or
disguised these proceeds with a view to avoiding the making of a
confiscation order or avoiding prosecution.
719. In relation to the knowledge of TCM, or whether he had reasonable
grounds to suspect that the funds were proceeds of criminal conduct, the
Crown points to the following matters:
- in response to a production order to produce internal records or ledgers
showing the receipt and disbursement of millions of dollars, TCM has
not produced any;
- a comparison between an account titled “Chalmers Misick & Co.
Windsor West Ltd. – Escrow”, found on a USB memory stick found on
TCM at the time of his arrest, and a document found at the home of MM
by police titled “Chalmers & Co. Account Quick Report”, reveal
186

different descriptions for what appears to be the same credits and
payments;
- there is no evidence of a lawyer/client relationship between TCM and
MM and McH; TCM was doing no transactional work for either of
them;
- the monies received by TCM for MM and McH were “vastly” more than
their ministerial salaries; in 2004 a Minister received an annual salary
of $105,000 increased in 2007 to $125,000 with $5000 per month in
allowances; in 2004 the Chief Minister received an annual salary of
$125,000 increased in 2007 to $150,000 plus certain benefits provided
for in the budget.
720. The facts in relation to the receipt of the funds into the accounts of
Chalmers & Co, and their subsequent disbursement by TCM for the benefit
of MM and McH, are not disputed. These facts have already been recited
earlier in this judgement and will not be repeated, except where it is
necessary in examining counsel’s submissions with respect to specific
payments.
THE LAW
721. TCM was charged under s.30(1)(a) of the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance
1998, which provides:
“30(2) A person is guilty of an offence if, knowing or having reasonable
grounds to suspect that any property is, or in whole or in part directly
or indirectly represents, another person’s proceeds of criminal
conduct, he-
(a) conceals or disguises that property; or
(b) …
187

for the purpose of assisting any person to avoid prosecution for an
offence to which this Ordinance applies or to avoid the making or
enforcement in his case of a confiscation order.
(3) In subsections (1) and (2), the references to concealing or disguising
any property include references to concealing or disguising its nature,
source, location, disposition, movement or ownership of any rights with
respect to it.”
722. In his written closing submissions, Mr. Smith KC pointed out that the 1998
Ordinance was repealed and replaced by the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance
2007, which came into force on 7th October 2007. Counsel submitted that
the payments received and disbursed by TCM after this date could not
properly be charged under the 1998 Ordinance, and ought to be disregarded.
After receiving oral submissions on the issue, the Court upheld Mr. Smith’s
arguments, and ordered that the indictment be amended so as to permit the
transactions after the cutoff date to be included under two new counts.
Accordingly, Counts 6 and 7 were added to deal with transactions that
postdate the operation of the 1998 Ordinance.
723. Section 124(1)(a) and (b) of the 2007 Ordinance create the offences of
concealing and disguising criminal property. Section122(1) defines
property as criminal property if:
(a) it constitutes a person’s benefit from criminal conduct or it
represents such a benefit, in whole or in part and whether directly
or indirectly; and
(b) the alleged offender knows or suspects that it constitutes or
represents such a benefit.
724. The actus reus of the offence under both enactments appears to be the
same. The offence is committed where the defendant conceals or disguises
the fruits of another person’s criminal conduct. The 1998 Ordinance calls
188

it “the proceeds of criminal conduct”. The 2007 Ordinance calls it “criminal
property”.
725. On its face, there appears to be a slight difference in the mens rea of the
offence. The 1998 Ordinance requires the offender to know or “have
reasonable grounds to suspect” that the property represents the proceeds of
criminal conduct. The later Ordinance requires that the offender knows or
“suspects” that the property constitutes a person’s benefit from criminal
conduct.
726. The 1998 Ordinance provides for an objective test involving an
examination of the facts and circumstances known to the defendant, while
the 2007 enactment on its face appears to require a subjective test of actual
suspicion.
727. Cases in which a defendant comes before the court and gives evidence that
he actually suspected that the property was the proceeds of criminal conduct
must be quite rare, unless the defendant intends to enter a plea of guilty. In
the vast majority of cases that go to trial, the defendant who gives evidence
will deny that he had actual suspicion. The third scenario is, as in this case,
the defendant elects to give no evidence.
728. In the last scenario described above, there will be no viva voce evidence
of actual suspicion. It cannot mean that the case against the defendant must
fail for absence of evidence of actual suspicion. It must mean that the issue
remains to be determined by the court from an examination of the
surrounding facts and circumstances known to the defendant, from which
the court must draw an inference as to whether the defendant suspected or
not that the property represented the proceeds of crime. It follows that in
practice the subjective test of actual suspicion is not far removed from the
objective test of reasonable suspicion.
189

729. In R v. Saik [2006] UKHL at paragraph 26 of his judgment, in relation to
the interpretation of the word “know”, Lord Nicholls of Birkenhead opined:
“The phrase under consideration (“intend or know” in section
1(2) (of the Criminal Law Act 1977) is a provision of general
application to all conspiracies. In this context the word “know”
should be interpreted strictly and not watered down. In this
context knowledge means true belief. Whether it covers wilful
blindness is not an issue arising on this appeal. As applied to
section 93C(2) (of the Criminal Justice Act 1993) it means that
in the case of identified property, a conspirator must be aware
the property was in fact the proceeds of crime. The prosecution
must prove the conspirator knew the property was the proceeds
of criminal conduct.”
730. The 1993 Act referred to in Saik created the offence of concealing or
disguising the proceeds of criminal conduct, by a person who “knows” or
has “reasonable grounds to suspect” that the property represents the
proceeds of criminal conduct, the same language used in the Proceeds of
Crime Ordinance 1998.
731. In R v. Da Silva [2006] EWCA Crim 1654, Longmore LJ considered the
meaning of the word “suspecting” in the context of section 93A(1)(a) of the
Criminal Justice Act 1988, which creates the offence of facilitating the
retention or control of the proceeds of criminal conduct by concealment,
removal or transfer of property by a person “knowing or suspecting” that
the person is or has been engaged in criminal conduct or has benefited from
criminal conduct. At paragraph 16, Longmore LJ expressed the view:
[16] “…It seems to us that the essential element in the word
“suspect” and its affiliates, in this context, is that the defendant
must think that there is a possibility which is more than fanciful,
190

that the relevant facts exist. A vague feeling of unease would not
suffice. But the statute does not require the suspicion to be
“clear” or “firmly grounded and targeted on specific facts,” or
based upon reasonable grounds. To require the prosecution to
satisfy such criteria as the strength of the suspicion would, in
our view, be putting a gloss on the section…”
732. Knowledge is imputed from wilful blindness, where the defendant shuts
his eyes to an obvious means of knowledge or deliberately refrains from
making inquiries that would have revealed the truth. In Westminster City
Council v. Croyalgrange Ltd [1986] 1 WLR 674 at 684D, Lord Bridge
remarked:
…“it is always open to the tribunal of fact, when knowledge on
the part of a defendant is required to be proved, to base a finding
of knowledge on evidence that the defendant had deliberately
shut his eyes to the obvious or refrained from inquiry because
he suspected the truth but did not want to have his suspicion
confirmed.”
733. In Saik, Lord Hope at paragraph 53 of his speech provided useful insight
into the difference between “reasonable grounds to suspect”, and actual
suspicion:
“53 The words used in section 93C(2) can, in my opinion, be
analysed in the same way. By requiring proof of knowledge or
of reasonable grounds to suspect that the property was criminal
proceeds, the subsection directs attention in the case of each of
these two alternatives to what was in the mind of the defendant
when he engaged in the prohibited activity. Proof that he had
reasonable grounds to suspect the origin of the property is
treated in the same way as proof of knowledge. The subsection
191

assumes that a person who is proved to have had reasonable
grounds to suspect that the property had a criminal origin did
in fact suspect that this was so when he proceeded to deal with
it. A person who has reasonable grounds to suspect is on notice
that he is at the same risk of being prosecuted under the
subsection as someone who knows. It is not necessary to prove
actual knowledge, which is a subjective requirement. The
prosecutor can rely instead on suspicion. But if this alternative
is adopted, proof of suspicion is not enough. It must be proved
that there were reasonable grounds for the suspicion. In other
words, the first requirement contains both a subjective part—
that the person suspects—and an objective part-that there are
reasonable grounds for the suspicion.”
734. At paragraph 54 of Saik, Lord Hope considered the link between the
mental element of the offence and the purpose of the defendant in
laundering the proceeds:
“54. The second requirement, as to what the purpose was, is
where the essence of the mens rea of the offence is to be found.
It must be proved that the defendant's purpose was to launder
the proceeds of another person's criminal conduct. If he knows
the criminal origin of the property, his knowledge is linked to
his purpose in engaging in the activity. If he had reasonable
grounds to suspect that it had a criminal origin, his suspicion is
linked to his purpose in the same way. Proof of what his purpose
was will usually have to be found by drawing inferences.
Evidence that the defendant knew or had reasonable grounds to
suspect will usually be sufficient to show what his purpose was
when, knowing or suspecting what the origin of the property
was, he actually engaged in the prescribed activity.”
192

Beaches
736. The payments received by TCM into his Chalmers Misick and Co. FCIB
account were paid by Sandals through Unique Vacations Ltd. (UVI) in three
payments in March 2006.
193

737. In relation to these payments, Mr. Smith KC submits:
- The prosecution has not led any evidence which elucidates what was the
reason behind the payment, leaving the court to engage in speculation;
- UVI was not a part of the Sandals/Beaches group, they had only a
contractual relationship with UVI;
- Ms. Lourdes Blanco, who was the Financial Controller of UVI, who
signed off on the payments, took instructions from Dr. Pyne and was
not in a position to question the description of the transaction that Dr.
Pyne gave to her;
- some of the documents behind the transactions were for UVI’s internal
processes and were never sent to TCM, and
- Ms. Blanco had no first-hand knowledge of the reason for the
transactions.
738. Mr. Smith KC further submitted:
- TCM was not a politician, and although MM was his brother and Chief
Minister, cannot be assumed to have knowledge of the dealings of
ExCo, and the TCIG;
- the $500,000 was a political donation;
- there is no evidence that the payments were bribes, and
- alternatively, if the Court concludes that they were bribes, there is no
evidence that TCM would have known or suspected (to the necessary
legal standard) that they were bribes.
739. In response the Crown agued:
- the payments were made over a period of three weeks, and disbursed in
the same month they were received;
- TCM was aware of his brother’s position as Chief Minister, and that he
was a politician receiving payments significantly larger than his salary;
194

- having regard to the substantial size of the payments, one would expect
that TCM would have kept a file with an explanation of the source and
reason for the payments;
- it is inconceivable that an attorney would accept funds in such
significant sums, without having any idea of their source, and reason for
payment;
- TCM was not acting in any capacity as an attorney in receiving the
payments and disbursing them;
- the size of the payments, in comparison with MM’s salary should have
raised red flags in TCM’s mind;
- TCM used his client account to conceal and disguise the funds, thus
ensuring that MM would not face prosecution in connection with the
funds;
- the prosecution does not have to prove that TCM knew about the
dealings of ExCo, the TCIG or Sandals, and
- the prosecution needs only to prove that TCM knew or had reasonable
grounds to suspect that the $500,000 received into his account from
UVI, was the proceeds of criminal conduct.
740. The Crown poses the following questions for the consideration of the
Court:
- Why was TCM receiving a payment described as “GAS legal” by the
sender when he was not engaged in any legal work for UVI or Sandals?
- Why did TCM record this payment and another payment as “Deposit”
in the Windsor West Escrow, when he was not engaged in the sale of
anything?
- If this was MM’s money, why was TCM receiving it into his client
account?
- Why did TCM disburse this money for MM’s benefit when there was
no identifiable client/attorney relationship between them?
- If it was a political donation, why was it not recorded as such?
195

741. The Court considers that two additional questions arise:
- if they were indeed political donations, why were they not paid to the
PNP accounts, as were the payments for $150,000 made on 27.10.2006,
and $363,332 made on 18.01.07, from UVI through Misick &
Stanbrook?
- if they were political donations, why were the funds disbursed for the
personal benefit of MM?
Salt Cay
$150,000, $2m, and $700,000.00.
742. Under the Cooperation Termination Agreement between Harbor
Management Development Ltd. (HMDL) acting jointly with Caribbean
Islands Investments Company Limited (CIIC), and Ben Walkin (BW)
signed on 29th September 2006 by BW, it was agreed that BW was to be
paid $3m by an initial payment of $300,000 by cheque from MHO, $2m by
transfer to Chalmers Misick & Co. from HMDL, and $700,000 to be
transferred through HMDL.
743. There is no dispute that BW received the sum of $300,000 in August 2006
and paid the sum of $150,000 as a retainer to TCM. There is also no dispute
that TCM received the sum of $2m on 10.10.06 and $700,000 on 11.12.06
from HMDL through J&T Banka, and that TCM paid $1m to BW by cheque
dated 17.10.06 endorsed “For Deposit Salt Cay Resort Development”.
744. Mr. Smith KC submits that there is no evidence that TCM was ever
informed by Walkin about the Cooperation Termination Agreement or that
TCM ever had sight of it.

having regard to the documentary evidence behind the payments, supports
196

the interpretation that there was “a joint purpose” behind the transfers. One

746. Further, it is submitted that there is no evidence that the funds were
criminal proceeds or that TCM knew or suspected them to be criminal
proceeds, or that he concealed or disguised them to avoid prosecution of the
other defendants.
747. Mr. Smith KC further argues that there were sufficient funds in Chalmers
& Co. FCIB account to facilitate the transfer of $500,000 to the Chalmers
& Co. BB account, without the deposit of the $2m on 12th October 2006.
Accordingly, it is submitted that the prosecution is unable to provide a nexus
between the cheque for $500,000 to the BB account, which was noted on
the cheque as “Escrow – Windsor West Ltd” and the $2m transfer.
748. Mr. Smith KC asserts that when the transaction is looked at objectively,
the funds that were received by TCM, were distributed by TCM according
to the instructions of the sender, keeping in mind that there is no evidence
that TCM had any knowledge of the Termination Agreement.
749. According to Mr. Smith KC, since the documentation supports the fact that
the payments of $2m and $700,000 were in fact loans presumably from J&T
Banka, the funds did not emanate from “an impugned source and can be
ignored by the court”.
750. In response, the Crown submits that the Termination Agreement was used
as a front to channel funds through TCM to MM under the guise that TCM
was receiving funds for BW. The clear evidence, it is argued, is that TCM
concealed the funds by passing them through his client accounts and then
197

disbursing them for MM’s benefit, thus assisting MM to avoid prosecution
for accepting corrupt payments.
751. The Crown further submits that the references in the supporting documents
to a loan clearly refer to a loan from J&T Banka to HMDL, not to any “loan”
from J&T Banka or HMDL to TCM. This was made clear in the evidence
of Mr. Glendinning, the Forensic Investigator.
752. As to the submission that TCM had not had sight of the Termination
Agreement, or knew of its existence, the Crown points out that this would
mean that TCM had no idea why he was receiving $2.7m from HMDL, and
why he was paying $1m to BW. Further, there is no evidence of any
instruction from the sender to pay BW $1m, as suggested by Mr. Smith KC,
that TCM merely disbursed the money as instructed by the sender. It is
noted as well that the payment was described as “for deposit Salt Cay Golf
Resort Development”, which was clearly a misstatement of the truth.
753. The Crown notes that Mr. Glendinning did not accept that there were
sufficient funds in the Chalmers & Co. FCIB account before the $2m
deposit to cover the cheque for $500,000 drawn by TCM and marked
“Escrow Windsor West Ltd.” and deposited into the Chalmers & Co. BB
account. Even so the Crown submits that this was a good example of co-
mingling money to layer, conceal and disguise funds in the absence of any
client ledgers.
754. The Crown notes that the $500,000 deposited into Chalmers BB account
was disbursed for the benefit of MM as follows:
- $100,000 to MM’s HSBC account;
- $70,717 towards charter fees;
- $200,000 deposit on property in California, and
198

- $105,931.16 wire transfer to J&T Banka to cover expenditure on MM’s
Amex card.
755. The Crown further notes that of the $500,000 placed on term deposit at
BB, between December 2006 and March 2007, TCM disbursed just short of
$430,000 for the benefit of MM.
756. The Crown submits that there is no lawful reason why MM shared in these
funds. The Crown contends that the clear inference is that TCM was aware
that these funds had no legitimate purpose, and the agreement was merely a
front for getting monies to MM, and TCM used his client account to
facilitate this deception by receiving and disbursing the funds paid as bribes.
$6m
757. It is undisputed that MM and LRM applied for a loan of $6m from J&T
Banka to purchase a house in California, USA. The application for the loan
was made on 20th April 2007 and was approved 10 days later. The funds
were sent to the Chalmers Misick FCIB account by J&T Banka on 14th May
2007, and disbursed by TCM during the period 15th May 2007 to 5th June
2007.
758. It is also not disputed that in addition to receiving and disbursing the
proceeds of the loan TCM played an active part in securing the loan. TCM
signed a mortgage of 50 shares held by Business Ventures Ltd. (BVL) in
Salt Cay Golf Course Ltd (SCGL) as security for the loan. TCM’s passport
was among the security documents. TCM signed as a Director of BVL
(which, according to the Financial Services Commission records he was
not). He was at the time, however, the sole shareholder of BVL.
759. Mr. Smith KC submits that the misapplication of the proceeds of the loan
gives rise to a legal right by the lender against the borrowers, and cannot
199

elevate the misapplication of the funds to a bribe. Further, there is no
evidence to make the tribunal of fact sure that TCM had knowledge or
suspicion that the funds were proceeds of a bribe, and even if the mens rea
of knowledge and reasonable suspicion is established, there is no evidence
to the required standard of concealing or disguising to avoid prosecution or
the making or enforcement of a confiscation order.
760. Further, TCM submits through his attorney that once the Court finds that
there was “nothing untoward” about MM and LRM taking a loan, then there
can be nothing untoward about MM giving TCM instructions to distribute
the proceeds of the loan. Since the funds are not proceeds of crime, their
distribution is unimpeachable.
761. The Crown seeks to connect the $6m loan to the savings made by MHo on
the purchase of 122.14 acres of Crown land for $6,102,000, which was later
valued at $12,932,000. The Crown views the loan transaction simply as a
device to legitimize payment of a bribe to MM and McH.
762. The Crown makes the following points:
- why were the loan proceeds sent to TCM, an attorney based in TCI,
when the purpose of the loan was for the purchase of a house in
California, USA;
- MM was engaged in negotiations with MHo for the Salt Cay
development;
- why would TCM, “the human face” of the guarantee for the loan, apply
the funds for wholly unrelated purposes;
- as guarantor, he should have been concerned whether MM could be able
to repay the loan, which required repayments averaging of $500,000 per
month plus interest at 10.5% per annum;
200

- why would BVL, a company controlled by TCM, hold 50% of the
shareholding in SCGL, which would later be used as security for the
loan, and
- there was no attempt to repay the loan, which was due on 29th April
2008, nor is there any evidence that J&T Banka took any steps to
recover the loan until December 2009.
763. The Crown seeks to draw the Court’s attention to the manner in which
TCM dealt with the funds:
- on 15th May 2007, the day after he received the loan proceeds, TCM
transferred $5m to a FCIB term deposit;
- TCM issued a cheque for $500,000 to his BB client account, which he
disbursed within a month; one payment was a wire transfer made on 15th
May 2007 for $40,564.75 to pay McH’s Amex Card;
- on 1st June 2007, TCM issued a cheque for $1m payable to My Way
Productions 2 Ltd (MWP2) marked “Proceeds of Loan – per Client
Instructions”;
- on 5th June TCM issued a cheque for $3m to Business Ventures Ltd
(BVL) marked “client instructions”;
- TCM had formed both companies, (BVL on 17th January 2006, and
MWP2 on 12th July 2006), about the time that MM started to receive
payments from MHo;
- the $1m paid to MWP2 was disbursed within one month, including
$350,000 to LRM, and $100,000 to Rayediant Jewels in Beverly Hills.
- proceeds of the $6m loan were used to complete the purchase of a condo
(“K9-CCH Core Holdings”) at North Caicos for MM;
- on 7th June 2007, the $3m cheque was credited to the account of BVL,
which was opened on 5th June 2007.
- the signatory on the BVL account was TCM with MM having power of
attorney if anything were to happen to TCM;
- the signatories on the MWP2 account were TCM, MM, and LRM;
201

- on 8th June 2007, the $3m was placed on a term deposit;
- on the same day BVL obtained a loan for $1m from BB for a term of
one year; TCM then transferred $937,000 to PNC Bank in the USA for
credit to Rock Wood Properties;
- on 1st October 2007, TCM transferred $214,640.00 to MM’s BB
account;
- on 27th September 2007 a cheque was drawn for $315,000 payable to
Jubilee Construction, which was engaged in construction of a cinema
complex for MM and LRM;
- on 21st December 2007, the $3m term deposit was returned with interest
of $92,630.14; on the same day TCM transferred $1m to his Chalmers
& Co BB account, which cleared his overdraft;
- on 28th December 2007, TCM issued a cheque on the BVL account for
$340,598.28 which cleared the overdraft on MM’s BB account;
- between January 2008 to June 2008, TCM made transfers totalling

- on 3rd March 2008, there was a transfer of $5,000 from the BVL account

764. The Crown submits that the overwhelming inference is that the BVL
account was opened to receive and disburse $3m from the $6m loan. This

765. In response to TCM’s submission that there is no evidence of concealing
or disguising, or that TCM possessed the knowledge necessary to have the
intention to conceal or disguise the proceeds, the Crown responds that the
submissions ignore the evidence and the inferences provided.
202

766. The Crown concludes that the inference to be drawn from the evidence is
that TCM assisted MM and McH in concealing, layering and disbursing
$6m paid as a bribe, and thereby reducing the risk of MM and McH being
prosecuted for bribery.

other payments. The Crown submits that TCM acted as a front for MM in

WEST CAICOS
$83,000 and $33,000
777. On 17th November 2006 and 24th November 2006 Logwood Hotel
Development Co. Ltd. (LHD) transferred $250,000 to Harold Charles (HC).
On 20th November 2006, HC paid Chalmers & Co. $83,000, and on 30th
November 2006, $33,000. On the same dates HC made two payments of
$83,000 to McH’s South Caicos PNP 2007 Campaign account, which was
opened on the same day that the first payment was deposited.
778. In November 2006, TCM disbursed more than $200,000 for the benefit of
MM from his client account as BB, including $31,025.53 for his Amex card
and deposits of $50,000 and $80,000 into an HSBC account at Beverly Hills
in the name of MM and LRM.
779. TCM submits that:
- there is no evidence that he knew of the decision of ExCo to sell Crown
land to Logwood Development Co. Ltd (LDCL) at $30,000 per acre, or
that this was illegal;
- there is no evidence that TCM knew that the funds originated from
LDCL;
- there is nothing on the face of the cheque from HC that could have
alerted TCM that the funds were anything other than
“payments/contributions” from a prominent businessman shortly before
the February 2007 elections, and so
- the transactions were unimpeachable, and the disbursements are
irrelevant, as they were undertaken on his client’s instructions.
205

780. The Crown responds:
- the Crown is not required to prove that TCM knew of decisions of ExCo,
or that such decisions are illegal, or that TCM knew the source of the
funds;
- the Crown is required to prove that the funds were the proceeds of crime
and that TCM knew or had reasonable grounds to suspect that they were;
- if the Court finds MM and McH guilty of Count 3, then the funds are
proved to the criminal standard to be the proceeds of crime, and
- the Court is entitled to look at the way in which the funds were received
and disbursed, and draw inferences with respect to the state of TCM’s
mind.
781. In drawing these inferences, the Court should consider:
- TCM kept no record of the funds received;
- TCM knew that the funds were for MM, because he disbursed them for
MM’s personal benefit;
- there is no evidence that HC or MM was a client of TCM;
- there is no client/attorney reason for the receipt or disbursement of the
funds, and
- the evidence clearly points to the fact that the payments to a politician
were disguised by a developer, and kept removed from the politician’s
personal bank accounts to conceal and disguise the payments by passing
them through his brother who was acting as a personal banker for him,
rather than a lawyer.
$1 million
782. It is undisputed that Henry Mensen paid HC $1m by cheque on 14th
November 2007. On the same day HC paid $1m by cheque to Chalmers &
Co BB account. Two days later TCM transferred $400,000 to pay McH’s
Amex card, and $300,000 to MM’s card. On 20th November 2007, TCM
paid $70,000 to MM which was deposited into MM’s FCIB account.
206

783. Mr. Smith KC argued that the Crown was seeking to link the $1m payment
to the ExCo decision made on 24th October 2007 granting certain
Permanent Residence Certificates, and Belonger status to persons
connected to the West Caicos Development as well as a reduction in
customs duties. Mr. Smith KC submits that there is no evidence that TCM
knew of these decisions so as to reasonably suspect that any of the funds
received were proceeds of criminal conduct. Further there is no evidence
of a nexus between the ExCo decisions and Henry Mensen or HC. In
addition, there is no evidence that the disbursements were made otherwise
than in accordance with “instructions from TCM’s client(s)”.
784. The Crown points out that the source of the funds can be traced to LDCL,
as HC was reimbursed by at least $900,000, through the client account of
HT Ltd, $700,000 of which was repaid to Mensen by HC.
785. The Crown further submits:
- there are no records that show there was any identifiable client/attorney
professional relationship between TCM and MM;
- there are no records to show why these funds were for the benefit of
MM and McH, or why they were used to pay off their credit cards in
sums higher than their annual salaries;
- HC acted as a go between to pass funds through TCM to benefit MM
and McH, politicians who were responsible for making decisions with
respect to the West Caicos development;
- there is no requirement for the Crown to prove that TCM knew of the
ExCo decisions;
- if the Court finds MM and McH guilty on Count 3, then the proceeds
are derived from criminal conduct, and
207

- given the circumstances of the payments and the disbursements the
Court should infer that TCM knew or reasonably suspected that the
funds were the proceeds of criminal conduct.
$500,000
786. On 31st December 2007, TCI Skyking paid $500,000 by cheque drawn on
its TCI Bank account into its FCIB account. By cheque drawn on its FCIB
account TCI Skyking paid the same sum to Chalmers & Co., deposited the
same day in his BB account. By wire transfer made the same day, the same
sum was transferred to MM’s account at J&T Banka.
787. Mr. Smith KC argues that the Crown is attempting to link this payment to
a decision of ExCo made on 12th December 2007, which he submits is
tenuous at best. He further submits:
- there is no evidence that TCM knew of ExCo’s decision, and even if he
did, that alone could not have led him to know or reasonably suspect
that the funds were the proceeds of criminal conduct;
- there is no evidence that the funds were the proceeds of criminal
conduct; Chart 9 simply notes that HC sent $500,000 from one TCI
Skyking account to its FCIB account, and thereafter transferred the sum
to Chalmers & Co. BB account;
- there is no evidence that the funds came from a source that TCM knew
or could have known, was impugned in any way;
