- there was nothing written on the cheque that was credited to Chalmers
& Co., which would have put TCM on notice that the funds were
criminal in nature, and
- the funds are unimpeachable, and therefore their distribution is
irrelevant.
788. The Crown submits in response that there was no professional legal work
that TCM was performing for MM, in receiving money from HC and paying
208

it out for MM, unless it was to conceal its source and the true purpose and
the nature of the transactions. The Crown repeats its submissions earlier
made as to TCM’s knowledge of the nature of the payments.
$700,000.00
789. On 10th April 2008 ITC received $2,510.00 from Westco Development
Group Ltd. (Westco) which was part proceeds of a loan from Belize Bank.
On the same day, ITC paid $700,000 to Chalmers & Co. by cheque
deposited on its FCIB account. By cheques dated 10th April 2008, for
$100,000 and $200,000 and a cheque dated 15th April 2008 for $400,000,
TCM deposited $700,000 into Chalmers & Co. BB account. About
$400,000 of the funds were disbursed by TCM between 10th April 2008 and
30th April 2008. The balance was disbursed in May 2008.
790. Mr. Smith KC assumes that the Crown is seeking to link this payment with
Cabinet’s decision on 20th February 2008 in which it rescinded its decision
to sell 2.1 acres of Crown land at $30,000 per acre to LDCL, and instead
decided to sell the parcel to West Harbour Development Co. Ltd. (WHDL)
at the same price. Mr. Smith KC submits that any such link is speculative
at best. He further submits:
- there is no evidence that TCM knew about the decision of Cabinet, and
even if he did, that alone could not have led him to know or reasonably
suspect that the funds were the proceeds of criminal conduct;
- there is no evidence that TCM knew that the source of the funds had
anything to do with Westco, as the cheque from ITC did not state the
purpose or the source;
- the original source of the funds is the proceeds of a loan; while the
proceeds of a loan can be used as a bribe, there is no evidence of the
reason or purpose for the funds being paid to ITC, and then to the
Chalmers & Co. account, and
209

- the source of the funds is unimpeachable, and therefore the distribution
is irrelevant.
791. In response, the Crown points out that the disbursements made in
April/May 2008, were predominantly for MM, and for McH to a lesser
extent, and included payments to J&T Banka to service McH’s Amex card.
Additionally payments were made for private jet charter, and transfers to
HSBC Beverly Hills for MM and LRM and legal fees. The funds were
disbursed over a short period of time.
792. The Crown further highlights that:
- there are no records produced by TCM for the payment and
disbursements;
- the disbursements were far beyond MM’s earnings from legitimate
sources;
- there is no evidence of any repayment by MM, assuming that the funds
were the proceeds of a loan;
- there is no lawful reason for the credits or disbursements;
- the process was a way of concealing bribes, and disbursing the money
in any way that hid the true beneficiary and the payer from inquiry.
Count 4
793. Count 4 charges TCM with concealing or disguising the proceeds of
criminal conduct knowing or having reasonable grounds for suspecting that
they represented in whole or in part the proceeds of the criminal conduct of
MM, with a view to avoiding the making or enforcement of a confiscation
order or avoiding prosecution for unlawful conduct.
794. Count 4 charges TCM for acts of concealing or disguising the criminal
proceeds of MM between 1st August 2003 and 8th October 2007. Count 6
210

charges the same offence for TCM’s acts which occurred between 7th
October 2007 and 31st August 2009.
795. The Court finds it appropriate to consider Counts 4 and 6 consecutively,
followed by Counts 5 and 7, which concern concealing and disguising the
proceeds of the criminal conduct of McH.
DISCUSSION
Question 1
796. The questions posed by the agreed Route to Verdict will be considered in
turn. The first question is: Did TCM receive the funds directly or indirectly
for the benefit of MM?
797. It is not disputed that TCM received the funds in question (except for the
$3m paid directly by HMDL to Guzman, which will be discussed shortly),
and disbursed them for the benefit of MM, as instructed, by “his client”,
who on the evidence, the Court infers must have been MM. How else would
TCM know the details of the amounts to be paid and the names and details
of the payees?
Question Two
798. The second question is: Did the funds received by TCM represent directly
or indirectly, wholly or in part the proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct?
799. In considering Counts 1-3, the Court made findings of fact that the sums
of money paid to TCM and disbursed for the benefit of MM, were the
proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct. The Court reconsidered its findings in
the light of TCM’s submissions that there is no evidence sufficient to satisfy
this court to the criminal standard that the funds he received were the
proceeds of criminal conduct. The Court examined the submissions and
found them to be unrealistic, untenable and unpersuasive. Accordingly the
211

Court rejected them. It follows that the findings of fact that the sums
received by TCM and disbursed for the benefit of MM, will stand.
Accordingly the answer to question two is in the affirmative.
Question Three
800. The third question is: Did TCM know or have reasonable grounds to
suspect that the monies he received were the proceeds of crime?
801. In considering this question it is necessary to examine each payment
received by TCM and disbursed for MM’s benefit.
$500,000 from UVI
802. The submissions of TCM and the Crown have been set out at paragraphs
711-721 ante. Mr. Smith KC submits, in essence, that the payments were
political donations. However, if the Court finds that they were bribes, there
is no evidence that TCM would have known or suspected to the required
legal standard, that they were bribes.
803. The Crown points to several factors that the Court should consider in
deciding whether TCM knew or had reasonable grounds to suspect that the
funds were unlawful payments:
- TCM must have been aware that his brother was a politician earning a
fixed salary, and was receiving large sums of money, in some cases
significantly more than his annual salary;
- TCM was not keeping ledgers, or files, recording the receipt of such
large payments, their disbursement, source and reason for payment;
- TCM was not acting as an attorney for MM; there is no record of any
professional business or legal transaction he was handling for MM;
- why was TCM using his client account to receive and disburse political
donations which could have been paid directly into PNP bank accounts
212

(as evidenced by the payments of $150,000 and $350,000 paid by UVI
through Misick & Stanbrook to the PNP), and
- if they were political donations, why was the payment of $200,000 not
recorded as such in the Windsor West Escrow Account found on a
memory stick on TCM at the time of his arrest?
804. The Court has already referred to the correspondence between TCM and
attorneys acting for Sandals in June – September 2010, after the COI had
started. The correspondence shows a reluctance by TCM to acknowledge
that the payments he received from UVI in March 2006 were political
donations, until the threat of criminal proceedings was raised by Patterson,
Mair Hamilton in their letter dated 18th August 2010. Of interest, as well,
is TCM’s confirmation in his letter dated 18th June 2010 in response to
Misick and Stanbrook’s inquiry made two days earlier, that his firm did not
at the time of receipt of the funds, nor at the time of writing, act for the PNP.
805. TCM as he was entitled to, elected to give no evidence or to call witnesses
on his behalf. The Court has no direct evidence of his knowledge as to
whether the funds were the proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct. TCM’s
knowledge or state of mind at the time he received and disbursed the funds,
must be inferred from the undisputed evidence:
- MM is TCM’s brother with whom on the evidence he was in contact
and communication;
- TCM must have known that MM was the Chief Minister, and as such
was on a fixed salary;
- TCM was receiving large sums of money into his client account from a
company based in Florida (UVI), for whom, based on the evidence, he
was not doing any legal work;
- there is no evidence of a client/attorney relationship between MM and
TCM;
213

- there is no evidence that TCM was handling any legal transaction for
MM;
- TCM must have been aware that the payments he was receiving was
significantly larger than MM’s annual salary;
- TCM must have been receiving instructions from MM to disburse the
funds, otherwise he would not have details of amounts and payee
information to effect payment;
- there is no evidence before the Court that TCM made any inquiries of
MM as to the reason for the payments he was receiving;
- there is no evidence that TCM made inquiries of the sender as to the
reason and purpose of the payments;
- there are no ledgers or files or records produced by TCM pursuant to
production orders, with respect to the payments, and their disbursement,
apart from the Windsor West Escrow account found on the memory
stick;
- TCM disbursed the funds for MM’s benefit within the same month he
received them – March 2006;
- there was no client/attorney relationship between TCM and the PNP,
and
- the disbursement of the funds was for the benefit of MM, not the PNP.
806. The Court notes the submissions of defence counsel that it was common
practice for attorneys to receive and pay out funds on behalf of clients. As
far as the Court is aware this by no means unusual, but as far as the evidence
shows in this case, it occurs in the context of a client/attorney relationship
where attorneys are handling legal transactions for their clients. In the
absence of a client/attorney relationship, attorneys would be acting simply
as bankers not legal practitioners. The evidence in this case illustrate
examples of attorneys receiving and paying out funds on behalf of their
clients, for example Misick and Stanbrook for Beaches and Miller Simon
O’Sullivan for MHo. It is noted as well that these firms kept ledgers
214

detailing payments received and funds paid out of their client accounts on
their client’s instructions.
807. Mr. Smith KC has further submitted that there was no legal requirement at
the time for attorneys to keep ledgers and financial records. While this may
be so, one would expect that a reasonably prudent attorney receiving and
disbursing large sums of money would keep records of receipts and
payments, not only to account to clients for their funds, but for his own
protection in the event that issues of financial misappropriation arise at a
later date.
808. From the facts and circumstances outlined in the preceding paragraphs, the
Court comes to the conclusion beyond reasonable doubt that TCM must
have known that the $500,000 he received into his client account were the
proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct. The Court finds it incredible that an
attorney in TCM’s position receiving such substantial payments and
knowing that his brother was a salaried politician would not ask his brother
about the source and reason for the payments. He was not acting for the
PNP, so he must have known that the payments were not intended for the
PNP. In fact, he disbursed the funds for the personal benefit of his brother,
supporting the clear inference that he knew the funds were intended to
benefit MM personally. Having regard to the size of the payments, he must
have been put on inquiry as to the purpose of the payments and their source.
His inquiries would have revealed that the payments were tainted by
illegality.
809. If TCM, deliberately refrained from making pertinent inquiries because he
suspected that they would have revealed the true nature of the payments,
then knowledge of the illegality of the payments must be imputed to him.
TCM would have placed himself in a position of wilful blindness as to the
true nature of the payments.
215

810. Even if the Court is wrong as to its finding of fact that TCM knew that the
payments were the proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct, there is strong and
compelling evidence that TCM had reasonable grounds to suspect that the
funds were such. These are:
- the size of the payments;
- the fact that his brother was the Chief Minister, and was in a position of
influence in terms of making decisions affecting potential investors;
- the fact that MM was not receiving the funds into his personal accounts,
but through TCM’s client account, in the absence of any transactional
legal business with his firm;
- TCM kept no record of the receipt of these substantial payments, the
reason for them, or their disbursement, and
- The speed at which the payments were disbursed upon instructions from
MM.
811. Accordingly the answer to question 3 is in the affirmative.
Question 4
812. Did TCM conceal or disguise the funds for MM?
813. This issue is clearly a matter of inference from the evidence. The
undisputed evidence is that TCM received the funds into his client account,
from a source (UVI) which was not his client, in favour of a recipient (MM)
who was not his client. The undisputed evidence is that TCM disbursed the
funds within the same month they were received.
814. Obvious questions arise. Some of them are:
- why was TCM receiving money into his client account, that he claims
was a political donation, when he was not acting for the PNP?
216

- why was TCM receiving payments into his client account when he was
not acting for UVI, PNP or MM in any legal transaction?
- why were the payments disbursed so quickly for the benefit of MM?
815. The obvious and compelling inference to be drawn from the evidence is
that TCM received the funds into his client account in order to conceal them.
If they were paid into MM’s bank accounts, they may have been traced to
MM. They were disbursed from TCM’s client account within the same
month for the benefit of MM, in such a way that they could not be traced to
any of MM’s personal accounts.
816. The answer to Question 4 is in the affirmative.
Question 5
817. Did TCM conceal or disguise the funds intending that MM avoid a
prosecution or the making or enforcement of a confiscation order?
818. The question addresses the purpose of TCM in concealing or disguising
the payments from UVI. As explained by Lord Hope in R v Saik (ante),
TCM’s knowledge of the criminal origin of the money is linked to his
purpose in concealing or disguising it. The same applies if he is not sure of
the origin, but has reasonable grounds to suspect that it is criminal in nature.
Proof of TCM’s purpose in concealing or disguising the funds is a matter of
inference. Evidence that TCM knew or had reasonable grounds to suspect,
that the funds were the proceeds of criminal conduct is sufficient to show
what TCM’s purpose was in concealing or disguising the payments for
MM’s benefit. The inference is clear and unavoidable: TCM’s purpose was
to avoid the making or enforcement of a confiscation order against or
avoiding the prosecution of MM for unlawful conduct.
217

819. It follows that the answer to Question 5 is in the affirmative, and TCM is
guilty of concealing or disguising the payment of $500,000 received from
UVI, pursuant to section 30(2)(a) of the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance 1998.
Salt Cay Payments
$150,000, $2,000,000 and $700,000
820. These payments are related to the Corporation Termination Agreement
made between HMDL, CIIC and BW, which provided for the separation of
BW from the Salt Cay development on payment to him of $3m.
821. There is no dispute that Ben Walkin received $300,000 through MHo’s
attorneys, Miller Simon, O’Sullivan in August 2006, out of which he paid
TCM $150,000 as a retainer fee to secure his services to protect BW’s
interest in the Salt Cay development.
822. BW’s evidence that he paid $150,000 out of the initial payment of
$300,000 has not been contradicted. BW struck the Court as a forthright
witness whose credibility was not shaken in cross-examination. The Court
accepts his evidence with respect to the receipt of $300,000 and his payment
of $150,000 to TCM as a retainer. No issue arises on the evidence, that the
payment of $150,000 from BW, represents the proceeds of criminal
conduct, and nothing more needs to be said about that payment.
823. It is not disputed that TCM received wire transfers into his FCIB client
account in the sum of $2m (less transfer fees) on 10th October 2006, and
$700,000 (less transfer fees) on 11th December 2006, from HMDL. From
the $2m payment TCM paid $1m to BW by cheque on 17th October 2006.
No further payment was made to BW out of the $2.7m received by TCM
from HMDL.
218

824. The submissions of Counsel with respect to these payments have been set
out in paragraphs 744-752 ante.
825. Mr. Smith KC submitted that there is no evidence that BW ever told TCM
about the Cooperation Termination Agreement, or that he ever had sight of
it. This is astounding, having regard to the evidence of BW that he retained
TCM to look after his interest in the development, and instructed TCM to
pursue the $1.7m that was still owed to BW under the agreement. How
could BW give this instruction to TCM without telling him of the terms of
the agreement, even if he did not actually provide him with a copy, which
the Court finds highly unlikely, given the fact that the basis of his
instructions would be the terms of the written agreement that he signed. The
evidence that BW instructed TCM to recover the balance owing of $1.7m
was in fact elicited in cross-examination by both Mr. Smith KC and Mr.
Lynch KC.
826. Mr. Smith KC further submitted that there was a joint purpose behind the
transfers from HMDL. One purpose was to satisfy the payments under the
Termination Agreement. The other purpose was a loan.
827. The first purpose contradicts the earlier suggestion that TCM was not told
about the terms of the agreement, or had sight of it. The second purpose,
that the transfers were loans, is equally problematic. There is no evidence
in the case that TCM or MM borrowed money from HMDL. In fact the
evidence of Mr. Glendinning made it clear that the references to “loan” were
to a loan that HMDL had taken from J&T Banka.
828. Mr. Smith further submitted that the funds were distributed according to
the instructions of the sender. The problem with this submission is that
there is no evidence to support it. There is no evidence of instructions from
the sender.
219

829. Assuming, as TCM submits, he had no knowledge of the Termination
Agreement, on what basis is he paying BW $1m? If the submission is
followed to its logical conclusion, it means that TCM received instructions
from the sender (HMDL) to disburse the bulk of the remaining $1.7m for
the benefit of MM.
830. Mr. Smith KC further argues that since the source of the funds was a loan,
the funds did not emanate from an “impugned source” and so can be ignored
by the Court. The submission merely has to be stated to be rejected. The
suggestion that payments that are alleged to be bribes, should be accepted
to be legitimate simply because they are paid out of a bank loan, is clearly
untenable, and deserves no further discussion.
831. The Crown contends that the Termination Agreement was used as a ploy
to channel funds through TCM for the benefit of MM. The Crown notes
the disbursements to MM out of TCM’s BB client account and submits that
there is no lawful reason why MM shared in these funds.
832. The Court will now address the questions set out in the Route to Verdict
in respect of these payments.
Questions 1 and 2
833. For the avoidance of repetition the answers to questions 1 and 2 are in the
affirmative for the reasons set out in paragraphs 796-799 ante.
Question 3
834. Question Three is the crucial question to be answered:
- Did TCM know or have reasonable grounds to suspect that the monies
he received were the proceeds of crime?
220

835. This question requires the Court to make findings with respect to the
knowledge and the state of mind of TCM at the time he received and
disbursed the $2.7m from HMDL.
836. The Court notes that there is no direct evidence on this issue from TCM,
since he elected not to give evidence, as is his right. The evidence as to his
knowledge, or whether he had reasonable grounds to suspect that the funds
he received from HMDL were the proceeds of criminal conduct, must be
gleaned from inferences available on the evidence.
837. TCM appears to find himself between a rock and a hard place. If he admits
that he was aware of the Cooperation Termination Agreement, then he
opens himself to allegations of misappropriation of his clients (BW’s) funds
in the sum of $1.7m. If he denies knowledge of the Termination Agreement,
(which he appears to be doing through counsel’s submission that there is no
evidence that he was told about it or had sight of it), then he finds himself
in a position where he is receiving large sums of money from HMDL, and
making a payment of $1m to BW, and sizeable disbursements for the benefit
of MM, without any idea of the reason for the payment of $2.7m into his
client account.
838. To fill this void, Mr. Smith KC submitted without any evidential basis,
that TCM was disbursing the money on the sender’s instructions. This
submission leads to further problems for TCM. Did the sender instruct
TCM to write on the face of the $1m cheque to BW “for deposit Salt Cay
Golf Resort Development”, when there is no evidence of any transaction
involving the purchase by BW of any land or interest in the development?
Why would TCM describe the payment in this way? Mr. Smith KC
understandably, makes no attempt to explain this deliberate misdescription.
221

839. The submission leads to further difficulties for TCM. If he was disbursing
the money on the sender’s instructions, it follows that HMDL also
instructed him to make quite substantial disbursements for the benefit of
MM.
840. The Crown’s solution to this apparent conundrum is to be found in its
submission that the Cooperation Termination Agreement was designed as a
device through which MHo was able to pass bribes to MM using HMDL (a
creature of MHo) to transfer funds to TCM to be used for MM’s benefit, as
well as to pay off BW for work done on the Salt Cay project.
841. The Court finds as a fact that TCM must have been aware of the existence
of the Termination Agreement. It simply makes no sense that BW would
retain MM to protect his interest in the Salt Cay Development, but fail to
inform him of the terms of the Termination Agreement under which he was
to be paid $2.7m by HMDL? In fact, BW’s evidence in cross-examination
that he instructed TCM to recover the balance of $1.7m still owed to him
puts the issue beyond doubt. If he gave TCM these instructions he must
have explained to him the basis on which the balance of $1.7m was
calculated. BW, whom the Court found to be a credible witness, was clearly
unaware of the dual purpose of the Termination Agreement.
842. One inference that may be drawn from the payment of the disbursements
by TCM for MM’s benefit is that TCM was aware of the dual purpose of
the Termination Agreement – part was intended for BW, and part was
intended for MM.
843. It is not in dispute that TCM had no client/attorney relationship with MM
or HMDL. He did have a professional relationship with BW, and clearly
there was a lawful basis for him to receive money on BW’s behalf pursuant
to the Termination Agreement. But on the evidence, there is no professional
222

reason for TCM to be receiving large sums of money from a foreign based
entity on behalf of MM and disbursing them for MM’s benefit.
844. Despite the suggestion of TCM through his counsel, there is no evidence
of any instructions from the sender of the funds (HMDL) for disbursement.
The disbursements themselves, for example $70,717.02 for charter fees to
Universal Jet Aviation for MM, reveal a level of detail, that could hardly be
within the knowledge of HMDL. The instructions would most likely have
been provided by MM himself.
845. TCM knew that MM was a politician, and as Premier, was on a fixed
salary, but was receiving sums of money from a foreign source in some
cases larger than his annual salary, which TCM was passing through his
client account, and disbursing for MM’s benefit. Surely, as a prudent and
experienced attorney, TCM must have considered, if not out of professional
propriety, then out of human curiosity, why these large sums were being
passed through him for disbursement to his brother.
846. The compelling inference to be drawn from the evidence is that TCM knew
that the funds he received from HMDL were partially intended for the
benefit of MM, and were the proceeds of criminal conduct. Even if TCM
was not certain that the funds were criminal in nature, it was open to him to
seek disclosure from MM as to the true nature of the funds which were
passing through his client account and exposing him to potential criminal
liability. It was open to him as well, to make inquiries of the sender, as to
the purpose of the payments, and instructions as to the intended
beneficiaries. There is no evidence in this case that TCM made any such
inquiries. The obvious inferences to be drawn from TCM’s failure to make
pertinent inquiries are either that he knew that the payments represented the
proceeds of crime, or that he deliberately refrained from making inquiries
because he suspected that the payments were unlawful and did not wish to
223

confirm his suspicions. The latter amounts to wilful blindness on the part
of TCM from which the Court is entitled to impute knowledge on his part.
847. This Court finds as a fact from the foregoing discussion that TCM knew
that the funds he was receiving were the proceeds of criminal conduct on
MM’s part. Even if the Court is wrong on this finding, the Court finds that
the evidence reveals reasonable grounds for TCM to suspect that the
payments represented proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct. This evidence
includes:
- TCM’s knowledge of the position of his brother as Premier, and the fact
that he was on a fixed income;
- Although TCM would not be aware of the details of Cabinet decisions,
he would have known that MM was in a position of influence, and
would be involved in negotiations with potential investors, and was in a
position to make important decisions with financial consequences for
them;
- The size of the payments were some times larger than MM’s annual
salary, and
- These large payments were being sent to MM for deposit into his client
account in the absence of any transactional business being handled by
TCM for MM.
Question 4
848. In relation to Question 4, there is no legitimate reason why TCM was
receiving money from a foreign entity (HMDL) for a beneficiary (MM), in
the absence of any client/attorney relationship with either of them. The
inference is clear that TCM was using his client account to receive money
on behalf of MM and making disbursements for MM’s benefit, without the
funds being received into MM’s account from the senders. The payments
are not directly traceable to MM, by virtue of the fact that they were sent
directly to TCM’s client account. The irrefutable inference from the facts
224

is TCM used his client account to conceal or disguise the funds intended for
MM’s benefit.
Question 5
849. From the fact of concealment of the funds through the use of his client
account, the obvious inference to be drawn is that TCM intended that MM
should avoid prosecution or the making or enforcement of a confiscation
order against him.
$6m loan
850. The undisputed facts with respect to the loan of $6m from J&T Banka to
MM and LRM and its disbursement by TCM, as well as submissions of Mr.
Smith KC and the Crown have been summarised at paragraphs 757-766
ante.
851. TCM played a significant role in obtaining the loan. He assisted in
providing security for the loan by signing a mortgage of 50 shares in Salt
Cay Golf Course Ltd (SCGL) held by Business Ventures Ltd (BVL). His
signature on the document was witnessed by Stefan Kral, a close associate
of MHo, who was the CEO of three of his Salt Cay companies. The
proceeds of the loan were transferred directly into TCM’s FCIB client
account, and disbursed by him within a three week period.
852. Mr. Smith KC submits that the misapplication of the proceeds of the loan
sounds in civil action by the bank against the borrowers, and cannot elevate
the misapplication of the funds to a bribe. Further, there is no evidence that
TCM knew or suspected that the funds represented the proceeds of a bribe,
and even if the mens rea is established, there is no evidence to the required
standard of concealing or disguising the funds to avoid prosecution or the
making or enforcing of a confiscation order.
225

853. The Court has found that the $6m loan was little more than a device used
by MHo to pass bribes to MM (and McH). Nothing that has been submitted
by TCM in his closing submissions persuades the Court that that finding
should be revised. The further submissions of TCM with respect to the
knowledge or suspicion of TCM, and the purpose of his receiving and
disbursing the proceeds of the loan, will be discussed in due course.
854. The Court now considers the questions agreed in the Route to Verdict in
relation to the $6m loan.
Question 1
855. The first question is whether TCM received the funds directly or indirectly
for the benefit of MM. It is undisputed that TCM received the proceeds of
the loan and disbursed them largely for the benefit of MM.
Question 2
856. The second question is whether the funds received by TCM represented
directly or indirectly, wholly or in part, the proceeds of MM’s criminal
conduct. This question has already been answered in the affirmative in
considering Count 2.
Question 3
857. The third question concerns the state of mind of TCM. Did TCM know or
have reasonable grounds to suspect that the funds he received were the
proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct?
858. The undisputed facts are that:
- TCM was actively involved in the application for the loan;
- TCM signed a security document mortgaging the 50% shareholding
held by BVL in SCGL, which was witnessed by Stefan Kral;
226

- As guarantor, TCM must have been aware of the amount of the loan,
the date for repayment of one year and the interest rate of 10.25%;
- the loan was approved by J&T Banka within a short time;
- the proceeds of the loan were transferred directly to TCM’s client
account on 14th May 2007;
- most of the proceeds of the loan were disbursed by TCM the following
day; and
- none of the disbursements made by TCM, the guarantor of the loan, was
made towards the acquisition of the house in California.
859. TCM knew that MM was the Premier and was on a fixed salary. He knew
that MM would have to pay $6m with interest at 10.25% at the end of one
year to repay the loan, which works out to be $6,615,000 or $551,250.00
per month, which was more than three times the Premier’s basic annual
salary of $150,000.
860. TCM registered Business Ventures Ltd (BVL) on 17th January 2006. TCM
was its sole shareholder from 18th November 2006 to 20th February 2008,
and Chalmers Management Ltd. was its sole director for the same period.
BVL held 50 shares in SCGL which was registered on 15th August 2006.
The other 50 chares was held by HMDL, which was registered on 28th
August 2006, MHo being its sole shareholder.
861. SCGL was granted a 99 year lease of 238.72 acres of Crown land for the
construction of a golf course at an annual rent of $1 per acre on 26th April
2006.
862. The Crown submits that there is no commercial reason why BVL was
given 50% shareholding in SCGL. The Court has considered the following
facts:
227

- BVL was owned and controlled by TCM, the brother of the Premier,
with whom MHo was negotiating for Crown land and concessions;
- TCM was receiving and disbursing funds for MM’s benefit;
- a cheque for $3m dated 5th June 2007, drawn by TCM and marked
“client instructions - Business Ventures Ltd.” was deposited into the
BVL account opened the same day by TCM;
- a cheque for $1m drawn by TCM on 5th June 2007 was deposited into
the BB account of My Way Productions 2 Ltd, a company registered
and controlled by TCM, which held an account at BB, of which the
signatories were MM, LRM and TCM.
- the $1m to MWP2 was disbursed by MM for the benefit of LRM and
himself within the space of a few days, and
- the $3m deposited in the BVL account was eventually disbursed by
TCM largely for the benefit of MM.
863. From the foregoing facts the Court draws the inference that BVL was
registered by TCM to serve the interest of MM, and as a means to hold and
disburse money on his behalf. The vesting of 50% shareholding in SCGL
in BVL shows an element of pre-planning. It was clearly contemplated that
the shareholding would be used at some stage to benefit MM. The passing
of the proceeds first to TCM’s client account, then to BVL and MWP2 and
eventually to be disbursed for MM’s benefit displays an intention to conceal
and disguise the funds from which the Court infers knowledge on TCM’s
part that the loan was simply a device used by MHo to pass a bribe to MM.
864. In addition, TCM must have known that MM did not have the means to
repay the loan. TCM was channeling funds to pay MM’s Amex card. He
must have been aware that at no stage did MM attempt to make any payment
towards the loan.
228

865. The Court further considers that there is no valid commercial reason why
TCM was receiving the proceeds of the loan into his client account for a
transaction for the purchase of a house in California. There is no evidence
that TCM was acting for any of the parties to the transaction. TCM was in
the position of guarantor of the loan, having provided security by way of a
mortgage of the shares held by BVL in SCGL. TCM should have been
concerned that the loan would be applied to the purpose for which it was
taken, and that the loan would be repaid so as not to risk the security he
provided. Yet, upon receipt of the proceeds, TCM proceeded to disburse
the proceeds the very next day for purposes totally unrelated to the purpose
of the loan. The inference from TCM’s actions is clearly that he knew that
MM never intended to proceed with the purchase of the house, and he was
a willing participant in assisting in furthering an enterprise the purpose of
which was to provide a means to channel illegal payments to MM in return
for favors granted to MHo.
866. For these reasons the Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM knew
that the funds represented the proceeds of criminal conduct on the part of
MM, or at the very least had reasonable grounds to suspect that they were.
Question 4
867. The fourth question is whether TCM concealed or disguised the money he
received from MM.
868. The undisputed evidence is that, for no apparent commercial or
professional reason, TCM received the funds into his client account. The
very next day TCM transferred $5m to a FCIB Term Deposit, which was
returned with interest to his client account on 4th June 2007. On 1st June
2007, TCM paid $1m to MWP2 by cheque marked “Proceeds of Loan – per
client instructions”. On 5th June 2007, TCM paid $3m to BVL by cheque
marked “Client instructions Business Ventures Ltd.” TCM eventually
229

disbursed the $3m largely for the benefit of MM. The $1m to MWP2 was
disbursed by MM for the benefit of himself and LRM in a matter of a few
days.
869. From the use of his client account to receive the proceeds in the absence
of any professional or transactional service, his transfer to a term deposit
for 20 days, and his use of MWP2, and BVL, in order to provide another
layer of separation from MM, there is clear evidence that TCM intended to,
and did conceal or disguise the funds, so that they would not be directly
traceable to MM. If the loan was a legitimate transaction, why were the
funds not paid directly to MM or attorneys acting for him in connection with
the purchase of the house in Los Angeles?
870. The Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM did conceal or
disguise the funds he received for MM.
Question 5
871. Question 5 involves the intention of TCM in concealing or disguising the
funds. Was it to avoid a prosecution or the making or enforcement of a
confiscation order?
872. The intention of TCM is clearly a matter of inference. Why would TCM
conceal or disguise funds that he knows or has reasonable grounds to
suspect are the proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct?
873. The obvious and compelling inference, which the Court finds beyond
reasonable doubt, is that TCM concealed or disguised the funds with the
intention of MM avoiding prosecution for bribery and the making of or
enforcement of a confiscation order against him.
$500,000
230

874. This sum was paid (less transfer fees) to TCM’s FCIB client account on
15th January 2009 by HMDL. The wire transfer instructions contained the
words “For credit to Business Ventures Ltd Purchase Price SCGC/Purchase
Price for share in SCGC RFB? SWF of 09/01/14”.
875. The purpose of the payment appears to be for the purchase of the 50 shares
in SCGL held by BVL which were mortgaged by TCM to J&T Banka as
security for the $6m loan to MM and LRM.
876. The legitimacy of this payment is questionable. By virtue of the mortgage,
J&T Banka held the legal title to the shares. If the loan was a legitimate
transaction, one would expect that upon default, the lender would move to
enforce its security. Yet there is no evidence that J&T Banka attempted to
foreclose on the mortgage of the shares, or to enforce the blank promissory
note signed by MM as a further security. Instead, the bank assigned the
debt to Pro Partners Ltd on 29th December 2009; about 20 months after MM
defaulted on the loan.
877. Curiously, HMDL was now paying $500,000 for the shares, for which
BVL no longer held the legal title. The Crown asks the Court to draw an
inference that the payment was for the further benefit of MM. The payment
was disbursed by TCM within a period of 18 days. The Crown claims that
the funds were disbursed partly for the benefit of MM, partly for TCM and
in further part for unknown parties.
878. The Court has reviewed the evidence of Mr. Glendinning with respect to
the disbursement of the funds by TCM. While Mr. Glendinning gave the
names of the persons to whom the funds were distributed, the Court is
unable to determine from the evidence whether the disbursements were
made for MM’s benefit. Accordingly, the Court finds that these payments
are irrelevant to Count 4, and will receive no further consideration.
231

West Caicos - $83,000 and $33,000
879. On 21st November 2006 and 30th November 2006, TCM received two
cheques from HC, for $83,000 and $33,000 respectively, which were
deposited into his client account at BB. Between 21st November 2006 and
30th November 2006, the funds were disbursed for the benefit of MM. The
disbursements included deposits of $50,000 and $80,000 into MM’s and
LRM’s HSBC account in Beverly Hills, $43,095 to Ashland Aviation LLC
(marked – Windsor West) and $31,025.53 to MM’s Amex card.
880. Mr. Smith KC’s submissions on these payments and the Crown’s response
are summarised in paragraphs 777-781 ante.
881. The Court has considered Mr. Smith’s KC submissions, and finds them
unpersuasive. Whether or not TCM knew about ExCo’s decisions, or
whether TCM knew that the funds originated from LDCL are not relevant
considerations. The issue is whether or not he knew or had reasonable
grounds to suspect that the funds represented proceeds of crime. The
submission that there was nothing on the face of the cheques that could have
alerted TCM that the funds were anything other than
“payments/contributions from a prominent businessman shortly before the
February 2007 election, is equally unpersuasive. Why was TCM receiving
these funds into his client account, when he had no client/attorney
relationship with MM, the PNP or HC. If these were political contributions,
then why were they not paid to the PNP, or even personally to MM to help
him finance his campaign?
Questions 1 and 2
882. The Court now addresses the questions posed in the agreed route to
verdict. There is no issue that TCM received the funds and disbursed them
for MM’s benefit. The Court has already found in considering Count 3 that
232

the proceeds represented the criminal conduct of MM. The answer to
questions 1 and 2 is yes.
Question 3
883. Question 3 concerns the state of mind of TCM. Did TCM know or have
reasonable grounds to suspect that the funds he received were the proceeds
of criminal conduct of MM?
884. There is of course no direct evidence from TCM or any witness, as to
whether TCM knew that the funds were the proceeds of MM’s criminal
conduct. The state of mind of TCM is a matter of inference to be gathered
from the evidence. The Court considers the following matters to be
relevant:
- TCM was receiving substantial payments into his client account from a
person who was not his client, to be disbursed to a person who also was
not his client;
- there is no professional reason for TCM to receive this money into his
client account;
- TCM knew that MM was the Chief Minister and was in a position to
influence decisions of ExCo, which could have financial advantages for
potential investors;
- TCM was receiving substantial funds from a “prominent businessmen”
(to use Mr. Smith’s KC words) for the benefit of the Premier;
- TCM was not acting for the PNP, and had no professional reason to
accept political donations into his client account;
- the payments were not disbursed by TCM for any discernible political
or campaign related expense;
- the funds were disbursed for the personal benefit of MM and LRM, and
- the funds were disbursed in a period of 9 days, at least two months
before the elections.
233

885. The compelling and irresistible inference to be drawn from the use of
TCM’s client account to receive the funds, and the haste with which the
funds were disbursed, is that TCM’s intention was to conceal or disguise
the money so that it could not be easily traced to MM. It is of note that the
only bank account into which funds were deposited was MM’s and LRM’s
HSBC account in Los Angeles. If TCM did not know that the funds were
criminal in nature, why the concealment in his client account, followed by
the rapid disbursements?
886. The Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM knew that the funds
represented the proceeds of the criminal conduct of MM, or at the very least,
the factors listed above show that he had reasonable grounds to suspect that
they were.
Questions 4 and 5
887. As discussed in considering Question 3, TCM’s conduct in relation to
receiving and disbursing the money, clearly discloses an intention to
conceal or disguise the funds. The inference that is inevitable from the
concealment or disguise is that in so doing, TCM’s purpose was to avoid a
prosecution of MM for bribery and to avoid the making or enforcement of
a confiscation order against MM.
888. The Court has considered the evidence in some detail. The Court finds the
evidence against TCM on the Fourth Count to be cogent and compelling.
The Court finds to the extent that it feels sure that TCM is guilty on the
Fourth Count. In coming to this verdict the Court expressly takes into
account TCM’s previously good character, and the propensity limb of the
Vye direction.
COUNT 6
234

889. This count involves TCM’s concealment or disguise of criminal property
on behalf of MM between 7th October 2007 to 31st August 2009, contrary
to section 124(1)(a) and (b) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2007.
Sea Horse Ranch
890. The payment of $3m was made by HMDL on 29th July 2008 to Guzman
Ariza Associates in the Dominican Republic for the purchase of Villa 127
Sea Horse Ranch.
891. The Court has already considered the evidence relating to the purchase of
Villa 127 in relation to Count 2. The Court has found that TCM was
actively involved in the purchase of Villa 127, and acquired the legal title
through the purchase of the shares in Villa 127 which were owned by Solar
127 SA, a company incorporated in the Dominican Republic. The shares in
SHR Solar were owned by Eastern Townships Investments Inc, a company
incorporated in Florida, USA.
892. By means of a share sale agreement made between SHR Solar, Eastern
Townships Investments and Portela Investments Group, Portela bought the
shares held by SHR Solar. Portela was a company registered in Panama on
17th April 2008 by a firm called Rosas and Rosas on the instructions of
Guzman Ariza Associates. On 12th May 2008 TCM acquired the shares in
Portela.
893. On 28th April 2008, TCM paid Guzman a “deposit” of $50,095, and two
days later $33,503 (described as “Legal Fees”). On 14th October 2008,
TCM made a further payment of $25,095, described as “client instructions”.
894. On 29th July 2008, the purchase price of $3m for the shares in SHR Solar
was provided by HMDL directly to Guzman. This payment did not pass
through TCM’s client account like the others previously discussed.
235

895. The evidence shows that TCM was the moving force behind the
acquisition of Villa 127. He paid the deposit and legal fees to Guzman, and
it must be inferred that he was giving instructions to Guzman, who was
instrumental in incorporating Portela Investments Group in Panama, which
acquired the shares in SHR Solar. In order for HMDL to send the purchase
price to Guzman, TCM must have been in communication with HMDL,
giving details of the transaction, and arranging for the transfer of the funds
to Guzman, who was handling the share purchase agreement on behalf of
TCM. In the absence of TCM coordinating the transaction, HMDL would
not have been able to make the payment to Guzman.
896. It makes no sense whatsoever that HMDL (an entity wholly owned by
MHo) would purchase Villa 127 for the benefit of TCM. The Court has
already found (in considering Count 2) that TCM acquired the legal title in
Villa 127 as a front for MM. Having considered Mr. Smith’s KC
submissions, the Court sees no reason to revise this finding.
897. The route to verdict (RTV) agreed by counsel was filed before the
indictment was amended to include Counts 6 and 7, which are brought under
the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance 2007, due to the dates of some
transactions. The RTV posed questions based on the wording of the 1998
Ordinance.
898. In the later Ordinance, “proceeds of criminal conduct” is replaced by
“criminal property”, and the mens rea of the offence was changed from
“knows or has reasonable grounds to suspect” to “knows or suspects” that
the property constitutes or represents a person’s benefit from criminal
conduct. In addition the purpose of avoiding prosecution or the making or
enforcement of a confiscation order, is not included in the 2007 Ordinance.
236

899. Despite these changes the essence of the offence remains the same: Did
TCM conceal or disguise the property which he knew or suspected
representing MM’s benefit from criminal conduct?
900. In the light of the differences between the 1998 and the 2007 Ordinance,
the Court proposes to frame the RTV for Counts 6 and 7 as follows:
Question 1: Were the funds criminal property?
Question 2: Did TCM know or suspect that the funds were criminal
property?
Question 3: Did TCM conceal or disguise the funds?
Question 1
901. In answer to the first question, the Court has already determined in
considering Count 2 that the payment of $3m by HMDL to Guzman was a
bribe made indirectly by MHo to obtain or reward favourable treatment of
his Salt Cay development by MM. The Court is not persuaded by any of
TCM’s submissions that this finding of fact should be revised. The Court
answers the first question in the affirmative.
Question 2
902. The second question raises the issue of the state of mind of TCM. The
Court has already made findings of fact that TCM did all the footwork in
acquiring Villa 127. He paid the deposit and legal fees to Guzman and must
have instructed Guzman to register Portela Investments in Panama, through
which he would acquire the legal title. TCM would not have taken all these
steps without knowing how the purchase price was to be paid. The
irresistible inference that the Court draws is that TCM knew that HMDL
would provide the funds for the acquisition of Villa 127, and as the Court
has already found, would have been in communication with HMDL to
provide instructions for payment to Guzman. TCM was clearly the
directing mind behind the transaction.
237

903. The Court has already commented that there is no conceivable reason why
MHo would seek to confer a benefit of $3m on TCM, with whom there is
no evidence he had a personal or professional relationship. If TCM knew
that the purchase price was to be provided by HMDL, for the benefit of
MM, then if he had not known the reason for the payment, he must have
asked himself why was HMDL providing $3m to acquire a luxury villa in
DR for the benefit of MM. If he refrained from making the appropriate
inquiries, because he did not wish to confirm his suspicions, then the
tribunal of fact will impute knowledge to him.
904. The Court finds as a fact beyond reasonable doubt that TCM knew, or at
the very least suspected that the $3m was criminal property representing the
proceeds of MM’s criminal conduct, and arranged for HMDL to make the
payment of the purchase price directly to Guzman.
Question 3
905. In answer to the third question, the evidence is that TCM did all the
groundwork for the acquisition of Villa 127, and coordinated the transaction
with HMDL to pay $3m to purchase the property. This was accomplished
without the $3m ever being sent to any financial institution in TCI, or any
corporate body associated with MM, and with the legal title being vested in
someone else (TCM) through ownership of a company registered in
Panama.
906. The tribunal of fact has no difficulty whatsoever in finding as a fact beyond
reasonable doubt that TCM concealed or disguised the $3m on behalf of
MM, knowing or suspecting that it was criminal property representing the
criminal conduct of MM.
West Caicos
238

$1m from Harold Charles
907. The undisputed facts with respect to the payment and disbursement of
these funds are summarised in paragraph 777-778, and the submissions are
set out in paragraphs 779-781.
908. The submissions of Mr. Smith KC on this payment are quite weak. The
Crown is not required to prove TCM’s knowledge of ExCo decisions that
favoured Logwood, or any nexus between these decisions and Henry
Mensen or HC. The submission that the disbursements were made in
accordance with instructions from TCM’s client(s), does not begin to
answer the issues raised.
909. Mr. Smith’s KC submissions do not answer any of the concerns raised by
the receipt of $1m into TCM’s client account and its disbursement two days
later to pay for MM’s Amex card ($300,000) and McH’s ($400,000),
followed 4 days later by a deposit of $70,000 into MM’s FCIB account.
910. The Court has to consider the inferences to be drawn from the following
facts:
- the size of the payment;
- the fact that TCM kept no record of the receipt of $1m into his client
account and no reason recorded for the payment;
- the absence of any client/attorney relationship between TCM, MM,
McH, or HC;
- the disposal of the payment over a matter of a few days; and
- the disbursement of the funds to benefit the Premier and a Minister, who
were in a position of influence to make decisions that may be favourable
to investors or businessmen, such as HC.
Question 1
239

911. The Court now considers the reframed RTV. In answer to the first
question, the Court has already determined by its verdict on Count 3 that
the $1m was criminal property, intended as a bribe to benefit MM (and
McH). There is nothing in the submissions of TCM that persuades the court
to revise its decision on this issue.
Question 2
912. From a consideration of the facts set out in paragraph 910, the cogent and
compelling inference to be drawn is that TCM knew, or at the very least
suspected that the $1m represented criminal property. Why did TCM pass
this $1m payment through his client account? There is no evidence that he
was handling any transaction, for HC, or legal work that would involve the
payment of a deposit or legal fees. $1m is a substantial sum. Why did TCM
keep no record of the payment and the reason for receiving it? Why did he
disburse it within a few days for the benefit of MM (and McH), in the
absence of any evidence that he was handling any transaction for MM, McH
or HC? Assuming that TCM did not know the reason for the payment, he
could have made inquiries of the sender as to the reason for the payment.
Mr. Smith KC submits that TCM disbursed the funds as instructed by “his
client(s)”, but does not disclose who his client(s) was/were.
913. The funds were disbursed largely for the benefit of MM (and McH). If
these were his clients, TCM could have inquired of them, the reason for the
payments. Since TCM elected to remain silent, and to call no witnesses,
there is no direct evidence before the Court as to the reason for the payment,
and whether TCM made any inquiry of his “client(s)” as to the origin and
reason for the payment. The failure by TCM to make such inquiries gives
rise to two possible inferences:
- TCM knew the origin and reason for the payment, or
- TCM suspected that the money was criminal property and did not wish
to confirm his suspicion.
240

914. Having carefully considered the evidence, the Court finds beyond
reasonable doubt that TCM knew or suspected that the $1m represented
criminal property derived from MM’s criminal conduct.
Question 3
915. The third question is `one of fact: Did TCM conceal or disguise the
criminal property. The undisputed evidence is that TCM received the
payment of $1m into his client account and proceeded to disburse the money
in a matter of a few days, the bulk of the money being paid to a foreign bank
to pay the Amex Centurion Cards of MM (and McH). The clear and
compelling inference to be drawn from these facts, is that TCM concealed
or disguised the $1m through the use of his client account, and the rapid
disposal of the funds largely through a foreign bank.
$500,000 – paid on 31st December 2007
916. The facts and submissions with respect to this payment are summarised in
paragraphs 786-788 ante.
917. Mr. Smith KC submitted that there is no evidence that the funds were the
proceeds of crime or came from a source that TCM knew or could have
known, was impugned. There was nothing written on the cheque to
Chalmers & Co, which could have put TCM on notice that the funds were
criminal in nature. TCM argues that the funds are unimpeachable in nature,
and therefore their distribution is irrelevant.
918. The submissions miss the point. There is no discernible professional
reason why TCM was receiving $500,000 into his client account from an
entity which was not his client, and paying it out the same day to his brother
who was also not his client.
241

Question 1
919. In considering Count 3 the Court has already determined that the funds
were criminal property. There is no submission made by TCM that
persuades the Court to review this finding of fact.
Question 2
920. The crucial question is whether TCM knew or suspected that the funds
were criminal property. For the avoidance of repetition, the same facts
outlined in paragraph 910 ante are relevant. Why was TCM receiving
$500,000, a sum more than three times MM’s basic annual salary from a
corporate entity with whom he had no professional relationship, and passing
it on to his brother the same day? If TCM did not know the reason for the
payment, surely he could have made inquiries of the sender or his brother,
and made a note of the reason for the payment and the disbursement, if not
out of professional propriety, then at least for his own protection. If TCM
was not certain, but suspected that the funds were criminal property, yet
declined to make appropriate inquires, because he did not want to confirm
his suspicion, then the Court is entitled to impute knowledge to him.
921. Based on the evidence, the Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM
knew or suspected that the $500,000 payment was criminal property which
represented MM’s benefit from criminal conduct.
Question 3
922. In answer to the third question, the Court concludes beyond reasonable
doubt from TCM’s receipt of the funds into his client account, for no
apparent professional reason and his immediate transfer of the money to
MM’s J&T Banka account, that there was a clear intention by TCM to
conceal or disguise the funds.
$700,000 - paid on 10th April 2008 by ITC
242

923. The facts and submissions in respect of this payment are set out in
paragraphs 789-792 ante.
924. Mr. Smith’s KC submissions that there is no evidence that TCM knew of
the ExCo decision to sell Crown land to West Harbour Development
Company (WHD), or that TCM knew that the source of the funds had
anything to do with Westco, do not assist TCM. It does not matter whether
he knew any of these things. The issue is whether TCM knew or suspected
that the funds represented criminal conduct.
925. TCM’s submission that there is no evidence of the reason or purpose of
the funds being paid to ITC, is also unpersuasive. The issue is whether the
funds paid by ITC for disbursement to MM (and McH), represented
criminal property.
926. TCM submits that the original source of the funds (part of a loan from
Belize Bank to Westco Development Ltd (Westco)), was unimpeachable,
and therefore their distribution is irrelevant. However, he concedes that the
proceeds of a loan can be used as a bribe, a position with which the Court
agrees.
Question 1
927. The Court now turns to consider the 3 questions posed in the revised RTV.
In answer to the first question, the Court has already determined by its
verdict on Count 3, that the funds represented criminal property.
Question 2
928. The second question concerns the state of mind of TCM. In answering
this question, the Court considers that the matters set out in paragraph 910
are relevant in determining this issue. To summarise briefly these factors
are:
243

- TCM’s failure to keep any records of the payment or transactions;
- the receipt of a substantial payment, several times the annual salary of
MM (or McH);
- the lack of a professional basis for the receipt of the money or its
disbursement; and
- the failure to make appropriate inquiries of the sender or the recipient to
discover the reason for the payment.
929. The Court concludes on the evidence beyond reasonable doubt that TCM
knew or suspected that the $700,000 payment from ITC represented
criminal property, being the proceeds of the criminal conduct of MM.
Question 3
930. The third question raises the issue of whether TCM concealed or disguised
the payments. This is a matter of inference to be drawn from the evidence.
Why did TCM receive this money into his client account in the absence of
any professional reason for doing so? Why did TCM disburse the funds
within the month of April and May 2008 for the benefit of MM (and McH)?
931. The evidence also reveals that, having deposited the ITC cheque for
$700,000 into his FCIB client account on 10th April 2008, the same date of
the cheque, TCM spilt the deposit into three cheques which he then
deposited into his BB client account – two cheques on the same day (10th
April 2008) for $100,000 and $200,000, and a cheque for $400,000 on 15th
April 2008.
932. The transactions give rise to the inference that this was a further layer of
concealment by TCM of the source of the funds before disbursement for the
benefit of MM (and McH).
244

933. From the evidence the Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM
concealed or disguised the payment of $700,000 received from ITC on
behalf of MM.
934. It follows from the above discussion that the Court finds TCM guilty on
Count 6. In coming to this verdict, the Court expressly takes into account
the propensity limb of the good character direction in Vye.
COUNT 5
935. Count 5 involves the concealing or disguising of the proceeds of criminal
conduct by TCM for the benefit of McH, during the period 1st August 2003
to 8th October 2007.
936. The disbursements by TCM for the benefit of McH during this period as
alleged by the Crown are:
- $40,564.76 on 15th May 2007
- $645,741.02 on 15th May 2007
- $150,000 on 1st June 2007
- $100,000 on 13th June 2007
- $92,000 on 26th June 2007
$40,564.76
937. This disbursement was part of the proceeds of the $6m loan obtained by
MM and LRM and received by TCM on 14th May 2007. This payment to
McH was made by TCM out of a $500,000 deposit made to TCM’s BB
client account from TCM’s FCIB client account on 15th May 2007. The
payment was made to J&T Banka towards expenditure on McH’s Amex
card.
$645,564.76
245

938. This sum comprises 4 payments made by TCM out of the $6m loan
proceeds on 15th May 2007 for McH’s benefit. The payments consisted of
2 cheques of $100,000 to Jaca TCI Ltd (involved in the construction of a
house for McH), and 2 cheques for $368,241.02, and $77,500 to Saunders
& Company in connection with McH’s acquisition of Unit 5 the Enclave (a
condo on the golf course). One cheque for $100,000 was made to AL
Services Ltd, one of McH’s companies.
$150,000 and $100,00
939. These payments were also made on McH’s behalf on 1st June 2007 and
13th June 2007 to Jaca (TCI) Ltd. These payments also originated from the
proceeds of the $6m loan.
$92,000
940. On 26th June 2007, McH issued a cheque in the sum of $92,000 to
Chalmers & Co. which was placed into the BB client account. Two days
later TCM transferred $91,905 to McH’s J&T Banka account. The $92,000
paid by McH to Chalmers & Co was part of a $500,000 loan from Saunders
& Co, which was repaid with a $1m payment from ITC to Saunders on 10th
April 2008. The origin of the $92,000 was a loan from Saunders to McH.
The Court finds that there is no issue of concealing or disguising of criminal
proceeds with respect to this payment.
DISCUSSION
941. The Court now turns to the five questions posed in the agreed Route to
Verdict. The issues that arise in all these questions, have already been
discussed and determined in considering Count 4 in relation to MM.
942. The Court will attempt to avoid repetition of the discussion, and the
findings of fact that have already been made in relation to the receipt of the
$6m loan from J&T Banka by TCM. The Court has already found as a fact:
246

- TCM received the $6m;
- the $6m represented directly or indirectly wholly or in part the proceeds
of criminal conduct;
- TCM knew or had reasonable grounds to suspect that the $6m were the
proceeds of crime.
- TCM did conceal or disguise the $6m.
943. The only difference in the wording between Count 4 and Count 5, is that
the name McAllister Eugene Hanchell appears in Count 5 in place of
Thomas Chalmers Misick in Count 4.
944. TCM disbursed $936,305.78 for the benefit of McH out of the $6m loan
proceeds. The clear inferences that arise out of the disbursements are:
- TCM must have received specific instructions from McH as to the
payments including specific amounts and the payees;
- TCM must have known that McH was “entitled” to have payments made
for his benefit and at his direction out of the $6m loan proceeds, and
- TCM must have known that McH was receiving almost $1m from a loan
that was granted to MM and LRM to purchase a house in California.
Questions 1 and 2
945. From these inferences, the answers to questions 1 and 2 present no
difficulty. The $6m received by TCM were directly or indirectly, partially
for the benefit of McH, and represented directly or indirectly in part the
proceeds of McH’s criminal conduct.
Question 3
946. With respect to the mens rea of the offence, the considerations are
essentially the same, as set out in the discussion of Count 4 in relation to
MM. These include:
247

- there is no evidence of a client/lawyer relationship between McH and
TCM;
- there is no professional reason why TCM was receiving the $6m
proceeds of a loan to purchase a house in California;
- there is no professional reason why TCM was making substantial
disbursements to McH from a loan to MM and LRM, and
- the disbursements to McH had nothing to do with the purpose of the
loan, of which TCM should have been aware, having provided security
for the loan.
947. The clear and compelling inference from the undisputed facts of this case
is that TCM knew or had reasonable grounds to suspect that the $6m he
received were the proceeds of the criminal conduct of McH.
Question 4
948. The disbursements benefited McH indirectly by making payments for his
benefit that did not involve him personally, thus concealing any direct
involvement of McH in receiving illicit funds. One notable payment was a
cheque for $100,000 by TCM to AL Services Ltd. described as “Repayment
of loan – South Point Ltd”. There is no evidence of such a loan. There were
three cheques drawn by TCM for the payment of $350,000 to Jaca (TCI)
Ltd, which was constructing a house for McH at Silly Creek, Chalk Sound.
949. South Point Ltd was the beneficial owner of Unit 5 the Enclave Ltd, which
was set up to acquire the condo on Provo Golf Course for McH’s benefit.
South Point Ltd was registered on 17th January 2006 by Chalmers
Management Ltd, of which Windsor East Ltd was the sole shareholder.
TCM and his wife were the shareholders of Windsor East Ltd. TCM
channeled the sums of $77,500 and $368,241.20 to Saunders & Co to
acquire Unit 5 Condo on behalf of McH.
248

950. The indirect means through which TCM channeled the funds and the speed
at which the disbursements were made for the benefit of McH, (most
transactions occurring the day after the $6m was received) reveal an
urgency to dispose of the funds in such a way that they were not directly
traceable to McH.
951. In the circumstances, the Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM
concealed or disguised the funds for the benefit of McH.
Question 5
952. The fifth question is a matter of inference. Why would TCM conceal or
disguise the funds he disbursed for the benefit of McH. The compelling
inference is that his intention must have been to avoid a prosecution or the
making or enforcement of a confiscation order against McH.
953. Accordingly, the Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM is guilty
on the Fifth Count. In coming to this verdict, the Court expressly takes into
account the previous good character of TCM, and the propensity limb of the
direction in Vye.
COUNT 7
954. This Count concerns the concealing or disguising of criminal property on
behalf of McH by TCM during the period 7th October 2007 and 31st August
2009 contrary to Section 124(1)(a) and (b) of the Proceeds of Crime
Ordinance 2007.
955. The Crown alleges that TCM concealed or disguised the following sums:
- $5,000.00 on 3rd March 2008
- $400,000 on 16th November 2007
- $15,000 on 14th April 2008
- $125,000 on 17th April 2008
249

- $19,985 on 28th April 2008
- $20,000 on 30th April 2008
- $10,000 on 16th May 2008.
$5,000
956. This sum was paid to Roxanne Chung a “friend” of McH by TCM, on 3rd
March 2008 from the $3m TCM deposited into the BVL account on 5th June
2007, out of the $6m loan proceeds TCM received into his FCIB client
account on 14th May 2007.
$400,000
957. This sum was paid by TCM on 16th November 2007 by transfer out of his
BB client account to J&T Banka to cover expenditure on McH’s Amex card.
The money came from a $1m payment from HC received two days earlier.
$15,000
958. This sum was paid by TCM from his BB client account on 14th April 2008
to Misick and Stanbrook in connection with the purchase of property by a
company owned by McH. The $15,000 came from a cheque for $700,000
drawn on ITC, which was deposited on 10th April 2008, and was later split
into three cheques of $100,000, $200,000, and $400,000 and deposited by
TCM into his BB clients account.
$125,000
959. This payment was made by TCM by wire transfer to J&T Banka on 17th
April 2008 to credit McH’s Amex card. The transfer was made from TCM’s
BB client account out of the $700,000 received by TCM from HC on 10th
April 2008.
$19,985 and $20,000
250

960. These payments were made by Saunders & Co. by cheques dated 28th April
2008 and 30th April 2008 which were deposited into TCM’s client accounts
in TCI Bank and FCIB respectively. The source of the funds was a $1m
cheque drawn on ITC on 10th April 2008 and deposited into Saunders & Co.
client account. The deposit slip was endorsed “Hanchell M deposit on
instruction”. The cheques to TCM were endorsed “Instruction Hanchell”.
$10,000
961. This payment was made to Roxanne Chung (a “friend” of McH) on 16th
May 2008, by TCM out of his BB client account from the $700,000 received
from HC on 10th April 2008.
962. The Court has already made findings of fact that the funds out of which
these payments were made by TCM for the benefit of McH were criminal
property.
963. The clear and compelling inference is that TCM received and held the
criminal property for McH. This inference is to be drawn from the fact that
TCM disbursed the funds for the benefit of McH. However, the Court notes
that section 122(2) of the Proceeds of Crime Ordinance 2007, provides that
for the purposes of defining “criminal property” it is immaterial who carried
out the criminal conduct or who benefited from it.
964. Section 124(1) of the 2007 Ordinance creates the offences of concealing,
disguising, converting, transferring or removing criminal property. Section
122(2) specifies that it does not matter who committed the criminal conduct
that resulted in the acquisition of criminal property or who benefited from
it, as long as the criminal property represents a benefit derived from
someone’s criminal conduct.
251

965. However, the wording of Counts 5 and 7 names the beneficiaries of the
criminal property, which appears to be unnecessary for the purposes of
section 124(1) of the 2007 Ordinance. Nevertheless, from the distribution
of the criminal property, it is clear who the intended beneficiaries were.
DISCUSSION
966. The Court will now consider whether or not the Crown has proved its case
in relation to Count 7. There are three issues:
(i) Were the funds criminal property?
(ii) Did TCM know or suspect that the funds he received were criminal
property?
(iii) Did TCM conceal or disguise the funds?
Question 1
967. The Court has already made findings of fact that the $6m loan proceeds,
the $1m received from HC on 14th November 2007, the $700,000 received
from HC on 10th April 2008 and the $1m cheque from ITC to Saunders &
Co. on the same day, (out of which TCM received $19,985 and $20,000)
were criminal property. While the Court is aware of the requirement to
consider each count separately, it appears to be a pedantic exercise to repeat
what has already been discussed at length.
Question 2
968. The second issue relates to the mens rea of TCM. Did he know or suspect
that the funds he received represented criminal property? This issue has
been discussed at length in considering Counts 4, 5, and 6. The Court does
not wish to repeat the discussion already rehearsed at length. Suffice it to
say that the Court considers the following circumstances to be relevant:
- there is no evidence of a lawyer/client relationship between TCM and
McH, or of any legal transaction which would entail the receipt and
disbursement of funds for and on behalf of McH;
252

- there is no professional reason why TCM was receiving the proceeds of
the $6m loan, or such substantial funds from HC and ITC, and then
disbursing them on the instructions of McH.
- there was no professional reason why TCM was receiving these
payments into his client account, and
- the payments were disbursed with a certain degree of urgency for the
benefit of McH.
969. The clear and compelling inference to be drawn from the evidence is that
TCM knew that he was receiving criminal property, which he assisted in
concealing by placing the payments into his client account. There is no
documentary record of the receipt, reason or disbursement of such large
sums of money. Even if TCM was not certain that the payments were
proceeds of the criminal conduct of a practising politician, who was in a
position to grant favors to potential investors, surely TCM could have made
inquiries of the persons making the payments, or the person giving
instructions for the disbursements. These inquiries could have revealed the
true nature of the payments. There is no evidence that TCM made any such
inquiries. The compelling inference is that TCM suspected the true nature
of the payments, but did not wish to confirm his suspicions.
970. The Court concludes beyond reasonable doubt that TCM knew or
suspected that the funds he received were criminal property.
Question 3
971. The issue of whether TCM concealed or disguised the criminal property is
a matter of inference. The evidence is that the funds were deposited into
TCM client accounts, sometimes shifted between accounts, and payments
were made that benefited McH. Large payments were made to J&T Banka
for McH’s Amex card. J&T Banka is a Slovakian Bank. Such payments
are difficult to trace at a local level. The payments were not made directly
253

to any local bank accounts of McH. They were made indirectly, for his
benefit. Significantly, the payments were disbursed within a short space of
time, indicating a certain level of urgency to dispose of them.
972. The Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM concealed or
disguised the criminal property for the benefit of McH.
973. It follows that the Court finds beyond reasonable doubt that TCM is guilty
on the 7th Count. In coming to this verdict the Court expressly takes into
account the previous good character of TCM.
974. In closing, the Court wishes to express its profound gratitude for the
assistance provided by both Crown Counsel and Counsel for the defendants,
who at all times performed their functions with a high level of
professionalism and forensic skill while at the same time displaying
candour, cordiality, and good humor to the Court as well as towards their
colleagues.
Dated the 4th day of February 2026
________________________
RAJENDRA NARINE
Judge
254
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Court convicted a former premier, a minister and a lawyer of bribery and concealing criminal proceeds based on compelling circumstantial evidence.
